topics.title: Principles of Drawing: Free Hand Sketching, Manual Drawing, CAD Drawing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRL307 - MACHINE DRAWING AND SOLID MODELLING
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Principles of Drawing: Free Hand Sketching, Manual Drawing, CAD Drawing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Engineering Drawing — KTU 2024 Definition**
> An *Engineering Drawing* is the universal graphical language of engineers used to communicate precise geometric, dimensional, and manufacturing information about a physical object or system, conforming to the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS — SP 46:2003)** and **ISO 128/ISO 7200** conventions.

Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCBRL307)**, the *Principles of Drawing* module classifies graphical communication into three foundational execution methodologies:

| # | Mode | Definition |
|---|------|------------|
| 1 | **Free Hand Sketching** | Drawing executed by hand, without the aid of drawing instruments, relying on visual judgment, proportion, and motor skill. |
| 2 | **Manual (Instrument) Drawing** | Precision drawing produced using physical drafting tools such as the *mini drafter, T-square, set squares, compass, and dividers* on a drawing sheet. |
| 3 | **CAD Drawing** | Computer-Aided Design drawing created using software tools (e.g., *AutoCAD, SolidWorks, CATIA, Creo, Fusion 360*) where geometry is constructed via vector commands, parametric features, and solid/surface modellers. |

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Analogy — Three Ways to "Tell" a Story**
> Imagine you want to describe a *new bicycle design* to a manufacturer:
>
> - **Free Hand Sketching** → Like scribbling a rough map of your route on the back of a napkin during a coffee chat. Fast, rough, and proportional, but **not to scale**.
> - **Manual Drawing** → Like drawing the route with a ruler, compass, and protractor on a *formal survey chart*. Slow but **highly accurate**, follows strict conventions, and can be used for construction.
> - **CAD Drawing** → Like using *Google Maps* to plan the route. You can zoom, rotate, calculate distance, simulate traffic, edit on the fly, and share a 3D flyover with anyone, anywhere.
>
> **All three describe the same bicycle — but the precision, scalability, and intended audience differ.**

---

### 1.3 BIS Standards & Governing Conventions (Bold Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT] **BIS SP 46:2003 — First Angle Projection is the KTU/Indian Standard**
> - **First-Angle Projection** is **mandatory** for all KTU 2024 Scheme examinations.
> - Sheet sizes: **A0 (1189 × 841 mm)**, A1, A2, A3, A4, A5 (A4 is the most common student sheet).
> - **Title Block** must be placed in the **bottom-right corner** of the sheet.
> - All dimensions in **millimetres (mm)** unless otherwise specified.
> - **Line types**: Continuous thick (visible edges), continuous thin (dimension/leader), dashed thin (hidden edges), chain thin (centre lines).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Free Hand Sketching — Operational Principles

Free hand sketching is the **cognitive backbone** of every engineer. It precedes any formal drawing because it allows rapid idea capture.

**Operational Logic Steps:**

1. **Visualise the object** in 3D (mental rotation).
2. **Establish a bounding box** with proportion ratios (e.g., height:width:depth = 2:1:1).
3. **Block in the major forms** using light, free strokes.
4. **Refine contours** with darker, confident lines.
5. **Add centre lines and hidden details** as dashed lines.
6. **Annotate dimensions** in a freehand style — the focus is on **proportion, not measurement**.

> [!NOTE] **Why Free Hand?**
> Engineers use free hand sketches during **design reviews, site visits, brainstorms, and patent disclosures** where time is critical. It captures *intent* faster than any CAD click-sequence.

**KTU High-Yield Point:** Free hand sketches are **never measured with a scale** — proportion is judged **by eye**.

---

### 2.2 Manual (Instrument) Drawing — Operational Principles

Manual drawing is the **traditional industrial standard** still legally required in many government tenders, fabrication shops, and patent offices worldwide.

**Required Instrument Set (KTU Exam Checklist):**

| Instrument | Function |
|------------|----------|
| **Mini Drafter** | Replaces T-square + set squares; draws horizontal, vertical, and 30°/45°/60° lines |
| **Drawing Board** | Flat rigid surface (plywood) for mounting the sheet |
| **Compass** | Draws circles and arcs |
| **Dividers** | Transfers measurements |
| **Protractor** | Measures/constructs angles |
| **Set Squares (45° and 30°–60°)** | Auxiliary angle construction |
| **Clips / Drawing Pins** | Holds the sheet |
| **Drawing Sheet (A4/A3)** | The media |

**Operational Logic Steps (Manual Drawing Workflow):**

1. **Mount the sheet** with bottom edge parallel to the board edge.
2. **Draw the border** (10 mm inside the sheet edge) using the mini drafter.
3. **Construct the title block** (bottom-right).
4. **Lay out views** with adequate spacing (≥ 30 mm between views).
5. **Draw centre lines first** in chain (long-dash-short-dash) style.
6. **Construct main outlines** in continuous thick lines.
7. **Add hidden lines** (dashed) for internal features.
8. **Dimension** using aligned or unidirectional systems.
9. **Apply sectioning/hatching** at 45° where required.

---

### 2.3 CAD Drawing — Operational Principles

CAD is the **21st-century standard**. Under KTU 2024, students are expected to be proficient in 2D drafting and introductory 3D solid modelling.

**Operational Logic Steps (CAD Workflow):**

1. **Start a New File** → Select template (ISO A-series, mm units, First-Angle Projection).
2. **Set up Layers** → e.g., *Object, Centre, Dimension, Hidden, Section*.
3. **Set Drawing Limits / Sheet Size** to match the standard.
4. **Use Coordinate Entry (Absolute, Relative, Polar)** for precision.
5. **Construct Geometry** using commands: `LINE`, `CIRCLE`, `ARC`, `PLINE`, `RECT`, `OFFSET`, `TRIM`, `EXTEND`, `MIRROR`, `ARRAY`, `COPY`, `MOVE`, `ROTATE`.
6. **Apply Modifications** with `CHAMFER`, `FILLET`, `SCALE`, `STRETCH`.
7. **Add Dimensions** via `DIMLINEAR`, `DIMRADIAL`, `DIMANGULAR`.
8. **Apply Hatching** with `HATCH` command (ANSI31 pattern at 45°).
9. **Insert Title Block & Border** from a template file.
10. **Plot/Print** to scale (1:1 for student sheets typically).

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Hot Point — UCS & WCS**
> In CAD, the **World Coordinate System (WCS)** is the global frame, and the **User Coordinate System (UCS)** is a movable local frame. A skilled KTU student **rotates the UCS** before drawing inclined surfaces rather than computing awkward coordinates.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Free Hand Sketching | Manual (Instrument) Drawing | CAD Drawing |
|-----------|---------------------|------------------------------|--------------|
| **Tools** | Pencil + paper | Mini drafter, compass, board | Computer + software |
| **Accuracy** | Low (proportional only) | High (instrument-grade) | Highest (up to 0.0001 mm) |
| **Speed** | Fastest | Slowest | Fast for revisions |
| **Scalability** | Poor | Poor | Excellent (zoom, scale) |
| **Editability** | Must redraw | Must erase/redraw | Non-destructive edits |
| **3D Capability** | Sketchy isometric | Purely 2D | Native 3D + 2D projection |
| **Cost** | Negligible | Moderate (one-time) | High (license, hardware) |
| **Legal Acceptance** | No | Yes (tender-grade) | Yes (with signature/seal) |
| **KTU Use Case** | Design ideation, exams | Lab internal exam | ESE, project reports |

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Drawing Scales (Mandatory Reference)

> [!NOTE] **The "K" Formula — Scale Representation**
> $$K = \frac{\text{Drawing Length (on sheet)}}{\text{Actual Length (of object)}}$$

| Scale Type | Formula Form | Example | Usage |
|------------|--------------|---------|-------|
| **Full Scale** | $1 : 1$ | $K = 1$ | When object fits the sheet |
| **Enlargement** | $X : 1$ (X > 1) | $5 : 1$ | Small machine parts (gears, fasteners) |
| **Reduction** | $1 : X$ (X > 1) | $1 : 10$ | Large assemblies (engine block, building) |

**Standard Reduced Scales (BIS Recommended):** $1:2,\ 1:5,\ 1:10,\ 1:20,\ 1:50,\ 1:100,\ 1:200,\ 1:500,\ 1:1000,\ 1:2000,\ 1:5000$

**Standard Enlarged Scales:** $2:1,\ 5:1,\ 10:1,\ 20:1,\ 50:1,\ 100:1$

**Real-World Engineering Utility:**
- **Architectural plans** → $1:50$ or $1:100$ reduction.
- **Civil site plans** → $1:1000$ or $1:5000$ reduction.
- **Machine part detail** → $5:1$ or $10:1$ enlargement.
- **PCB layout** → $2:1$ or $4:1$ enlargement.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Pitfall — "Scale" is dimensionless.** It is a *ratio*, so $1:5$ means the drawing is **5 times smaller** than the actual object, regardless of unit.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Procedures & Derivation Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Drawing Scale Formula (K = Drawing / Actual)

Let:
- $D$ = Length measured on the drawing sheet
- $A$ = True (actual) length of the object
- $K$ = Scale factor (a dimensionless ratio)

**Step 1 — Define the scale as a ratio of like quantities:**

$$K = \frac{D}{A}$$

Both $D$ and $A$ must be in the **same unit** (e.g., both in mm) so the units cancel, leaving a pure number.

**Step 2 — Express the actual length in terms of the drawing length:**

$$A = \frac{D}{K}$$

**Step 3 — Express the drawing length in terms of actual length:**

$$D = K \cdot A$$

**Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU 3-mark style):**

> *A machine shaft has an actual length of 480 mm. It is to be drawn at a scale of 1:8. Find the length to be drawn on the sheet.*

Given:
- $A = 480$ mm
- $K = 1 : 8 \Rightarrow K = \dfrac{1}{8}$

Calculation:

$$D = K \cdot A = \frac{1}{8} \times 480 \text{ mm}$$

$$D = 60 \text{ mm}$$

**Answer:** The shaft must be drawn **60 mm long** on the sheet.

**Step 5 — Reverse Problem (Enlargement):**

> *A bolt of actual diameter 6 mm is drawn 30 mm in diameter. Find the scale.*

$$K = \frac{D}{A} = \frac{30}{6} = 5$$

$$K = 5 : 1 \quad \text{(enlarged 5 times)}$$

---

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Manual Drawing Procedure — Bisection of a Line (KTU Practical)

**Problem:** Divide a 100 mm horizontal line AB into **4 equal parts** using a *mini drafter and 30°–60° set square* (a classic 3-mark question).

**Step 1:** Draw the line AB = 100 mm using the mini drafter's horizontal scale.

**Step 2:** From point A, draw a ray AC at **30°** (or 60°) to AB, using the set square.

**Step 3:** On ray AC, mark **4 equal arbitrary steps** with dividers (e.g., points P1, P2, P3, P4 at equal spacing).

**Step 4:** Connect P4 to B using a straight edge.

**Step 5:** Through P3, P2, P1, draw lines **parallel to P4B** using the set square.

**Step 6:** The intersections on AB divide it into 4 equal parts of 25 mm each.

**Verification:**

$$AB = 4 \times 25 = 100 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE] **Why this works (The "Why"):** By the **Basic Proportionality Theorem (Thales' Theorem)**, if a set of parallel lines cuts off equal segments on one transversal (ray AC), it cuts off equal segments on every other transversal (line AB). Hence $AP_1' = P_1'P_2' = P_2'P_3' = P_3'B$.

---

### 3.3 Step-by-Step CAD Command Sequence — Drawing a Hexagonal Nut (KTU Practical)

> *Model task: Draw a hexagonal nut of side 20 mm across flats in AutoCAD, with a central hole of diameter 15 mm.*

**Step 1 — Set Units & Limits**

```
Command: UNITS  → Decimal, Millimetres
Command: LIMITS  → 0,0 to 420,297  (A3 sheet)
```

**Step 2 — Draw Construction Circle**

The hexagon is inscribed in a circle of radius equal to the **across-flats (AF)** distance:
$$R_{\text{circle}} = AF = 20 \text{ mm}$$

```
Command: CIRCLE  → centre 100,100, radius 20
```

**Step 3 — Inscribe the Hexagon (POLYGON Command)**

```
Command: POLYGON  → sides = 6, centre = 100,100
               → inscribed in circle, radius = 20
```

**Step 4 — Draw the Central Hole**

```
Command: CIRCLE  → centre 100,100, radius 7.5
                  (since diameter = 15 mm → radius = 7.5 mm)
```

**Step 5 — Add Centre Lines**

Activate the *Centre* layer (linetype = *CENTER2*), then:
```
Command: LINE  → from 75,100 to 125,100   (horizontal centre)
Command: LINE  → from 100,75 to 100,125   (vertical centre)
```

**Step 6 — Dimension**

```
Command: DIMLINEAR  → on opposite flats = 20 mm
Command: DIMRADIAL → on hole = R 7.5 (or diameter 15)
```

**Step 7 — Hatching the Section View (if shown in section)**

```
Command: HATCH  → pattern = ANSI31, scale = 2, angle = 45°
```

**Step 8 — Save & Plot**

```
Ctrl + S  → Save as NutDrawing.dwg
Ctrl + P  → Plot at 1:1 scale
```

**Verification — Across Flats Formula for a Hexagon:**

$$AF = 2 \cdot R \cdot \cos\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right) = R\sqrt{3}$$

Confirming our construction: $20 = R \cdot \sqrt{3}$ for a hexagon **inscribed** in a circle of radius R. Here we set $R = 20$, which means the hexagon's across-flats is $\dfrac{20}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 11.547$ mm. **Correction:** To get AF = 20 mm, we should use $R = \dfrac{20}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 11.547$ mm and choose *circumscribed* mode, or keep $R = 20$ and interpret "AF" as the **across-corners (AC)** distance.

**Corrected Construction:**

$$AC = 2R = 40 \text{ mm (across corners)}$$

$$AF = R\sqrt{3} = 20\sqrt{3} \approx 34.64 \text{ mm (across flats)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 — Memorise these two hexagon relations:**
> $$AF = R\sqrt{3}, \qquad AC = 2R$$
> Always confirm whether the question specifies "across flats" or "across corners" before drawing.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Drawing Mode

| Scenario | Recommended Mode | Reason |
|----------|------------------|--------|
| Quick idea sharing in a meeting | Free Hand | Speed over precision |
| First-year B.Tech lab internal | Manual | Tests instrument skill (KTU mandate) |
| Final year project report | CAD | Modern industry standard |
| Patent filing (India) | Manual OR CAD | Both accepted if signed |
| Workshop floor fabrication | Manual (often) | Print-friendly, no software dependency |
| CNC/3D-print manufacturing | CAD (with .dxf/.stl export) | Required digital input |
| Government tender submission | Manual with wet signature | Legal traceability |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Drawing Workflow Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Engineer Visualises Object in 3D Mind] --> B1{Choose Drawing Mode}
    B1 -- Rapid Idea --> C1[Free Hand Sketching]
    B1 -- Formal Record --> C2[Manual Instrument Drawing]
    B1 -- Digital Output --> C3[CAD Software Workflow]
    C1 --> D1[Proportional Check by Eye]
    C2 --> D2[Instrument Based Construction]
    C3 --> D3[Command Based Vector Geometry]
    D1 --> E1[2D / Isometric Sketch on Paper]
    D2 --> E2[2D Orthographic Views on Sheet]
    D3 --> E3[2D Drafts or 3D Solid Models]
    E1 --> F1[Annotation Dimensioning]
    E2 --> F1
    E3 --> F1
    F1 --> G1[Title Block and Border]
    G1 --> H1[Final Output Sheet Drawing or File]
```

### 4.2 CAD Drawing Internal Command Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Start New DWG File] --> P2[Set Units to mm and Limits A4]
    P2 --> P3[Create Layers Object Centre Dim Hidden]
    P3 --> P4[Set UCS and Snap Grid]
    P4 --> P5[Draw Centre Lines on Centre Layer]
    P5 --> P6[Construct Geometry using Line Circle Arc]
    P6 --> P7[Modify using Trim Offset Mirror Fillet]
    P7 --> P8[Add Dimensions using Dim commands]
    P8 --> P9[Hatch Sectioned Regions with ANSI31]
    P9 --> P10[Insert Title Block from Template]
    P10 --> P11[Save as DWG and Plot to PDF]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Drawing Mode

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{What is the End Use of the Drawing}
    Q1 -- Brainstorm or Concept Stage --> R1[Use Free Hand Sketching]
    Q1 -- Academic Lab or Tender --> R2{Is Digital Output Required}
    R2 -- No --> S1[Use Manual Instrument Drawing]
    R2 -- Yes --> S2[Use CAD Software with Plot Output]
    Q1 -- Manufacturing or Simulation --> T1[Use CAD with Native 3D Solid Model]
    Q1 -- Field Measurement --> U1[Use Manual Sketchbook First then Convert to CAD]
```

### 4.4 Anatomy of a Standard KTU Drawing Sheet (Block Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SheetA4[A4 Drawing Sheet 210 x 297 mm] --> Border[Border Line 10 mm Inside]
    Border --> Zone1[Folding Marks Top and Bottom]
    Border --> Zone2[Drawing Area 180 x 277 mm]
    Border --> Zone3[Title Block Bottom Right 185 x 65 mm]
    Zone3 --> TB1[Name of Part]
    Zone3 --> TB2[Material Specification]
    Zone3 --> TB3[Scale and Projection Symbol]
    Zone3 --> TB4[Drawing Number]
    Zone3 --> TB5[Drafter and Checker Signatures]
    Zone2 --> VA[Orthographic Views Front Top Side]
    Zone2 --> VA2[Isometric or Sectional View]
    Zone2 --> VA3[Dimensioning and Notes Block]
```

> [!NOTE] **Diagram Reading Note (KTU Exam Tip):** When you reproduce the title block in your exam answer sheet, the *projection symbol (1st angle)* must appear **inside** the title block — not floating in the drawing area. Examiners specifically look for this.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Differentiate between First-Angle and Third-Angle Projection. Which one is recommended by BIS and the KTU 2024 Scheme?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | First-Angle Projection | Third-Angle Projection |
|--------|------------------------|------------------------|
| **Standard** | **BIS (India) and ISO** | ANSI (USA) |
| **Object placement** | Between observer and projection plane | Projection plane between observer and object |
| **Top view position** | Below the front view | Above the front view |
| **Left side view** | To the right of the front view | To the left of the front view |

**BIS and KTU 2024 mandate First-Angle Projection.** *(1 mark for table, 1 mark for placement logic, 1 mark for KTU/BIS statement.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Retest]**
*List any six standard drawing instruments used in manual drafting and state the function of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks = 6 × 0.5 marks):**

1. **Mini Drafter** — Combines T-square and set squares; draws horizontal, vertical, and inclined lines.
2. **Compass** — Draws circles and arcs of specified radii.
3. **Dividers** — Transfers and compares distances.
4. **Protractor** — Measures and constructs angles from 0° to 180°.
5. **Set Squares (45° and 30°–60°)** — Draws inclined lines and parallel/perpendicular constructions.
6. **French Curves** — Draws smooth non-circular curves.
7. **Drawing Sheet (A4/A3)** — The media on which the drawing is produced.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each ESE Part B question carries **14 marks** and may include sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. *Answer any one full question from the choice.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — July 2024, Supplied Model]**

**(a)** *A machine component has an actual length of 840 mm. It is to be represented on an A3 sheet (420 × 297 mm) such that the longest dimension fits within 240 mm of the drawing area. Calculate a suitable standard BIS scale for the drawing and state whether it is a reduction or enlargement.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Explain with a neat sketch the procedure to construct a regular hexagon inscribed in a circle of 50 mm diameter using (i) a compass and straightedge, and (ii) a 30°–60° set square. Also derive the relation between the across-flats (AF) and across-corners (AC) distances of the hexagon.* **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Scale Calculation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Identify the limiting factor:**

The longest object dimension must fit within the drawing area.

$$A = 840 \text{ mm}, \qquad D_{\text{max}} = 240 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the minimum scale factor:**

$$K = \frac{D}{A} = \frac{240}{840} = \frac{2}{7} \approx 0.2857$$

**Step 3 — Select the next smaller standard BIS reduction scale:**

From the standard scale list ($1:2,\ 1:5,\ 1:10,\ 1:20,\ \ldots$), the next smaller reduction is:

$$K_{\text{std}} = 1:5 = 0.20$$

**Step 4 — Verify that the drawing fits:**

$$D = 0.20 \times 840 = 168 \text{ mm} \le 240 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{K = 1:5 \quad \text{(Reduction scale)}}$$

> [!NOTE] **Why not 1:4?** Although 1:4 is mathematically workable, **BIS only allows the standard ratios listed in SP 46**. Always quote a *standard* scale in KTU exams.

---

**Part (b) — Hexagon Construction & Derivation (7 marks)**

**Method (i) — Compass and Straightedge:**

1. Draw a circle of diameter 50 mm (radius R = 25 mm) with centre O.
2. With the **same radius** (25 mm) and centre on the circle, cut arcs to obtain six equally spaced points on the circumference.
3. Join consecutive points in sequence to form the hexagon.

**Method (ii) — Set Square Method:**

1. Draw the horizontal centre line and the vertical centre line.
2. Place the 30°–60° set square with its 60° edge on the vertical centre line.
3. Slide along the horizontal centre line to draw 30° and 150° inclined lines through the centre — these are two of the hexagon's sides.
4. Rotate/flip the set square to obtain the other four sides at 60° increments.

**Derivation of the AF–AC Relation:**

In a regular hexagon, the **across-corners (AC) distance** is the diameter of the circumscribing circle:

$$AC = 2R$$

The **across-flats (AF) distance** is the perpendicular distance between two opposite parallel sides. The hexagon can be split into 6 equilateral triangles, each of side $R$.

In each equilateral triangle, the height is:

$$h = R \cdot \sin(60^\circ) = R \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$$

The across-flats distance equals twice this height:

$$AF = 2h = 2R \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = R\sqrt{3}$$

**Final relation:**

$$\boxed{AF = R\sqrt{3} = \frac{AC \cdot \sqrt{3}}{2}}$$

**Verification (numerical):** For R = 25 mm:
- $AC = 2 \times 25 = 50$ mm ✓ (matches the given diameter)
- $AF = 25\sqrt{3} \approx 43.30$ mm

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** *Explain the BIS conventions for line types used in engineering drawings. List any four standard line types with their thickness ratios and applications.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *With the help of a neat flowchart, explain the step-by-step workflow of producing an orthographic projection of a machine component in AutoCAD. State at least six commands and their purpose.* **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — BIS Line Conventions (7 marks = 4 × 1.5 marks + table bonus)**

> [!IMPORTANT] **BIS line thickness convention (per SP 46:2003):**
> - **Thick lines:** **0.5 mm to 0.7 mm** (continuous) — for visible edges and outlines.
> - **Thin lines:** **~0.35 mm** (about **half** the thick line) — for hidden, centre, dimension, and hatching lines.

| Line Type | Line Style (Visual) | Thickness | Application |
|-----------|---------------------|-----------|-------------|
| **Continuous Thick** | —————— | 0.5–0.7 mm | Visible edges, outlines, visible curves |
| **Continuous Thin** | —————— | 0.35 mm | Dimension lines, extension lines, leader lines, hatching, section lines |
| **Dashed Thin (Hidden)** | - - - - - - | 0.35 mm | Hidden edges, hidden contours |
| **Chain Thin (Centre)** | —·—·—·— | 0.35 mm | Centre lines, pitch circles, trajectories |
| **Chain Thick (Cutting Plane)** | =·=·=·= | 0.5–0.7 mm | Cutting plane lines for sections |
| **Phantom Thin** | -·-·- | 0.35 mm | Adjacent parts, repeated detail, alternative positions |

**Note on Gap Convention:** Hidden and centre lines should **start and end with a dash**, and **intersect at dashes** (not at gaps).

---

**Part (b) — AutoCAD Workflow (7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Flowchart (auto-graded by the examiner for completeness):**

| Step | Command / Action | Purpose |
|------|------------------|---------|
| 1 | `NEW` → Select ISO A3 template (`.dwt`) | Start a standard sheet with title block |
| 2 | `UNITS` → Decimal, mm | Set measurement system |
| 3 | `LAYER` → Create layers: `OBJ`, `CENT`, `DIM`, `HID`, `HATCH` | Logical grouping for clean edits |
| 4 | `LIMITS` → Set A3 extents | Define the printable region |
| 5 | `LINE`, `CIRCLE`, `PLINE`, `RECT` | Construct the primary geometry of the part |
| 6 | `OFFSET`, `TRIM`, `EXTEND`, `MIRROR` | Modify geometry to derive multiple views |
| 7 | `COPY`, `ARRAY`, `MOVE` | Place the top/side views from the front view |
| 8 | `DIMLINEAR`, `DIMANGULAR`, `DIMRADIAL` | Apply dimensions |
| 9 | `HATCH` → ANSI31, 45°, scale 2 | Section any cut regions |
| 10 | `SAVE` → `.dwg`; `PLOT` → PDF | Deliverable output |

**Six Key Commands with Purpose (1 mark each):**

1. **`LINE`** — Draws straight line segments between two points using coordinate entry.
2. **`CIRCLE`** — Draws a circle by centre-and-radius or centre-and-diameter entry.
3. **`OFFSET`** — Creates a parallel copy of a line/curve at a specified distance — *essential for dimensioning construction lines.*
4. **`TRIM`** — Removes unwanted portions of geometry up to a cutting edge.
5. **`MIRROR`** — Reflects geometry about a mirror line — *used to create symmetric half-views.*
6. **`HATCH`** — Fills closed regions with a pattern (ANSI31 = standard 45° parallel lines) for sectional views.
7. *(Bonus)* **`DIMLINEAR`** — Produces aligned linear dimensions with extension and dimension lines automatically.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING] **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks on "Principles of Drawing" Questions**
> 1. **Wrong projection symbol** — Drawing the 3rd-angle symbol instead of the 1st-angle symbol costs **1–2 marks** instantly. **Always sketch the truncated cone with the small circle on the right** (1st-angle convention).
> 2. **Forgetting the scale declaration** — The title block must show the scale, e.g., `Scale 1:1` or `Scale 1:5`. A drawing without a stated scale loses **1 mark**.
> 3. **Drawing hidden lines as continuous thin** — Hidden lines **must be dashed thin**, never continuous. Misclassification costs the entire dimensioning sub-question.
> 4. **Centre lines not extending beyond the feature** — Centre lines must overshoot the circle by **2–4 mm** at each end. Forgetting this is a recurring 0.5-mark deduction.
> 5. **Confusing AF vs AC for a hexagon** — A hexagon with side 20 mm has $AF = 20\sqrt{3} \approx 34.64$ mm **only if** the 20 mm is the side length. If 20 mm is given as AF, then $R = \dfrac{20}{\sqrt{3}}$.
> 6. **In CAD answers, listing commands without explaining workflow** — The examiner wants the **process** (`Start → Set units → Layer → Draw → Modify → Dimension → Hatch → Save → Plot`), not just a list.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Three drawing modes:** Free Hand, Manual (Instrument), CAD — each with distinct tools, accuracy, and use cases.
- [x] **BIS SP 46:2003** is the governing standard. **First-Angle Projection** is mandatory in KTU exams.
- [x] **Sheet sizes** follow the ISO A-series: A0 (largest) to A5 (smallest). A4 (210 × 297 mm) is the standard student sheet.
- [x] **Title block** is always placed in the **bottom-right corner** of the sheet.
- [x] **Line types to memorise:** Continuous Thick (visible), Continuous Thin (dimension/hatch), Dashed Thin (hidden), Chain Thin (centre), Chain Thick (cutting plane), Phantom Thin (adjacent parts).
- [x] **Thick-to-thin line ratio** is approximately **2:1** (0.5–0.7 mm : 0.35 mm).
- [x] **Scale formula:** $K = \dfrac{\text{Drawing Length}}{\text{Actual Length}}$. $K < 1$ = Reduction; $K > 1$ = Enlargement; $K = 1$ = Full scale.
- [x] **Only standard BIS scales** are acceptable in KTU answers: $1:1,\ 1:2,\ 1:5,\ 1:10,\ 1:20,\ 1:50,\ 1:100,\ 2:1,\ 5:1,\ 10:1,$ etc.
- [x] **Hexagon relations:** $AF = R\sqrt{3}$, $AC = 2R$, side length = $R$.
- [x] **Free hand sketches are NEVER measured** — proportion is judged by eye.
- [x] **CAD workflow order:** Units → Limits → Layers → Draw → Modify → Dimension → Hatch → Title Block → Save → Plot.
- [x] **Mini drafter** replaces the legacy T-square + set-square combination in modern KTU labs.
- [x] **Dimensioning units:** Always **millimetres (mm)** unless otherwise specified; angles in degrees.
- [x] **Hidden lines** start and end with a dash, intersect at a dash.
- [x] **Centre lines** extend 2–4 mm beyond the feature and are drawn as long-dash-short-dash-long-dash.
- [x] **Alphanumeric sketch rule for KTU:** No arrowheads on free hand lines — they should be **slightly tapered, hand-drawn lines**, not instrument-style.

> **End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — Principles of Drawing**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme, PCBRL307, Outcome-Based Education framework, aligned with Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyse levels).*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Code of Practice for Engineering Drawing: BIS Specifications, Lines and Types of Lines, Dimensioning, Sectional Views, Welding Symbols, Riveted Joints, Keys, Fasteners (Bolts, Nuts, Screws)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRL307 - MACHINE DRAWING AND SOLID MODELLING
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Code of Practice for Engineering Drawing: BIS Specifications, Lines and Types of Lines, Dimensioning, Sectional Views, Welding Symbols, Riveted Joints, Keys, Fasteners (Bolts, Nuts, Screws)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a "Code of Practice for Engineering Drawing"?

A **Code of Practice for Engineering Drawing** is a standardized set of rules, conventions, symbols, and graphical notations prescribed by national and international bodies to ensure that every engineering drawing communicates design intent **unambiguously**, regardless of the reader's location or industry.

In India, the principal standards are issued by the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)**, formerly the Indian Standards Institution (ISI). The most relevant BIS codes for mechanical engineering drawing are:

| BIS Code | Title | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **IS 696** | Code of practice for general engineering drawing | Lines, scales, dimensioning, projections |
| **IS 2708** | Code of practice for limits and fits | Tolerancing, fits, allowances |
| **SP 46:2003** | Engineering Drawing Practice for Schools & Colleges | Standardized school/college-level practice |
| **IS 8000** | Code of practice for welding symbols | Welding joints and symbols |
| **IS 9191** | Recommendations for limits of surface roughness | Surface finish symbols |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024 Scheme Machine Drawing (PCBRL307), the **single most important code is IS 696** (in conjunction with SP 46:2003). The examiner expects you to draw lines, dimension, section, and represent fasteners *exactly* as prescribed by IS 696. Deviation from BIS conventions is treated as a drawing error and attracts mark deductions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Universal Language" View

Imagine two civil engineers — one from Tokyo, one from Thrissur — meeting to construct a bridge. They do not share a spoken language, but they *both* read a common *blueprint*. That blueprint follows a shared graphical grammar: a circle is a circle, a dashed line means "hidden," a dimension `25` means "25 mm, exactly."

> **Engineering Drawing is to a Mechanical Engineer what Grammar is to a Writer.**

Without a *Code of Practice* (grammar), the drawing (essay) becomes incomprehensible. BIS codes (IS 696, SP 46) act as that shared grammar for India.

## 1.3 Scope of This Module Experiment

This module combines **eight sub-topics** that together form the "vocabulary" of machine drawing:

1. **Lines and Types of Lines** — the *alphabet* of the drawing.
2. **Dimensioning** — the *numerical labels* on the geometry.
3. **Sectional Views** — the *X-ray* of internal features.
4. **Welding Symbols** — shorthand for permanent joints.
5. **Riveted Joints** — the classical permanent joint.
6. **Keys** — the torque-transmitting link between shaft and hub.
7. **Fasteners (Bolts, Nuts, Screws)** — detachable joints.
8. **BIS Specifications (IS 696 / SP 46)** — the rule book binding all of the above.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical constants to memorize:**
> - **Standard sheet sizes (as per IS 696):** A0 = 841 × 1189 mm; A1 = 594 × 841; A2 = 420 × 594; A3 = 297 × 420; A4 = 210 × 297.
> - **Default line thickness ratio:** Thin line ≈ **0.25 ×** Thick line (e.g., 0.5 mm thick → 0.25 mm thin).
> - **First-angle projection is the BIS standard** (IS 696) for India; symbol is a truncated cone placed in the lower-left of the title block.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Lines and Types of Lines (IS 696 / SP 46)

Lines are classified by **function**, not by shape. The eleven standard line types, in order of frequency of use, are tabulated below.

| Line Type | Line Style | Thickness | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Visible (Object) Line** | Continuous | Thick (≈ 0.5–0.7 mm) | Outlines, edges, visible boundaries |
| **Hidden Line** | Dashed (short, even dashes) | Thin | Edges hidden behind the part |
| **Centre Line** | Long dash – short dash – long dash | Thin | Axes of symmetry, centres of circles |
| **Section Line (Hatching)** | Continuous, thin, at 45° | Thin | Cut surfaces in sectional views |
| **Dimension Line** | Continuous | Thin | Extension of dimension values |
| **Extension / Witness Line** | Continuous | Thin | Projecting from the feature to the dimension line |
| **Cutting Plane Line** | Long dash – short dash – thick dash | Thick | Indicates the section plane |
| **Leader Line** | Continuous, thin, terminating in arrow or dot | Thin | Notes, part numbers, callouts |
| **Break Line (Short)** | Continuous, freehand zig-zag | Thick | Short breaks in long uniform sections |
| **Break Line (Long)** | Continuous zig-zag or S-curve | Thin | Long breaks in shafts, tubes |
| **Phantom Line** | Long dash – short dash – short dash | Thin | Alternative positions, adjacent parts |

### Rules of Line Practice
- Two lines that coincide must become the **thicker** of the two.
- Hidden lines should be **omitted** if they make the drawing confusing.
- Centre lines must **extend slightly beyond** the feature they identify.
- Hatching on adjacent parts in the same section must be in **opposite directions**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A common KTU error: drawing visible and hidden lines with the *same* thickness. Examiners specifically deduct 1–2 marks for this.

## 2.2 Dimensioning (IS 696 Clause 7)

Dimensioning answers three questions: **What is the size? Where is it located? How accurate is it?**

**Key elements of a dimension:**
- **Dimension line** — thin, continuous, terminated by **arrowheads** (45° solid wedges) or **oblique strokes**.
- **Extension lines** — thin, with a gap of **1 mm** from the object outline.
- **Dimension text** — placed **above and centred** on the dimension line, in **mm** (the unit symbol may be omitted in mm drawings).

**Rules of Placement (KTU-High-Yield):**
1. Dimensions are placed **outside** the view whenever possible.
2. Dimensions are read from the **bottom** of the sheet (for horizontal) and the **right** of the sheet (for vertical) — *unidirectional system* (IS standard).
3. **Aligned system** (text parallel to dimension line) is also permitted.
4. **Never dimension to a hidden line** unless absolutely necessary.
5. **Avoid duplication** of dimensions.
6. **Hole diameters** use the symbol `Ø` (e.g., `Ø20`), preceded by the word `HOLE` or `DRILL`.
7. **Radii** use `R`, with the centre marked by a small cross.
8. **Chamfers** are dimensioned as `C × 45°` or by note `CHAMFER 2 × 45°`.

## 2.3 Sectional Views

A sectional view reveals the **internal features** by hypothetically cutting the part with a plane and removing the front portion.

**Why section?** — Hidden-line clutter is reduced; internal features become *visible lines*; the drawing is unambiguous.

**Hatching rules (KTU-essential):**
- Hatching is drawn as **thin, parallel, equally spaced lines at 45°** to the principal axes.
- Spacing of hatching lines: **3 to 5 mm**, uniform across the part.
- Different parts in the same section are hatched in **opposite directions** (or with different pitch).
- **Shafts, bolts, nuts, rivets, keys, pins, ribs, spokes** are **never sectioned** even when the cutting plane passes through them — they are shown in **external view**.
- **Ribs in section** are shown unsectioned to avoid a false impression of solidity.

**Types of section:**
- Full section
- Half section
- Offset section
- Aligned section
- Removed (or Revolved) section
- Local (Broken-out) section

## 2.4 Welding Symbols (IS 8000 / AWS A2.1)

A welding symbol is a **compact shorthand** placed on the drawing to specify the type, size, length, and location of a weld.

**Standard welding symbol structure (left-to-right):**

1. **Reference line** — horizontal base.
2. **Arrow** — points to the joint.
3. **Weld symbol** — placed **below** the line = *arrow side*; **above** the line = *other side*.
4. **Tail** — for supplementary information (process, electrode).

**Common weld symbols (KTU must-know):**
- Fillet weld: right-triangle
- Butt / Square weld: flush line
- V-groove: V
- U-groove: U
- Plug / Slot weld: rectangle

## 2.5 Riveted Joints

A riveted joint is a **permanent, non-detachable** joint formed by deforming the tail of a rivet (a cylindrical pin with a pre-formed head) to clamp plates together.

**Classifications (KTU-favourite):**
- **By application of rivets:** Lap joint, Butt joint (single-strap, double-strap).
- **By number of rivet rows:** Single-riveted, Double-riveted, Triple-riveted.
- **By rivet pattern (load direction):** Chain riveting, Zig-zag (staggered) riveting.

**Terminology:**
- **Pitch (p):** centre-to-centre distance between consecutive rivets in a row.
- **Diameter (d):** shank diameter of rivet.
- **Gauge (g):** transverse distance between adjacent rows.
- **Margin (m):** distance from the centre of the rivet hole to the nearest edge of the plate. Standard `m = 1.5 d`.

**Unwin's Formula** for pitch:
$$p = C \cdot d + 0.8 \, t \quad \text{(in mm)}$$
where `C = 3` for chain riveting, `C = 3.5` for zig-zag riveting, `d` in mm, `t` = plate thickness in mm.

**Strength of a riveted joint** — three modes of failure govern design:

| Failure Mode | Per Rivet Capacity (Single Shear) |
|---|---|
| **Shearing of rivet** | $P_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\, d^2 \, \tau$ |
| **Crushing / Bearing of rivet (or plate)** | $P_c = d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c$ |
| **Tearing of plate between rivets** | $P_t = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$ |

**Efficiency of a riveted joint:**
$$\eta = \dfrac{\text{Strength of the joint per pitch length}}{\text{Strength of the un-riveted (solid) plate per pitch length}} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta = \dfrac{\min(P_s, P_c)}{p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t} \times 100\%$$

## 2.6 Keys and Keyways

A key is a small metal piece inserted between a shaft and a hub to **transmit torque** by positive (frictional) engagement.

**Types of keys (IS 2494):**
- Sunk key (rectangular, square, parallel, taper)
- Saddle key (flat, hollow)
- Round key
- Splines (multiple keys integrated into the shaft)

**Design rules of thumb:**
- Width `b = d/4` (shaft diameter)
- Height `h = 1.5 b = (5/16) d` (rectangular sunk key, IS 2292)
- Length `L = 1.5 d` to `2 d`
- For taper key, taper is 1:100 on height.
- **Proportions for square key:** `b × b` where `b = d/4`.

**Shear and crushing strengths of a key:**
- Shearing: $P_s = b \cdot L \cdot \tau_k$
- Crushing: $P_c = \dfrac{h}{2} \cdot L \cdot \sigma_c$

The shaft torque that a single key can transmit:
$$T = P \cdot \frac{d}{2} = \min(P_s, P_c) \cdot \frac{d}{2}$$

## 2.7 Fasteners — Bolts, Nuts, Screws

**Bolt:** a cylindrical shank with a head, threaded at one end; used with a nut.
**Screw:** a bolt with a head but no nut; threads cut into the body being joined.
**Stud:** a bar threaded on both ends.

**Hexagonal head proportions (IS 1364 / ISO 4014):**
- Across flats `s = 1.5 d` (nominal)
- Across corners `e = 1.732 s ≈ 1.732 \cdot 1.5 d`
- Head height `k = 0.7 d`
- Washer thickness `≈ 0.15 d`

**Nut proportions (IS 1363):**
- Across flats `s = 1.5 d` (same as bolt head)
- Thickness `m = d`

**Standard threads (IS 1362 / ISO 261):**
- Metric coarse (M) and Metric fine (MF).
- Designation: `M 10 × 1.5` — major diameter 10 mm, pitch 1.5 mm.

**Strength of a bolt under tension:**
The critical section is at the **root (minor) diameter** $d_c$, called the *core diameter*.
$$P_{tensile} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \, d_c^{\,2} \, \sigma_t$$

**Strength of a bolt in shear (single shear):**
$$P_{shear} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \, d^2 \, \tau$$

**Strength of a bolt in shear (double shear, e.g., clevis):**
$$P_{shear,\,ds} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} \, d^2 \, \tau = \dfrac{\pi}{2} \, d^2 \, \tau$$

**For turning a bolt, the equivalent torsional stress under tension is:**
$$\tau_{eq} = \sqrt{\tau^2 + 3 \tau_t^2} \quad \text{(or use the more rigorous formula)}$$

$$\tau_{eq} = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi d_c^{\,3}} \quad \text{combined with tensile stress} \quad \sigma_t = \dfrac{4 P}{\pi d_c^{\,2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Allowable stresses (typical KTU values, mild steel):**
> - Tensile stress $\sigma_t \approx 80$ N/mm²
> - Shear stress $\tau \approx 60$ N/mm²
> - Crushing stress $\sigma_c \approx 120$ N/mm²

## 2.8 Engineering Utility

> **Real-world impact:** A single misread dimension on a hydraulic cylinder, or a missing welding symbol on a pressure-vessel drawing, can lead to catastrophic failure, multi-million-dollar rework, or — worst of all — loss of life. BIS codes exist precisely to remove this risk by enforcing a uniform visual contract between designer, manufacturer, and inspector. From the auto-rickshaw you rode to college to the turbofan in an Airbus, every component began as a drawing that obeyed a code of practice.

## 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Reference

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rivet pitch (Unwin) | $p = C \cdot d + 0.8 t$ | `C = 3` chain, `3.5` zig-zag |
| 2 | Shear of rivet | $P_s = (\pi / 4) \, d^2 \, \tau$ | Single shear |
| 3 | Crushing of rivet | $P_c = d \cdot t \, \sigma_c$ | Bearing |
| 4 | Plate tearing | $P_t = (p - d) \cdot t \, \sigma_t$ | Between rivets |
| 5 | Joint efficiency | $\eta = \min(P_s, P_c) / (p \, t \, \sigma_t) \times 100$ | As percentage |
| 6 | Key width | $b = d / 4$ | Rectangular sunk |
| 7 | Key height | $h = 1.5 \, b = 5d/16$ | Rectangular sunk |
| 8 | Key shear | $P_s = b \, L \, \tau_k$ | — |
| 9 | Key crushing | $P_c = (h/2) \, L \, \sigma_c$ | — |
| 10 | Torque on shaft | $T = P \cdot (d/2)$ | From key force P |
| 11 | Bolt core area | $A_c = (\pi / 4) \, d_c^{\,2}$ | $d_c \approx 0.84 d$ for ISO coarse |
| 12 | Bolt tensile capacity | $P_t = A_c \cdot \sigma_t$ | — |
| 13 | Bolt single-shear capacity | $P_s = (\pi / 4) \, d^2 \, \tau$ | — |
| 14 | Bolt double-shear capacity | $P_{s,ds} = (\pi / 2) \, d^2 \, \tau$ | — |
| 15 | Hex across flats | $s = 1.5 \, d$ | Bolt / nut |
| 16 | Hex head height | $k = 0.7 \, d$ | Bolt head |
| 17 | Nut thickness | $m = d$ | Hex nut |
| 18 | Margin | $m = 1.5 \, d$ | Plate edge to rivet centre |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Shear Strength of a Single Rivet

### Statement
A cylindrical rivet of diameter $d$ is loaded in single shear. Derive the average shear-stress force capacity $P_s$.

### Step 1 — Identify the shearing plane
When two plates are pulled in opposite directions, the rivet shank is sheared along the **interface plane** between the plates. For single shear, there is **one** such plane.

### Step 2 — Identify the shearing area
The area resisting shear is the **cross-sectional area of the rivet shank** at the shear plane:
$$A_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \, d^2$$

### Step 3 — Apply the average shear-stress relation
By Newton's law of shear, the resisting force equals the allowable shear stress $\tau$ multiplied by the area:
$$P_s = \tau \cdot A_s$$

### Step 4 — Substitute the area expression
$$P_s = \tau \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} \, d^2$$

$$\boxed{P_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \, d^2 \, \tau}$$

### Step 5 — Physical interpretation
Doubling the rivet diameter *quadruples* the shear capacity (area scales as $d^2$). This is why heavy-load joints use **fewer large rivets** rather than many small rivets.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Crushing Strength of a Rivet (Bearing)

### Statement
A rivet bears on the plate. The contact is a **cylindrical projected area** with diameter $d$ and length equal to the plate thickness $t$. Derive the crushing capacity $P_c$.

### Step 1 — Identify the projected bearing area
The rivet presses on the plate along a **rectangle of width $d$ and length $t$** when projected onto the failure plane. Hence:
$$A_b = d \cdot t$$

### Step 2 — Apply the bearing-stress relation
The crushing force equals the allowable bearing stress $\sigma_c$ times the projected area:
$$P_c = \sigma_c \cdot d \cdot t$$

$$\boxed{P_c = d \cdot t \, \sigma_c}$$

### Step 3 — Note on double-shear / double-strap
For a **double-strap butt joint**, the bearing is shared between two strap–plate interfaces. The crushing capacity per rivet becomes $2 \, d \cdot t \, \sigma_c$ if the strap and main plate have the same thickness.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Tearing Strength of Plate Between Rivets

### Statement
The plate may tear along the line passing through the rivet hole, between two consecutive rivets. Derive the tearing capacity per pitch length.

### Step 1 — Identify the net section resisting tension
The plate has a thickness $t$ and a *pitch* $p$. The hole removes material of width $d$. The net section resisting tension is:
$$A_{net} = (p - d) \cdot t$$

### Step 2 — Apply tensile-stress relation
$$P_t = A_{net} \cdot \sigma_t = (p - d) \, t \, \sigma_t$$

$$\boxed{P_t = (p - d) \, t \, \sigma_t}$$

### Step 3 — Pitch selection criterion
For *safe* pitch, the plate must not be the weakest link. Hence:
$$P_t \ge P_s \quad \text{and} \quad P_t \ge P_c$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Efficiency of a Single-Riveted Lap Joint

### Statement
For a single-riveted lap joint with one rivet per pitch, derive the joint efficiency $\eta$.

### Step 1 — Strength of the *joint* per pitch
The joint fails at the weakest of the three modes. The controlling capacity is:
$$P_{joint} = \min \left( \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2 \tau, \, d \, t \, \sigma_c \right)$$

### Step 2 — Strength of the *solid plate* per pitch
The full plate, had it not been drilled, would have resisted:
$$P_{solid} = p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$$

### Step 3 — Define efficiency as the ratio
$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{joint}}{P_{solid}} = \dfrac{\min(P_s, P_c)}{p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t}$$

### Step 4 — Express as a percentage
$$\boxed{\eta \,(\%) = \dfrac{\min(P_s, P_c)}{p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t} \times 100}$$

### Step 5 — Numerical example (KTU-style)
Given: $d = 20$ mm, $t = 12$ mm, $\tau = 80$ MPa, $\sigma_c = 120$ MPa, $\sigma_t = 100$ MPa, chain riveting.

**Compute shear capacity:**
$$P_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (20)^2 \times 80 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 400 \times 80$$

$$P_s = \pi \times 80 \times 100 = 25\,132.7 \, \text{N} \approx 25.13 \, \text{kN}$$

**Compute crushing capacity:**
$$P_c = 20 \times 12 \times 120 = 28\,800 \, \text{N} = 28.80 \, \text{kN}$$

**Controlling mode:** $P_s = 25.13$ kN (shear governs).

**Pitch from Unwin's formula:**
$$p = 3 \times 20 + 0.8 \times 12 = 60 + 9.6 = 69.6 \, \text{mm} \approx 70 \, \text{mm}$$

**Strength of solid plate per pitch:**
$$P_{solid} = 70 \times 12 \times 100 = 84\,000 \, \text{N} = 84.0 \, \text{kN}$$

**Efficiency:**
$$\eta = \dfrac{25.13}{84.0} \times 100 \approx 29.92\%$$

**Analytical matrix:**

| Quantity | Value | Role |
|---|---|---|
| $P_s$ | 25.13 kN | Shear capacity (controlling) |
| $P_c$ | 28.80 kN | Crushing capacity |
| $P_t$ | $(70 - 20) \times 12 \times 100 = 60.0$ kN | Plate-tearing capacity (safe) |
| $p$ | 70 mm | Pitch |
| $\eta$ | 29.9% | Joint efficiency |

> [!NOTE]
> A single-riveted lap joint is rarely used in real bridges or boilers because efficiencies are typically **< 50%**. Multi-row zig-zag riveting pushes efficiency to **70–80%**.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Torque Transmitted by a Sunk Key

### Statement
A rectangular sunk key of width $b$, height $h$, and length $L$ is mounted on a shaft of diameter $d$ transmitting torque $T$. Derive the safe torque.

### Step 1 — Force on the key side
The key is in compression on one flank (the side facing the hub). The bearing area on the key side is $A_b = (h/2) \cdot L$ (only half the height takes the bearing, the other half fits in the keyway).

### Step 2 — Force the key can resist in crushing
$$P_c = \dfrac{h}{2} \cdot L \cdot \sigma_c$$

### Step 3 — Force the key can resist in shear
The shear area is $A_s = b \cdot L$:
$$P_s = b \cdot L \cdot \tau_k$$

### Step 4 — The key fails at the smaller force
$$P = \min(P_c, P_s)$$

### Step 5 — Relate the force to torque
The torque is the force times the shaft radius:
$$T = P \cdot \dfrac{d}{2}$$

$$\boxed{T = \min\!\left(b \, L \, \tau_k, \, \dfrac{h \, L}{2} \, \sigma_c \right) \cdot \dfrac{d}{2}}$$

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Tensile Capacity of a Bolt at the Root Thread

### Statement
A bolt of nominal diameter $d$ with metric ISO threads transmits an axial tensile load. Derive the safe load.

### Step 1 — Identify the critical section
The smallest cross-section of a threaded bolt is at the **root of the thread**, called the *core diameter* $d_c$. For ISO metric coarse threads:
$$d_c \approx d - 1.0825 \, p$$
where $p$ is the thread pitch. For a quick approximation used in KTU problems:
$$d_c \approx 0.84 \, d$$

### Step 2 — Compute the core area
$$A_c = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \, d_c^{\,2}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the tensile-stress relation
$$P_{tensile} = A_c \cdot \sigma_t = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \, d_c^{\,2} \, \sigma_t$$

$$\boxed{P_{tensile} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \, d_c^{\,2} \, \sigma_t}$$

### Step 4 — Numerical example
Given: $M 16$ bolt, $\sigma_t = 100$ MPa, ISO coarse pitch $p = 2$ mm.
$$d_c = 16 - 1.0825 \times 2 = 16 - 2.165 = 13.835 \, \text{mm}$$

$$A_c = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (13.835)^2 = 150.4 \, \text{mm}^2$$

$$P_{tensile} = 150.4 \times 100 = 15\,040 \, \text{N} \approx 15.04 \, \text{kN}$$

## 3.7 Derivation 7 — Equivalent Torsional Stress in a Tightened Bolt

When a bolt is tightened, it experiences both:
- **Tensile stress** $\sigma_t$ from the clamp load.
- **Torsional shear stress** $\tau$ from the friction at the thread helix.

Using the **maximum shear-stress (3rd strength) theory**:
$$\tau_{eq} = \sqrt{\tau^2 + 3\tau_t^2}$$

where $\tau_t$ is the torsional shear stress in the shank:
$$\tau_t = \dfrac{T}{Z_p} = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi d_c^{\,3}}$$

For combined design:
$$\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_t^{\,2} + 3 \tau^2} \le \sigma_{allow}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Hierarchy of BIS Drawing Standards

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[BIS Bureau of Indian Standards] --> B[IS 696 General Engineering Drawing]
    A --> C[SP 46 2003 School and College Practice]
    A --> D[IS 8000 Welding Symbols]

    A --> F[IS 1364 Hexagon Bolts and Screws]
    A --> G[IS 9191 Surface Roughness]

    B --> B4[Projections First Angle]

    C --> C2[Title Block Layout]
    C --> C3[Line Thickness Ratios]
    D --> D1[Fillet and Groove Weld Symbols]
    D --> D2[Arrow Side and Other Side]

```
