<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
List any **six standard types of lines** used in engineering drawing as per IS 696, and state the relative thickness (thick / thin) of each.

#### Model Answer
| # | Line Type | Line Style | Thickness |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Visible (Object) line | Continuous | **Thick** |
| 2 | Hidden line | Dashed, short even dashes | **Thin** |
| 3 | Centre line | Long dash – short dash – long dash | **Thin** |
| 4 | Cutting plane line | Long dash – short dash – thick dash | **Thick** |
| 5 | Section line (hatching) | Continuous, parallel, 45° | **Thin** |
| 6 | Dimension line | Continuous, terminated with arrows | **Thin** |

(Any 6 from the eleven standard types, with thickness correctly stated, fetch full 3 marks.)

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
A double-riveted zig-zag lap joint is to be designed for a plate of thickness $t = 10$ mm using rivets of diameter $d = 18$ mm. Compute the **pitch** using Unwin's formula.

#### Model Answer
**Given:** $d = 18$ mm, $t = 10$ mm, zig-zag riveting → $C = 3.5$.

**Unwin's formula:**
$$p = C \cdot d + 0.8 \, t$$

$$p = 3.5 \times 18 + 0.8 \times 10$$

$$p = 63.0 + 8.0$$

$$\boxed{p = 71.0 \, \text{mm}}$$

(Students commonly write $C = 3$ for zig-zag; the **correct** value is $C = 3.5$ — see 5.4 pitfall below.)

---

### Q3. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
Define *efficiency* of a riveted joint. State the relationship between joint strength, solid-plate strength, and efficiency.

#### Model Answer
**Definition:** The efficiency of a riveted joint is the ratio of the strength of the joint per pitch length to the strength of an un-riveted (solid) plate of the same dimensions, expressed as a percentage.

**Mathematical form:**
$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{joint}}{P_{solid\,plate}} \times 100\%$$

where:
- $P_{joint} = \min(P_s, P_c, P_t)$ — the weakest failure mode.
- $P_{solid\,plate} = p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$ — pitch × thickness × tensile strength of plate material.

A well-designed boiler joint has $\eta \approx 70\text{–}80\%$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> KTU pattern: each Part B question carries **14 marks**, divided into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. You must answer either **Question A** or **Question B**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Sketch the standard **welding symbol** for a 6 mm fillet weld on the *arrow side*, length 50 mm, with 75 mm pitch, and explain the location rule of weld symbols relative to the reference line.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A single-riveted lap joint connects two mild-steel plates of thickness $t = 12$ mm using $d = 20$ mm rivets. The allowable stresses are $\tau = 80$ MPa, $\sigma_c = 120$ MPa, and $\sigma_t = 100$ MPa. Determine the **pitch**, the **strength of the joint per pitch length**, and the **efficiency** of the joint.

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Sketch of the welding symbol (textual description suitable for drawing in script):**

```
                                       (Reference line)
   <-- arrow points to joint ---------  =========== 6 | 50 | S 75   (none)
                                                  |     |    |
                                                  v     v    v
                                              size  length pitch
                                              (mm)   (mm)  (mm)
   Triangle ▽ (fillet weld) is drawn BELOW the reference line = arrow side.
   Tail: optional, e.g.  "SMAW / E6013"
```

**Location rule:**

1. The **reference line** is drawn horizontally.
2. The **arrow** points to the joint location on the parent metal.
3. The **weld symbol** (e.g., the fillet triangle) is placed:
   - **Below** the reference line → weld to be made on the **arrow side** (the side the arrow points to).
   - **Above** the reference line → weld on the **other side**.
4. **Dimensions** appear in this order (left to right) on the reference line: weld *size*, weld *length*, *pitch* (with the symbol `S`).
5. The **tail** at the right (or left) end carries supplementary info — welding process, electrode specification, etc.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Given:**
- $t = 12$ mm, $d = 20$ mm
- $\tau = 80$ MPa, $\sigma_c = 120$ MPa, $\sigma_t = 100$ MPa
- Single-riveted, lap joint → $C = 3$ (chain riveting)

**Step 1 — Pitch (Unwin's formula):**
$$p = 3 \times 20 + 0.8 \times 12 = 60 + 9.6 = 69.6 \approx 70 \, \text{mm}$$

**Step 2 — Shear strength per rivet (single shear):**
$$P_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \, d^2 \, \tau = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 400 \times 80 = 25\,132.7 \, \text{N} \approx 25.13 \, \text{kN}$$

**Step 3 — Crushing strength per rivet:**
$$P_c = d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c = 20 \times 12 \times 120 = 28\,800 \, \text{N} = 28.80 \, \text{kN}$$

**Step 4 — Plate tearing capacity:**
$$P_t = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t = (70 - 20) \times 12 \times 100 = 60\,000 \, \text{N} = 60.0 \, \text{kN}$$

**Step 5 — Strength of the joint (minimum of the three):**
$$P_{joint} = \min(25.13,\, 28.80,\, 60.0) = 25.13 \, \text{kN (shear governs)}$$

**Step 6 — Strength of solid plate per pitch:**
$$P_{solid} = p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t = 70 \times 12 \times 100 = 84\,000 \, \text{N} = 84.0 \, \text{kN}$$

**Step 7 — Efficiency:**
$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{joint}}{P_{solid}} \times 100 = \dfrac{25.13}{84.0} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 29.92\%}$$

**Summary table:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Pitch $p$ | 70 mm |
| $P_s$ | 25.13 kN (controlling) |
| $P_c$ | 28.80 kN |
| $P_t$ | 60.0 kN |
| Joint strength | 25.13 kN |
| Efficiency $\eta$ | 29.92% |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the **three types of sectional views**: full section, half section, and offset section. State **why shafts, bolts, and keys are not sectioned** even when the cutting plane passes through them.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A $M 20$ bolt of ISO metric coarse thread (pitch $p = 2.5$ mm) is used to clamp a cylinder cover. The allowable tensile stress is $\sigma_t = 100$ MPa and the allowable shear stress is $\tau = 60$ MPa. Determine:
- (i) the **tensile load capacity** of the bolt at the root thread,
- (ii) the **single-shear capacity** of the bolt.

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Three sectional views — sketch description:**

```
+ Full section:         |         + Half section:          |     + Offset section:
|                       |         |                        |     |
|   ____________        |         |   ___________         |     |   ____________
|  |            |       |         |  |  (upper)   |        |     |  |  (cut at two
|  |  section   |       |         |  |  = outside |        |     |  |   different
|  |   through  |       |         |  |  view      |        |     |  |   levels)
|  |  entire    |       |         |  |  lower =    |        |     |  |   45° steps
|  |  object    |       |         |  |  section   |        |     |  |   bring
|  |____________|       |         |  |   view     |        |     |  |   features
|  hatching 45°         |         |  |___________|        |     |  |   to one plane
+-----------------------+         +-----------------------+     +--+______________+
```

- **Full section:** Cutting plane passes entirely through the object. The front half is removed; the cut surface is hatched.
- **Half section:** Used for symmetric objects. One quarter is removed; one half shows outside view, the other half shows section. Saves drawing time.
- **Offset section:** Cutting plane is "stepped" (offset by 90° bends) to pass through features that lie in different planes. All features appear in one section.

**Why shafts, bolts, nuts, keys, pins, ribs, spokes are *not* sectioned:**

1. Sectioning these solid, slender, rotationally symmetric members would give a *false impression* of a hollow interior.
2. The standard practice (IS 696) requires them to be shown in **external view** for clarity.
3. Ribs in particular are left unsectioned to avoid the misleading appearance of a solid disk.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Given:**
- $M 20$ bolt → nominal diameter $d = 20$ mm
- Pitch $p = 2.5$ mm
- $\sigma_t = 100$ MPa, $\tau = 60$ MPa

**Step 1 — Core (minor) diameter:**
$$d_c = d - 1.0825 \, p = 20 - 1.0825 \times 2.5 = 20 - 2.706 = 17.294 \, \text{mm}$$

**Step 2 — Core area:**
$$A_c = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \, d_c^{\,2} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (17.294)^2$$

$$A_c = 0.7854 \times 299.08 = 234.93 \, \text{mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Tensile load capacity (at root):**
$$P_t = A_c \cdot \sigma_t = 234.93 \times 100 = 23\,493 \, \text{N}$$

$$\boxed{P_t \approx 23.49 \, \text{kN}}$$

**Step 4 — Single-shear capacity (at shank):**
$$P_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \, d^2 \, \tau = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (20)^2 \times 60$$

$$P_s = 0.7854 \times 400 \times 60 = 18\,850 \, \text{N}$$

$$\boxed{P_s \approx 18.85 \, \text{kN}}$$

**Comparison:**

| Loading Mode | Capacity | Controls for? |
|---|---|---|
| Tension at root thread | 23.49 kN | Axial pull-out of the bolt |
| Single shear at shank | 18.85 kN | Transverse load on the bolt |

The bolt is **stronger in tension** (because the shank diameter is larger than the core diameter). The governing design check is usually the **shear** in applications where the bolt acts like a pin.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 mark-losing mistakes on this module — Avoid them!**
> 1. **Confusing $C$ in Unwin's formula.** $C = 3$ for *chain* riveting, $C = 3.5$ for *zig-zag* riveting. Many students write $C = 3$ for both — deduct ½ mark.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units.** $d$ in mm, $t$ in mm, stresses in N/mm² (MPa) → final answer in **N** or **kN**. If you leave the answer in N/mm² the examiner will treat it as zero.
> 3. **Using nominal diameter $d$ instead of core diameter $d_c$** for tensile strength of bolts. The threads reduce the effective section; failure occurs at the **root**.
> 4. **Sectioning shafts, bolts, keys, and ribs.** IS 696 explicitly forbids this. Each occurrence costs ½ mark.
> 5. **Placing welding symbols on the wrong side of the reference line.** Arrow side = below; other side = above. Examiner checks the first one and gives 0 if reversed.
> 6. **Not mentioning the unit "mm"** in dimension values. SP 46 says the unit symbol may be omitted, but the word "MILLIMETRES" must appear in the title block — a missing unit indication attracts a ½-mark penalty.
> 7. **Confusing pitch of rivets with pitch of threads.** They are completely different concepts.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist**

**1. BIS codes to remember:**
- **IS 696** — general engineering drawing (lines, dimensioning, sections, first-angle projection)
- **SP 46:2003** — Indian school/college drawing practice
- **IS 8000** — welding symbols
- **IS 2494** — keys
- **IS 1364** — hex bolts and screws
- **IS 9191** — surface roughness

**2. Lines (must draw 11 types correctly):**
- Visible: thick, continuous.
- Hidden: thin, dashed.
- Centre: thin, long-dash/short-dash.
- Cutting plane: thick, long-dash/short-dash.
- Hatching: thin, continuous, 45°.

**3. Dimensioning essentials:**
- Dimension line *thin*, with arrowheads.
- Extension line with 1 mm gap from outline.
- Holes: `Ø d`; radii: `R d`.
- Avoid dimensioning to hidden lines.

**4. Sectioning essentials:**
- Hatching 45°, thin, 3–5 mm pitch.
- Never section: shaft, bolt, nut, key, pin, rib, spoke.
- Hatching alternates direction for adjacent parts.

**5. Welding symbol essentials:**
- Reference line horizontal; arrow points to joint.
- Below = arrow side; above = other side.
- Order: size — length — pitch (`S`).

**6. Riveted joint essentials:**
- $p = C d + 0.8 t$ ($C = 3$ chain, $3.5$ zig-zag).
- $P_s = (\pi/4) d^2 \tau$ (single shear).
- $P_c = d t \sigma_c$ (crushing).
- $P_t = (p - d) t \sigma_t$ (plate tearing).
- $\eta = \min(P_s, P_c) / (p t \sigma_t) \times 100$.
- Margin $m = 1.5 d$.

**7. Key essentials:**
- $b = d/4$, $h = 1.5 b = 5d/16$ (rectangular sunk).
- $L \approx 1.5 d$ to $2 d$.
- $P_s = b L \tau_k$, $P_c = (h/2) L \sigma_c$.
- $T = P \cdot d/2$.

**8. Fastener essentials:**
- Hex across flats $s = 1.5 d$; head height $k = 0.7 d$.
- Nut thickness $m = d$.
- Bolt core diameter $d_c = d - 1.0825 p \approx 0.84 d$.
- Tensile: $P_t = (\pi/4) d_c^2 \sigma_t$.
- Single shear: $P_s = (\pi/4) d^2 \tau$.
- Double shear: $P_{s,ds} = (\pi/2) d^2 \tau$.

**9. Numerical constants to memorize:**
- Across-corners / across-flats of hex: $\sqrt{3} = 1.732$.
- Thread-engagement factor: $1.0825$.
- $\pi/4 = 0.7854$.

**10. Mnemonic for line types (KTU-trick):**
> **"Very Hungry Cats Crave Fresh Hashed Delicacies"**
> Visible • Hidden • Centre • Cutting-plane • Freehand-break • Hatching • Dimension

**Final Examiner Tip:** Whenever you draw a riveted joint, bolt, or weld, always write the **assumed allowable stresses** in the calculation column. The examiner will give 1 mark extra for a "well-stated assumption list."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Limits, Fits, and Tolerances: Tolerances of Individual Dimensions, Specification of Fits, Geometric and Dimensional Tolerances, Surface Roughness and Indication
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRL307 - MACHINE DRAWING AND SOLID MODELLING
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Limits, Fits, and Tolerances: Tolerances of Individual Dimensions, Specification of Fits, Geometric and Dimensional Tolerances, Surface Roughness and Indication

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Limits, Fits & Tolerances — The Fundamental Trilogy

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PCBRL307 – Machine Drawing and Solid Modelling**, the study of *Limits, Fits, and Tolerances* forms the numerical backbone of all functional engineering drawings. Without an unambiguous specification of how "loose" or "tight" a part must be, no two manufactured components can ever be guaranteed to assemble correctly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> - **Limit:** The extreme permissible values (maximum and minimum) within which a given dimension (size or length) must lie for the part to be acceptable. There are two limits per dimension — the **Upper Limit (UL)** and the **Lower Limit (LL)**.
> - **Tolerance (of an Individual Dimension):** The *total permissible variation* of a dimension, defined mathematically as `T = Upper Limit − Lower Limit`. Tolerance is **never a negative** value; it represents the size of the acceptance window.
> - **Fit:** The *degree of tightness* or looseness between two mating parts (a hole and a shaft) before they are fastened. It is determined entirely by the algebraic difference between the hole and shaft tolerances.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Footwear Size Analogy"

Imagine you are ordering a pair of shoes online. Your *true* foot length is **260 mm**, but the shop cannot guarantee an exact match.

- The shoe manufacturer says, *"We will deliver any shoe in the range 258 mm to 262 mm."* → The **limits** of the shoe size.
- The manufacturer accepts a 4 mm variation (262 − 258) → This is the **tolerance** band of the shoe.
- Now your foot (shaft) is exactly 260 mm, and the shoe (hole) is 261 mm. There is a **1 mm gap** → This is the **fit** (specifically, a *clearance fit* because the gap is positive).

Just as a footwear company prints a *size label with an accepted comfort range*, every engineering dimension on a KTU drawing must carry its own **nominal size + tolerance band**, encoded numerically (e.g., `Ø30 H7/g6`) so that any factory anywhere in the world — Kerala, Stuttgart, or Tokyo — can produce mating parts that assemble correctly.

### 1.3 Nominal, Basic, Actual, and Design Sizes

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Nominal Size** | D | The labelled size on the drawing (e.g., Ø30). It is a *reference* value only. |
| **Basic Size** | D | Same as nominal in ISO/IS 919; used to calculate deviations. |
| **Actual Size** | — | The *measured* size of the manufactured part. |
| **Upper Limit** | UL | Maximum permissible size of the part. |
| **Lower Limit** | LL | Minimum permissible size of the part. |
| **Maximum Material Condition (MMC)** | — | The size containing the *most material* (for a hole → LL; for a shaft → UL). |
| **Least Material Condition (LMC)** | — | The size containing the *least material* (for a hole → UL; for a shaft → LL). |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** The **deviation** is the algebraic difference between a limit and the basic size.
> - **Upper Deviation** = Upper Limit − Basic Size
> - **Lower Deviation** = Lower Limit − Basic Size
> The *tolerance* is the difference of deviations (Upper Deviation − Lower Deviation), which equals the tolerance zone width.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Tolerance of an Individual Dimension

An individual tolerance is the permissible variation allowed on **a single dimension**, independent of any other dimension or geometric feature. The KTU/IS 919 system (which is essentially the ISO system adapted as the Indian Standard) prescribes a series of **fundamental tolerances (i)** and **fundamental deviations (upper for holes, lower for shafts)** that standardise this variation.

### 2.2 The Three Families of Fits (IS 919)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The three fit families, by KTU 2024 syllabus mandate:**
> 1. **Clearance Fit:** Shaft diameter is *always smaller* than the hole diameter across the entire tolerance band. Used for sliding pairs, bearings, loose pulleys.
> 2. **Interference Fit (Force Fit):** Shaft diameter is *always larger* than the hole diameter. Used for permanent couplings, gear hubs on shafts, bushings.
> 3. **Transition Fit:** May be either clearance or interference depending on the actual realised sizes. Used for precision locating, removable keys, dowels.

### 2.3 Hole-Basis vs Shaft-Basis System

- **Hole-Basis System:** The hole is fixed as the *reference* (always H) and varying tolerance grades are applied to the shaft (e.g., g6, k6, p6). This is the **preferred and universally recommended** system in IS 919 and ISO 286.
- **Shaft-Basis System:** The shaft is fixed (always h) and the hole varies (e.g., H7, K7, P7). Used only for specific industrial reasons (e.g., when a standard shaft is cut to length from stock).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Tolerance (T)** | $T = UL - LL$ | mm | Always positive |
| 2 | **Tolerance from Deviations** | $T = es - ei$ (shaft) or $ES - EI$ (hole) | mm | Upper deviation − lower deviation |
| 3 | **Upper Limit** | $UL = Basic + es$ (shaft) or $Basic + ES$ (hole) | mm | |
| 4 | **Lower Limit** | $LL = Basic + ei$ (shaft) or $Basic + EI$ (hole) | mm | |
| 5 | **Fundamental Tolerance (i)** | $i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{D} + 0.001 \cdot D$ | µm | For D in mm; D ≤ 500 mm |
| 6 | **Fundamental Tolerance (IT Grade)** | $IT = k \cdot i$ | µm | k = 7, 10, 16… for IT7, IT8, IT9… |
| 7 | **Maximum Clearance** | $C_{max} = H_{max} - s_{min} = ES - ei$ | mm | Clearance Fit |
| 8 | **Minimum Clearance** | $C_{min} = H_{min} - s_{max} = EI - es$ | mm | |
| 9 | **Maximum Interference** | $I_{max} = s_{max} - H_{min} = es - EI$ | mm | Interference Fit |
| 10 | **Minimum Interference** | $I_{min} = s_{min} - H_{max} = ei - ES$ | mm | |
| 11 | **Allowance** | $A = \vert C_{min} \vert$ or $\vert I_{min} \vert$ | mm | Tightest fit condition |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbol convention (mandatory for KTU 2024):**
> - *Lower-case* letters (`es`, `ei`) → **shaft** deviations.
> - *Upper-case* letters (`ES`, `EI`) → **hole** deviations.
> - `e` (or `E`) = upper deviation, `i` (or `I`) = lower deviation (mnemonic: "e → exit on top, i → index below").

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

This system is the *lingua franca* of global manufacturing. A KTU B.Tech Mechanical/Aeronautical engineer specifying a `Ø25 H7/g6` fit tells the supplier — without ambiguity — that the hole is 25 mm with an IT7 tolerance and the shaft has a transition clearance, and any machinist with a caliper, micrometer, or CMM can verify compliance. It is used in engine pistons, gearbox shafts, bearing housings, aerospace fasteners, biomedical implants, and even consumer electronics (laptop hinge pins, drone motor shafts).

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Specifying a Clearance Fit (Hole-Basis)

**Problem:** Specify a clearance fit between a hole and shaft of basic size **Ø40 mm** using **H7/f6**. Determine (a) the limits of hole and shaft, (b) the maximum and minimum clearance, and (c) the tolerance on each. Use IS 919 / ISO 286 data.

### Given Data
- Basic Size $D = 40$ mm (which lies in the 30–50 mm diameter step).
- Hole: H7 → Lower deviation $EI = 0$ (always for H), IT7 = 25 µm (standard value).
- Shaft: f6 → Upper deviation `es` is negative; IT6 = 16 µm. For `f` symbol, fundamental deviation (upper) is $-16 \cdot \sqrt[3]{D} \cdot k$ … in practice we use IS tables: for f6 in the 30–50 mm range, $es = -25$ µm, $ei = es - IT6$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Fundamental Tolerance for IT7 at 40 mm (from standard tables):**
Standard ISO/IS 286 table gives $IT7 = 25$ µm for the 30–50 mm diameter step.

**Step 2 — Hole H7 limits (since EI = 0 for all H holes):**

$$Hole\_Lower\_Limit = Basic + EI = 40.000 + 0.000 = 40.000 \text{ mm}$$

$$Hole\_Upper\_Limit = Basic + ES = Basic + (EI + IT7) = 40.000 + 0.025 = 40.025 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Shaft f6 limits (from standard tables, f6 at 30–50 mm range):**
Standard tables give for f6: $es = -25$ µm, $ei = -41$ µm.

$$Shaft\_Upper\_Limit = Basic + es = 40.000 + (-0.025) = 39.975 \text{ mm}$$

$$Shaft\_Lower\_Limit = Basic + ei = 40.000 + (-0.041) = 39.959 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Tolerance on Hole and Tolerance on Shaft:**

$$T_{hole} = 40.025 - 40.000 = 0.025 \text{ mm} = 25 \text{ µm} \quad (IT7)$$

$$T_{shaft} = 39.975 - 39.959 = 0.016 \text{ mm} = 16 \text{ µm} \quad (IT6)$$

**Step 5 — Maximum and Minimum Clearance:**

$$C_{max} = H_{max} - s_{min} = 40.025 - 39.959 = 0.066 \text{ mm} = 66 \text{ µm}$$

$$C_{min} = H_{min} - s_{max} = 40.000 - 39.975 = 0.025 \text{ mm} = 25 \text{ µm}$$

**Step 6 — Allowance (the tightest condition, the minimum clearance):**
$$A = C_{min} = 25 \text{ µm} = 0.025 \text{ mm}$$

This is a **running clearance fit** suitable for sliding bearings, plain bushings, or low-speed journal support.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Specifying an Interference Fit (Hole-Basis)

**Problem:** Specify an interference fit for a gear hub to be press-fitted on a shaft of basic size **Ø60 mm**, using **H7/s6**. Compute limits, tolerances, and the maximum/minimum interference.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Reference Data (60 mm falls in the 50–80 mm diameter step):**
- $IT7 = 30$ µm
- $IT6 = 19$ µm
- For s6 in 50–80 mm range, IS tables give: $es = +59$ µm, $ei = +40$ µm
- For H7: $EI = 0$, $ES = +30$ µm

**Step 2 — Hole H7 limits:**

$$H_{min} = 60.000 \text{ mm}, \quad H_{max} = 60.000 + 0.030 = 60.030 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Shaft s6 limits:**

$$s_{max} = 60.000 + 0.059 = 60.059 \text{ mm}$$

$$s_{min} = 60.000 + 0.040 = 60.040 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Tolerances:**

$$T_{hole} = 0.030 \text{ mm} = 30 \text{ µm} \quad (IT7)$$

$$T_{shaft} = 0.019 \text{ mm} = 19 \text{ µm} \quad (IT6)$$

**Step 5 — Interference Calculations:**

$$I_{max} = s_{max} - H_{min} = 60.059 - 60.000 = 0.059 \text{ mm} = 59 \text{ µm}$$

$$I_{min} = s_{min} - H_{max} = 60.040 - 60.030 = 0.010 \text{ mm} = 10 \text{ µm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The interference ranges from 10 µm to 59 µm, meaning the press-fit force must overcome this range. Such fits require a hydraulic press and heating/cooling of the mating parts.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right IT Grade

| IT Grade | Typical Application (KTU Reference) |
|---|---|
| **IT5** | Gauges, high-precision bearings, instrument shafts |
| **IT6** | Ball/roller bearing inner races, high-quality machine tool spindles |
| **IT7** | General machine assembly fits, gears, pulleys, couplings |
| **IT8** | Less critical rotating parts, medium-quality production |
| **IT9–IT11** | Sheet metal, pressings, cast iron parts, agricultural machinery |
| **IT12–IT16** | Rough castings, forgings, free-cutting tolerances |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Tolerance Formula (derived from ISO 286 / IS 919):**
> For nominal diameter $D$ in mm, the fundamental unit of tolerance is:
> $$i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{D} + 0.001 \cdot D \quad (\text{in } \mu m)$$
> The actual tolerance for grade ITn is: $ITn = k_n \cdot i$, where $k_n$ is a multiplying factor: $k_5 = 7$, $k_6 = 10$, $k_7 = 16$, $k_8 = 25$, $k_9 = 40$, $k_{10} = 64$, $k_{11} = 100$, $k_{12} = 160$, $k_{13} = 250$, etc.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Limits, Fits & Tolerance Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engineering Drawing with Mating Parts] --> B{Which Basis System?}
    B -->|Preferred ISO/IS| C[Hole-Basis H Reference]
    B -->|Special Cases| D[Shaft-Basis h Reference]
    C --> E{What Type of Fit is Needed?}
    D --> E
    E -->|Sliding Freely| F[Clearance Fit - H/g H/h H/f]
    E -->|Precise Locating Removable| G[Transition Fit - H/k H/js H/m]
    E -->|Permanent Bond Press Fit| H[Interference Fit - H/p H/r H/s H/u]
    F --> I[Calculate UL LL from Tables]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Apply Tolerance Grade IT5 to IT16]
    J --> K[Determine Geometric Tolerances GD&T]
    K --> L[Specify Surface Roughness Ra]
    L --> M[Final Drawing with All Callouts]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid — Relationship Between Hole, Shaft, Fits, and Tolerances

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    NodeA[Hole H7 Range] --> NodeB[Basic Size 40 mm]
    NodeC[Shaft f6 Range] --> NodeB
    NodeB --> NodeD{Calculate Gap}
    NodeD -->|Positive| NodeE[Clearance Fit Running Sliding]
    NodeD -->|Zero| NodeF[Line Contact]
    NodeD -->|Negative| NodeG[Interference Press Fit]
    NodeA --> NodeH[Tolerance IT7 25 microns]
    NodeC --> NodeI[Tolerance IT6 16 microns]
    NodeH --> NodeJ[Total Allowance Allowance 25 to 66 microns]
    NodeI --> NodeJ
```

## 4.3 Mermaid — Block-Level Functional Architecture of GD&T

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    GD[Geometric Dimensioning and Tolerancing GD&T] --> FORM[Form Tolerances]
    GD --> ORIENT[Orientation Tolerances]
    GD --> LOC[Location Tolerances]
    GD --> RUN[Runout Tolerances]
    FORM --> F1[Straightness]
    FORM --> F2[Flatness]
    FORM --> F3[Roundness Circularity]
    FORM --> F4[Cylindricity]
    ORIENT --> O1[Perpendicularity]
    ORIENT --> O2[Parallelism]
    ORIENT --> O3[Angularity]
    LOC --> L1[Position]
    LOC --> L2[Concentricity Coaxiality]
    LOC --> L3[Symmetry]
    RUN --> R1[Circular Runout]
    RUN --> R2[Total Runout]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid — Surface Roughness Specification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SR[Surface Roughness] --> SR1[Ra Arithmetic Mean Deviation - micrometres]
    SR --> SR2[Rz Mean Peak to Valley Height]
    SR --> SR3[Symbol Selection Basic Triangle]
    SR3 --> SR4[Add Cutting Allowance Symbol Circle Inside]
    SR3 --> SR5[Add Machining Method Symbol]
    SR1 --> SR6[Specify Numerical Ra Value e.g. 0.8 1.6 3.2]
    SR6 --> SR7[Place Symbol on Drawing Surface Edge]
```

## 4.5 Tabular Reference — Standard Surface Roughness Values (KTU 2024)

| Grade | Ra (µm) | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| N1 | 0.012 | Optical flats, precision gauges |
| N2 | 0.025 | High-precision instrument bearings |
| N3 | 0.05 | Hydraulic cylinder bores |
| N4 | 0.1 | Bearing seats, journal surfaces |
| N5 | 0.2 | Crankshaft journals, piston pins |
| N6 | 0.4 | Spindles, precision slides |
| N7 | 0.8 | Gear teeth working surfaces |
| N8 | 1.6 | General machined surfaces |
| N9 | 3.2 | Production turned surfaces |
| N10 | 6.3 | Rough shafts, unfinished castings |
| N11 | 12.5 | Hot-rolled surfaces |

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the terms (i) Tolerance, (ii) Allowance, and (iii) Maximum Material Condition (MMC) as applied to engineering fits. Give one example of a part where MMC is the lower limit.*

**Model Answer:**
- **(i) Tolerance:** The total permissible variation in the dimension of a part, equal to the difference between the upper and lower limits. $T = UL - LL$.
- **(ii) Allowance:** The *tightest* fit condition — the minimum clearance (or minimum interference) between mating parts, determined by the closest overlap of their tolerance zones.
- **(iii) MMC:** The condition of a feature containing the *maximum amount of material*. For a **hole**, MMC is the **smallest** hole size (lower limit). For a **shaft**, MMC is the **largest** shaft size (upper limit).
- *Example:* For a hole, MMC = Lower Limit (e.g., a Ø30 H8 hole with limits 30.000 to 30.033 has MMC = Ø30.000). For a shaft, MMC = Upper Limit. **(3 marks)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Distinguish clearly between a Clearance Fit, a Transition Fit, and an Interference Fit. State one practical engineering application for each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Fit Type | Definition | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Clearance Fit** | Shaft is always smaller than the hole; always positive gap | Bearing journal-shaft, sliding door roller |
| **Transition Fit** | May give either small clearance or small interference | Removable gear on shaft, dowel pin in reamed hole |
| **Interference Fit** | Shaft is always larger than the hole; always negative gap | Permanent coupling hub, engine valve guide bushing |

**(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**
*(a)* A hole and shaft assembly has a basic diameter of **Ø45 mm**. The fit is specified as **H7/g6** in the hole-basis system. Using IS 919 / ISO 286 tabulated data (Diameter step 30–50 mm: $IT7 = 25$ µm, $IT6 = 16$ µm; for g6: $es = -9$ µm, $ei = -25$ µm; for H7: $EI = 0$):
   (i) Calculate the upper and lower limits of the hole. (2 marks)
   (ii) Calculate the upper and lower limits of the shaft. (2 marks)
   (iii) Find the maximum and minimum clearance, and the allowance. (3 marks)

*(b)* Define Geometric Tolerances. With a neat sketch, explain the difference between **Form Tolerance**, **Orientation Tolerance**, and **Location Tolerance**. Give two examples of symbols for each category. (7 marks)

### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a):**

(i) **Hole H7 limits** (with $EI = 0$, $ES = EI + IT7 = 0 + 25 = +25$ µm):
$$H_{min} = 45.000 + 0.000 = 45.000 \text{ mm}$$
$$H_{max} = 45.000 + 0.025 = 45.025 \text{ mm}$$

(ii) **Shaft g6 limits** (with $es = -9$ µm, $ei = -25$ µm):
$$s_{max} = 45.000 + (-0.009) = 44.991 \text{ mm}$$
$$s_{min} = 45.000 + (-0.025) = 44.975 \text{ mm}$$

(iii) **Clearance calculations:**
$$C_{max} = H_{max} - s_{min} = 45.025 - 44.975 = 0.050 \text{ mm} = 50 \text{ µm}$$
$$C_{min} = H_{min} - s_{max} = 45.000 - 44.991 = 0.009 \text{ mm} = 9 \text{ µm}$$
$$\text{Allowance } A = C_{min} = 9 \text{ µm} = 0.009 \text{ mm}$$

(7 marks for part a)

**Part (b):**
Geometric Tolerances (GD&T) are the maximum permissible variations in *form, profile, orientation, location, and runout* of a feature, independent of its size. They are specified in feature control frames attached to the feature on the engineering drawing, per **ASME Y14.5** and **ISO 1101**.

- **Form Tolerances** — Control the *shape* of a single feature (no datum needed). Examples: **Straightness**, **Flatness**, **Roundness (Circularity)**, **Cylindricity**.
- **Orientation Tolerances** — Control the *angular relationship* of a feature to a datum. Examples: **Perpendicularity**, **Parallelism**, **Angularity**.
- **Location Tolerances** — Control the *position* of a feature relative to a datum. Examples: **Position**, **Concentricity (Coaxiality)**, **Symmetry**.

*Sketch description:* Three rectangles labelled "Form (no datum)", "Orientation (1 datum)", "Location (2-3 datums)" with arrows showing symbol progression from the feature surface to the datum frame. (7 marks)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*(a)* A Ø80 mm shaft is to be press-fitted into a hub. Specify the fit as **H7/s6** in the hole-basis system. Using IS 919 data for the 50–80 mm step ($IT7 = 30$ µm, $IT6 = 19$ µm; s6: $es = +59$ µm, $ei = +40$ µm; H7: $EI = 0$):
   (i) State the limits of the hole and shaft. (2 marks)
   (ii) Compute the maximum and minimum interference. (3 marks)
   (iii) Is this fit suitable for a permanent coupling hub? Justify. (2 marks)

*(b)* Explain the **Surface Roughness** notation as per IS 10360 / ISO 1302. Draw the standard roughness symbol and explain the meaning of (i) basic triangle, (ii) circle inside the triangle, (iii) the numerical Ra value, and (iv) machining method symbol. State four standard Ra grades commonly used in KTU 2024 practice. (7 marks)

### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a):**

(i) **Limits:**
- Hole H7: $H_{min} = 80.000$ mm, $H_{max} = 80.000 + 0.030 = 80.030$ mm.
- Shaft s6: $s_{max} = 80.000 + 0.059 = 80.059$ mm, $s_{min} = 80.000 + 0.040 = 80.040$ mm.

(ii) **Interference:**
$$I_{max} = s_{max} - H_{min} = 80.059 - 80.000 = 0.059 \text{ mm} = 59 \text{ µm}$$
$$I_{min} = s_{min} - H_{max} = 80.040 - 80.030 = 0.010 \text{ mm} = 10 \text{ µm}$$

(iii) **Yes**, this is a *medium-force interference fit* with 10–59 µm of interference. It is ideal for permanent coupling hubs and gear assemblies, typically achieved with a hydraulic press (5–20 tonne capacity). (7 marks for part a)

**Part (b):**
The Surface Roughness symbol is an **equilateral triangle** whose apex touches the surface line. Variants:
- **(i) Basic triangle** (√) — Surface to be machined by *any* method.
- **(ii) Circle inside the triangle** — Surface must be *machined by turning* (lathe).
- **(iii) Numerical Ra value** — Placed *above* the triangle (e.g., "1.6") indicating the *Arithmetic Mean Deviation* in micrometres.
- **(iv) Machining method symbol** — Placed in an *extended arm* (rectangle attached to the triangle base), e.g., "TURN", "GRIND", "MILL".

*Four standard Ra grades used in KTU practice:*
- **Ra 0.8 µm** — Precision bearing seats
- **Ra 1.6 µm** — General machined mating surfaces
- **Ra 3.2 µm** — Standard turned surfaces
- **Ra 6.3 µm** — Rough machined/unfinished surfaces

(7 marks for part b)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error on deviation:** Students often forget that for *holes* the deviations are `ES` (upper) and `EI` (lower), and for *shafts* the deviations are `es` and `ei`. A sign mistake of just 0.025 mm can lose **3 full marks**.
> 2. **Confusing Allowance with Tolerance:** Allowance is the *tightest clearance or interference*, NOT the tolerance. Tolerance is the *bandwidth*.
> 3. **Mixing Hole-Basis and Shaft-Basis:** If the question says "H7/g6", it is *hole-basis* — never try to compute the hole tolerance from the shaft letter.
> 4. **Forgetting Units:** Always write `µm` when using ISO tables, then convert to `mm` for limits. KTU examiners specifically look for unit consistency.
> 5. **Surface Roughness Symbol Direction:** The symbol's *apex* must touch the surface, NOT the base. Marks are deducted for inverted symbols.
> 6. **GD&T Datum Reference:** Form tolerances need *no datum*; orientation tolerances need *one*; location tolerances need *two or three*. Drawing a GD&T symbol without the correct datum frame is a common 1-mark deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Tolerance** = $UL - LL$, always **positive**, expressed in µm or mm.
- **Deviation** = Limit − Basic Size; can be positive, zero, or negative.
- **Hole-Basis (H)** is the **international default** — use unless stated otherwise.
- **Hole symbol `H` always has $EI = 0$** (lower deviation is zero). This is the cornerstone of IS 919.
- **Shaft symbol `h` always has $es = 0$** for shaft-basis system.
- **Clearance Fit:** Shaft always smaller → $H_{min} \ge s_{max}$.
- **Interference Fit:** Shaft always larger → $s_{min} \ge H_{max}$.
- **Transition Fit:** $H_{min} \le s_{max}$ AND $s_{min} \le H_{max}$ (overlap of tolerance zones).
- **Allowance** = the **minimum** clearance or **minimum** interference (the tightest condition).
- **IT Grade numbers** increase with looser tolerance: IT5 (precision) → IT16 (rough).
- **Fundamental tolerance unit** $i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{D} + 0.001 \cdot D$ µm.
- **GD&T categories:** Form, Orientation, Location, Runout — each with specific symbol sets.
- **Surface Roughness Ra** values follow the *preferred number series*: 0.012, 0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.8, 1.6, 3.2, 6.3, 12.5, 25 µm.
- **Roughness symbol** = an **equilateral triangle with apex touching the surface line**, with the Ra value written **above** the symbol.
- **MMC vs LMC:** MMC = most material (hole → lower limit, shaft → upper limit); LMC = least material (hole → upper limit, shaft → lower limit).
- **KTU 2024 marker-tip:** When in doubt about a fit specification, write the **complete expression** (e.g., `Ø30 H7/g6`) — this single string carries the basic size, basis system, tolerance grades, and fit type in *one* go.
- **Conversion:** $1$ mm $= 1000$ µm; $1$ µm $= 0.001$ mm. Always check units in your final answer.
- **Remember the mnemonic:** "Holes are CAPITAL, Shafts are small (lower-case)" — H, G, F, K, P are holes; h, g, f, k, p are shafts.
- **Three fit families** map to three uses: Clearance → sliding, Transition → locating, Interference → permanent.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Production Drawings — Formal KTU Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Production Drawing (Working Drawing):** A complete, dimensioned, and toleranced engineering drawing that conveys all information required to manufacture, inspect, and assemble a single component or a complete product. It is the final output delivered from the design office to the shop floor, and is governed by **BIS SP 46:2003** (aligned with ISO 128 & ISO 129) for the Indian context.

A **Production Drawing set** typically includes:

| Sheet Type | Purpose | KTU Exam Weight |
|---|---|---|
| **Part Drawing (Component Drawing)** | Details of one individual piece | Very High |
| **Assembly Drawing** | Shows how parts fit together | Very High |
| **Detailed/Production Drawing** | All manufacturing info on a single component | High |
| **Sub-Assembly Drawing** | Intermediate stage of assembly | Moderate |
| **Installation Drawing** | Field installation details | Low |

## 1.2 Part Drawing vs Assembly Drawing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Part Drawing (Component Drawing):** Shows **ONE part only** with all views, hidden details, center lines, dimensions, tolerances, surface finish symbols, geometric tolerances, material specification, and a **Bill of Material (BOM)** in the title block. Used by the **machi

topics.title: Couplings: Oldham's Coupling, Flexible Couplings, Universal Joints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRL307 - MACHINE DRAWING AND SOLID MODELLING
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Couplings: Oldham's Coupling, Flexible Couplings, Universal Joints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Coupling?

A **Coupling** is a mechanical device used to connect two shafts together at their ends for the purpose of transmitting power (torque) from one shaft to another, without any slippage. Couplings can also be used to introduce or absorb shock loads, accommodate misalignment, and protect connected machinery from overload conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive:** Under PCBRL307 (Machine Drawing and Solid Modelling), couplings form a core part of **Module Experiment 2**. Students are expected to draw orthographic projections, sectional views, and assemble the parts of various coupling types using standard proportions.

### 1.2 Broad Classification of Couplings

Couplings are broadly divided into two main families based on their operational behaviour:

1. **Rigid Couplings** — Used when shafts are perfectly aligned (e.g., Sleeve/Muff Coupling, Clamp or Split Muff Coupling, Flange/Plate Coupling, Marine Coupling).
2. **Flexible Couplings** — Used when shafts have slight misalignments (parallel, angular, or axial) due to manufacturing errors, deflection under load, or thermal expansion.

## 1.3 The Three Couplings in Focus

### 1.3.1 Oldham's Coupling

Oldham's Coupling is a special form of flexible coupling designed specifically to connect **two parallel shafts** that are slightly **laterally misaligned** (offset in the transverse direction but not angularly misaligned).

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** *Oldham's Coupling is a third-class lever type flexible coupling consisting of two flanged hubs with diametrically opposite slots and a central floating disc (middle member) with two perpendicular tongues or tenons sliding in those slots, used to transmit motion between two parallel but laterally displaced shafts.*

**Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine two trains running on **parallel tracks** that are slightly offset by a small distance. They cannot directly connect because of the lateral gap. Oldham's coupling acts like a floating "floating tray" — it accepts motion from one train (flange 1) and delivers it to the other train (flange 2) by sliding sideways on the tray. The tray (floating disc) simply translates back and forth in the middle, absorbing the lateral offset.

### 1.3.2 Flexible Couplings (Bushed-Pin Type)

A flexible coupling provides flexibility between the connected shafts using a resilient material like **rubber, leather, or canvas** inserted between metallic flanges.

The most common design used in KTU machine drawing labs is the **Bushed-Pin Flexible Coupling** (also called the Pin-and-Bush Coupling). It consists of two flanges, each with a set of pins. The pins of one flange are surrounded by rubber bushes that fit into the holes of the opposite flange.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Think of it as a **suspension bridge joint**. Two rigid road sections are joined by a stretchable rubber pad. When the wind pushes one side slightly, the rubber absorbs the twist without breaking the bridge. The rubber bushes in a flexible coupling perform exactly this role — they twist, compress, and shear to absorb shock and slight misalignment.

### 1.3.3 Universal Joint (Hooke's Joint / Cardan Joint)

A Universal Joint is a coupling used to transmit rotary motion between two shafts whose axes **intersect at an angle** (non-parallel and non-coaxial).

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** *A Universal Joint (Hooke's Joint) is a positive, semi-flexible coupling consisting of two yokes, one on each shaft, connected through a central cross-shaped intermediate member (spider), transmitting torque between shafts having angular misalignment.*

**Intuitive Analogy:** Picture the **steering column of a car**. The steering wheel shaft and the front-wheel shaft are at an angle, yet the steering motion is transmitted perfectly between them. The cross-shaped "spider" in the universal joint is exactly the small joint you see under the steering column, allowing your hand to turn the wheel which then turns the road wheels at an angle.

## 1.4 Why These Three Couplings Are Studied Together

| Coupling Type | Misalignment Handled | Key Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Oldham's Coupling** | Parallel (lateral) misalignment only | Machine tool drives, printing presses |
| **Flexible (Bushed-Pin)** | Slight parallel, angular, axial misalignment | Pumps, compressors, electric motors |
| **Universal Joint** | Angular misalignment (intersecting shafts) | Automobiles, propeller shafts, machine tools |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Memory Aid:** **O.P.U.** — **O**ldham = **P**arallel offset, **U**niversal = angular Union. Flexible = all-rounder.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Oldham's Coupling — Operational Theory

### 2.1.1 Construction (in detail)

Oldham's Coupling consists of **three main parts**:

1. **Flange A** (Driver Flange) — A disc with a hub, mounted on the driving shaft. It has a **slot** cut diametrically across its face.
2. **Flange B** (Driven Flange) — Identical to Flange A, mounted on the driven shaft. Its slot is also diametrical but is **perpendicular in orientation to the assembly** relative to Flange A.
3. **Floating Disc (Middle Member)** — A central disc of thickness slightly greater than the combined flange thicknesses. It carries **two perpendicular tenons (tongues)** projecting from each face.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The tongues on one face of the floating disc engage the slot of Flange A. The tongues on the opposite face engage the slot of Flange B. The disc therefore can **slide radially** within the slots.

### 2.1.2 Working Principle (Why and How)

- The driving shaft rotates with Flange A. The slot in Flange A drags the tongue on one side of the floating disc, making the disc rotate.
- Because the disc's other tongue is engaged with the perpendicular slot in Flange B, the rotation of the disc forces Flange B to rotate as well — at exactly the same speed (1:1 ratio, no slip).
- The floating disc simultaneously **translates** (oscillates) sideways in a small circular path to accommodate the lateral offset between the two shaft axes.

### 2.1.3 Velocity Analysis

The angular velocity of the driven shaft always equals the angular velocity of the driving shaft, but there is an **instantaneous sliding velocity** at the disc tongues. The maximum sliding velocity $V_s$ at the centre of the disc is given by:

$$V_s \;=\; \omega \cdot r_o$$

where $\omega$ is the angular velocity in rad/s and $r_o$ is the offset between the shaft centres.

## 2.2 Bushed-Pin Flexible Coupling — Operational Theory

### 2.2.1 Construction

- **Two Cast Iron Flanges** — Each flange is keyed to its respective shaft. One flange (driven side) has **projecting forged-steel pins** with **rubber bushes** pressed onto them.
- The other flange has **equi-spaced holes** of slightly larger diameter than the bush outer diameter. The rubber bushes of one flange fit into the holes of the other flange.
- A **flange-retaining nut or cotter pin** may secure each pin at the back.

### 2.2.2 Working Principle (Why and How)

- Torque is transmitted from the driver flange through its **steel pins** to the **rubber bushes** (in shear and compression) to the driven flange.
- The rubber bushes are compressible and resilient, so the coupling can absorb:
  - **Parallel misalignment** (radial offset)
  - **Angular misalignment** (slight tilt)
  - **Axial misalignment** (end play)
  - **Shock loads and torsional vibrations**

> [!NOTE]
> Rubber cushions the impact, protects the shaft from sudden load fluctuations, and reduces noise — making this coupling ideal for electric motor-pump sets.

## 2.3 Universal Joint (Hooke's Joint) — Operational Theory

### 2.3.1 Construction

- **Two Yokes** — Each is keyed or splined to its shaft. A yoke has **two prongs (forks)** with aligned holes.
- **One Spider (Cross)** — A central cross-shaped member with **four arms** machined precisely perpendicular. The arms pass through the prong holes of the two yokes and are secured by **needle bearings or taper pins**.
- The two yokes lie in **mutually perpendicular planes** when the shafts are inclined at an angle.

### 2.3.2 Working Principle (Why and How)

- When the driving yoke rotates, the spider arms (needles) push the prongs of the driven yoke, causing it to rotate.
- The spider is constrained to rotate in 3D, with its arm tips always lying on the axes of the two shaft bearings.
- This makes the output shaft rotate at the **same mean speed** as the input, but with a **sinusoidal fluctuation** in instantaneous angular velocity when the shaft angle $\theta$ is non-zero.

### 2.3.3 The Critical Velocity Equation (KTU High-Yield)

The angular velocity ratio between driving and driven shafts in a single universal joint is given by:

$$\frac{\omega_2}{\omega_1} \;=\; \frac{\cos \theta}{1 - \sin^2\theta \cdot \cos^2 \alpha}$$

where:
- $\omega_1$ = angular velocity of driving shaft
- $\omega_2$ = angular velocity of driven shaft
- $\theta$ = angle of rotation of driving shaft
- $\alpha$ = angle between the two shafts

> [!WARNING]
> When the two shafts are at right angles ($\alpha = 90°$), the driven shaft **stops momentarily** at certain positions. This is a KTU favourite conceptual trap.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> All dimensions below are derived from standard machine design handbooks (PSG, Khurmi, and Shigley) and are used universally in KTU board examination drawing questions.

### 2.4.1 Oldham's Coupling Standard Proportions

| Parameter | Standard Proportion | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Outer diameter of flange D | $D = 2d$ to $2.5d$ | $d$ = shaft diameter |
| Hub diameter $D_h$ | $D_h = 1.5d + 25$ mm | Internal bore fits shaft |
| Hub length $L_h$ | $L_h = 1.5d$ to $2d$ | Provides key seating |
| Flange thickness $t$ | $t = 0.5d$ to $0.75d$ | For slotting and rigidity |
| Slot width $w$ | $w = d/3$ to $d/2$ | Tongue engagement width |
| Slot depth $L_s$ | $L_s = D/3$ to $D/2$ | Radial extent into flange |
| Floating disc thickness $T_d$ | $T_d = 2t + 4$ mm | Clearance accommodated |
| Tongue thickness | Equal to slot width $w$ | Slip fit condition |

### 2.4.2 Bushed-Pin Flexible Coupling Standard Proportions

| Parameter | Standard Proportion | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Flange pitch circle diameter PCD | $PCD = 3d$ to $4d$ | Bolt/pin circle |
| Number of pins $n$ | $n = 4$ to $8$ | Typically 6 for $d \le 50$ mm |
| Pin diameter $d_p$ | $d_p = d/3$ to $d/2$ | Forged steel |
| Pin length $L_p$ | $L_p = 2t + 15$ mm | Through both flanges + bush |
| Bush outer diameter $D_b$ | $D_b = 1.5 d_p$ to $2 d_p$ | Rubber grade |
| Bush length $L_b$ | $L_b = 1.5 d_p$ to $2 d_p$ | Compressibility range |
| Flange thickness $t$ | $t = 0.5d$ to $d$ | Cast iron |

### 2.4.3 Universal Joint (Hooke's Joint) Standard Proportions

| Parameter | Standard Proportion | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Yoke prong outside diameter D | $D = 2d$ to $2.5d$ | $d$ = shaft diameter |
| Spider arm diameter $d_s$ | $d_s = 0.5d$ to $0.6d$ | Forged steel cross |
| Prong hole diameter | Equal to $d_s$ (with needle bearing) | Slip/needle fit |
| Yoke hub length $L_h$ | $L_h = 1.5d$ | Keyway seating |
| Bearing pin length $L_b$ | $L_b = 2 \times \text{prong thickness} + 4$ mm | Retained by circlip or nut |
| Maximum operating angle $\alpha$ | $\le 45°$ (typically 30°) | For uniform wear |

### 2.4.4 Universal Joint Kinematics

| Quantity | Equation | Condition |
|---|---|---|
| Mean velocity ratio | $\overline{\omega_2/\omega_1} = 1$ | Time-averaged over one cycle |
| Maximum velocity ratio | $\omega_2/\omega_1 = \cos\alpha / (1 - \sin^2\alpha \cdot \cos^2\theta)$ | Peaks at $\theta = 0°, 180°$ |
| Minimum velocity ratio | $\omega_2/\omega_1 = \cos\alpha / (1 - \sin^2\alpha \cdot \sin^2\theta)$ | Troughs at $\theta = 90°, 270°$ |
| Torque transmitted | $T = (n \cdot \pi \cdot d_p^2 \cdot \tau_p \cdot PCD) / 4$ | $n$ = number of pins, $\tau_p$ = allowable shear |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Real-World Use:** Universal joints in pairs (with a *mid-shaft*) are used in rear-wheel-drive vehicles and machine tool spindles — this is called a **Double Hooke's Joint** and it **eliminates velocity fluctuation**, producing a constant 1:1 ratio even with angular misalignment. This is a common KTU theory question.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Proportion Deductions & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Oldham's Coupling Geometry (Drawing Layout)

### 3.1.1 Setup of the Problem

A **shaft of diameter $d = 40$ mm** is to be connected to a parallel shaft displaced by a lateral offset of $r_o = 5$ mm. Design all dimensions of an Oldham's Coupling according to standard proportions.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Deduction

**Step 1 — Determine the outer flange diameter $D$.**

Using the standard proportion:

$$D = 2.5 \cdot d$$

Substituting $d = 40$ mm:

$$D = 2.5 \times 40 \;=\; 100 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Determine the hub diameter $D_h$.**

$$D_h = 1.5d + 25 \text{ mm}$$

$$D_h = 1.5 \times 40 + 25 \;=\; 60 + 25 \;=\; 85 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Determine the hub length $L_h$.**

$$L_h = 1.75d$$

$$L_h = 1.75 \times 40 \;=\; 70 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Determine the flange thickness $t$.**

$$t = 0.6d$$

$$t = 0.6 \times 40 \;=\; 24 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Determine the slot width $w$ and slot depth $L_s$.**

$$w = d / 3$$

$$w = 40 / 3 \;\approx\; 13.33 \text{ mm} \;\;\text{(round to 13 mm)}$$

$$L_s = D / 3$$

$$L_s = 100 / 3 \;\approx\; 33.33 \text{ mm} \;\;\text{(round to 33 mm)}$$

**Step 6 — Determine the floating disc thickness $T_d$.**

$$T_d = 2t + 4 \text{ mm}$$

$$T_d = 2(24) + 4 \;=\; 48 + 4 \;=\; 52 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 7 — Determine the floating disc diameter $D_d$.**

The disc must extend beyond the flange slots by a small margin to allow tongue engagement:

$$D_d = D - 2 \times 5 \text{ mm} \;=\; 100 - 10 \;=\; 90 \text{ mm}$$

### 3.1.3 Final Tabulated Drawing Dimensions for Oldham's Coupling

| Part | Dimension | Computed Value (mm) |
|---|---|---|
| Shaft diameter | $d$ | 40 |
| Flange outer diameter | $D$ | 100 |
| Hub diameter | $D_h$ | 85 |
| Hub length | $L_h$ | 70 |
| Flange thickness | $t$ | 24 |
| Slot width | $w$ | 13 |
| Slot depth | $L_s$ | 33 |
| Disc thickness | $T_d$ | 52 |
| Disc diameter | $D_d$ | 90 |
| Lateral offset | $r_o$ | 5 |

## 3.2 Derivation: Bushed-Pin Flexible Coupling Geometry

### 3.2.1 Setup

For $d = 50$ mm, design the bushed-pin flexible coupling.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Deduction

**Step 1 — Number of pins $n$.**

For $d \le 50$ mm, choose $n = 6$ pins.

**Step 2 — Pitch circle diameter PCD.**

$$PCD = 3.5d$$

$$PCD = 3.5 \times 50 \;=\; 175 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Pin diameter $d_p$.**

$$d_p = d / 3$$

$$d_p = 50 / 3 \;\approx\; 16.67 \text{ mm} \;\;\text{(round to 17 mm)}$$

**Step 4 — Pin length $L_p$.**

Assuming flange thickness $t = 0.6d = 30$ mm:

$$L_p = 2t + 15$$

$$L_p = 2(30) + 15 \;=\; 60 + 15 \;=\; 75 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Bush outer diameter $D_b$.**

$$D_b = 1.6 \cdot d_p$$

$$D_b = 1.6 \times 17 \;\approx\; 27 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — Bush length $L_b$.**

$$L_b = 1.5 d_p$$

$$L_b = 1.5 \times 17 \;\approx\; 25.5 \text{ mm} \;\;\text{(round to 26 mm)}$$

**Step 7 — Flange thickness $t$.**

$$t = 0.6d = 30 \text{ mm}$$

### 3.2.3 Summary of Dimensions

| Parameter | Value (mm) |
|---|---|
| Shaft diameter $d$ | 50 |
| PCD | 175 |
| Number of pins $n$ | 6 |
| Pin diameter $d_p$ | 17 |
| Pin length $L_p$ | 75 |
| Flange thickness $t$ | 30 |
| Bush outer diameter $D_b$ | 27 |
| Bush length $L_b$ | 26 |

## 3.3 Derivation: Universal Joint Maximum Velocity Ratio

### 3.3.1 Setup

A Hooke's joint connects two shafts at an angle of $\alpha = 30°$. The driving shaft rotates at $\omega_1 = 1500$ rpm. Find the **maximum** and **minimum** angular velocities of the driven shaft.

### 3.3.2 Maximum Velocity Condition

The maximum driven-shaft velocity occurs when $\cos^2\theta = 1$ (i.e., $\theta = 0°$ or $180°$):

$$\left(\frac{\omega_2}{\omega_1}\right)_{max} = \frac{\cos\alpha}{1 - \sin^2\alpha \cdot 1}$$

$$\left(\frac{\omega_2}{\omega_1}\right)_{max} = \frac{\cos\alpha}{1 - \sin^2\alpha} = \frac{\cos\alpha}{\cos^2\alpha} = \frac{1}{\cos\alpha} = \sec\alpha$$

Substituting $\alpha = 30°$:

$$\left(\frac{\omega_2}{\omega_1}\right)_{max} = \sec 30° = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 1.1547$$

Therefore:

$$\omega_{2,\,max} = 1.1547 \times 1500 \;=\; 1732.05 \text{ rpm}$$

### 3.3.3 Minimum Velocity Condition

The minimum driven-shaft velocity occurs when $\cos^2\theta = 0$ (i.e., $\theta = 90°$ or $270°$):

$$\left(\frac{\omega_2}{\omega_1}\right)_{min} = \frac{\cos\alpha}{1 - \sin^2\alpha \cdot 0} = \cos\alpha$$

Substituting $\alpha = 30°$:

$$\left(\frac{\omega_2}{\omega_1}\right)_{min} = \cos 30° = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.8660$$

Therefore:

$$\omega_{2,\,min} = 0.8660 \times 1500 \;=\; 1299.04 \text{ rpm}$$

### 3.3.4 Mean and Torque Analysis

Mean angular velocity:

$$\overline{\omega_2} = \frac{\omega_{2,\,max} + \omega_{2,\,min}}{2} = \frac{1732.05 + 1299.04}{2} = 1515.5 \text{ rpm} \approx \omega_1$$

This confirms that the **time-averaged velocity is preserved** at 1:1 ratio, validating the theoretical identity $\overline{\omega_2} = \omega_1$.

Torque transmitted by a single pin in shear:

$$T = n \cdot \frac{\pi \cdot d_p^2}{4} \cdot \tau_p \cdot \frac{PCD}{2}$$

For $n = 4$, $d_p = 20$ mm, $\tau_p = 40$ MPa, $PCD = 200$ mm:

$$T = 4 \times \frac{\pi (0.020)^2}{4} \times 40 \times 10^6 \times \frac{0.200}{2}$$

$$T = 4 \times 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \times 40 \times 10^6 \times 0.1$$

$$T \approx 5.03 \times 10^3 \text{ N·m} \;=\; 5.03 \text{ kN·m}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Coupling Selection Decision Tree

| Shaft Condition | Recommended Coupling | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Perfectly aligned, in-line, heavy torque | Flange (Plate) Coupling | Rigid, no flexibility needed |
| Perfectly aligned, light load, easy assembly | Sleeve / Clamp Muff | Low cost, simple construction |
| Parallel lateral offset (small, fixed) | **Oldham's Coupling** | Floating disc absorbs offset |
| Slight parallel + angular + axial misalignment | **Bushed-Pin Flexible Coupling** | Rubber bushes absorb all three |
| Slight angular misalignment, small loads | Universal Joint (single) | Cheap, compact, transmits angle |
| Constant-velocity angular misalignment | **Double Universal Joint** | Two Hooke's joints cancel fluctuation |
| Heavy shock load, frequent start-stop | Bushed-Pin with rubber spider | Cushioning effect |
