# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**List any three differences between Oldham's Coupling and a Bushed-Pin Flexible Coupling.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Oldham's Coupling | Bushed-Pin Flexible Coupling |
|---|---|---|
| **Misalignment type** | Handles only **parallel (lateral) offset** | Handles parallel, angular, and axial misalignments |
| **Flexibility medium** | Mechanical sliding of a **floating disc** in slots | **Rubber bushes** providing resilient cushioning |
| **Shock absorption** | **Poor** — rigid metallic contact | **Excellent** — rubber absorbs shock and vibration |
| **Velocity ratio** | Exactly **1:1** (no slip) | Approximately 1:1 with minor rubber deformation |
| **Typical use** | Machine tool drives, printing presses | Motor-pump sets, compressors |

> [!NOTE]
> Any 3 correctly tabulated differences fetch **3 marks**; partial credit is awarded for any single valid difference.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Why are two universal joints used in tandem (Double Hooke's Joint) in automobile propeller shafts?**

**Model Answer:**

When a **single universal joint** operates with an angular misalignment $\alpha \neq 0°$, the driven shaft's angular velocity **fluctuates sinusoidally** with rotation. The mean velocity equals the input velocity, but instantaneous velocity varies between $\omega_1 \cos\alpha$ and $\omega_1 / \cos\alpha$.

When **two universal joints** are connected back-to-back on a common intermediate shaft, with the yokes of the intermediate shaft placed **in phase (parallel planes)**, the velocity fluctuation introduced by the first joint is **exactly cancelled** by the second joint. The result is a **constant 1:1 velocity ratio** between the input and output shafts regardless of the angle.

This is essential in rear-wheel-drive cars where the propeller shaft transmits power from the gearbox (front of car) to the differential (rear axle) with a continuously varying angle due to road undulations. **3 marks** for a clear, complete answer.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw the **orthographic views** (front view as half-sectional and top view) of an **Oldham's Coupling** connecting two parallel shafts of diameter **$d = 45$ mm**, with a lateral offset of **$6$ mm**. Use standard proportions. Show all dimensions as per BIS conventions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat sketch the **construction and working** of a **bushed-pin flexible coupling**. State the standard proportions used.

---

#### Model Solution to A(a)

**Step 1 — Apply Standard Proportions for $d = 45$ mm:**

$$D = 2.5d = 2.5 \times 45 = 112.5 \text{ mm} \;\;\text{(round to 112 mm)}$$

$$D_h = 1.5d + 25 = 1.5(45) + 25 = 92.5 \text{ mm} \;\;\text{(round to 93 mm)}$$

$$L_h = 1.75d = 1.75 \times 45 = 78.75 \text{ mm} \;\;\text{(round to 79 mm)}$$

$$t = 0.6d = 0.6 \times 45 = 27 \text{ mm}$$

$$w = d/3 = 15 \text{ mm}$$

$$L_s = D/3 = 37 \text{ mm}$$

$$T_d = 2t + 4 = 58 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Drawing Layout (procedure):**

1. Draw two **horizontal centre lines** representing the two parallel shaft axes, separated by **$6$ mm** vertically.
2. On each centre line, draw the **hub** (rectangle of length $L_h = 79$ mm, height $D_h = 93$ mm).
3. Beyond each hub, draw the **flange disc** (rectangle of length $T_d = 58$ mm in section, height $D = 112$ mm) overlapping.
4. In the front view (half-section), show the **floating disc** between the two flanges. Show the **tongue and slot** engagement clearly with section lines.
5. In the top view, show the **lateral offset** of $6$ mm between the two shaft centres; draw the disc in plan with two **perpendicular tongues** visible.
6. Show **keyways** at each hub-shaft junction.
7. Dimension all major features. Add the **title block** at bottom right.

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Distribution (7 marks):** Proportion table — 2 marks, Front view drawing — 2 marks, Top view drawing — 2 marks, Dimensioning and title block — 1 mark.

#### Model Solution to A(b)

**Construction:**

A bushed-pin flexible coupling consists of:
- **Two cast iron flanges** keyed to their respective shafts. One flange (driver) has **$n = 6$** projecting **forged-steel pins** (for $d = 45$ mm, pin diameter $d_p = d/3 = 15$ mm).
- **Rubber bushes** are press-fitted onto these pins.
- The other flange (driven) has **6 holes** of diameter slightly greater than bush outer diameter $D_b = 1.6 d_p = 24$ mm, through which the bushes are inserted.
- Each pin is retained at the back by a **nut or taper pin** to prevent axial slippage.

**Working:**

When torque is applied, the driver flange rotates, dragging its pins (with rubber bushes) inside the holes of the driven flange. The torque is transmitted through the **shear of the rubber bushes** and **friction at the contact surfaces**. The rubber cushions absorb:
- **Shock loads** at start-up or sudden braking
- **Torsional vibrations** of the motor
- Slight **parallel, angular, and axial** misalignments between the shafts

**Standard Proportions:**

$$PCD = 3.5d, \quad d_p = d/3, \quad L_p = 2t + 15, \quad D_b = 1.6 d_p, \quad L_b = 1.5 d_p$$

> [!NOTE]
> 7 marks distribution: Construction details — 2 marks, Working explanation — 2 marks, Sketch (neat) — 2 marks, Proportions — 1 mark.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a **neat sketch**, describe the **construction and working of a Universal Joint (Hooke's Joint)**. Mention the standard proportions of its main parts.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Two shafts are connected by a **single universal joint** at an angle of $\alpha = 20°$. The driving shaft rotates uniformly at **1200 rpm**. Determine the **maximum and minimum** angular velocities of the driven shaft and sketch the **fluctuation curve** qualitatively.

---

#### Model Solution to B(a)

**Construction (with sketch elements):**

A universal joint has the following parts:
1. **Two yokes (forks)** — one keyed to each shaft. Each yoke has a hub and two prongs.
2. **One spider (cross)** — a forged-steel cross with four arms, perpendicular in pairs.
3. **Four needle bearings (or plain pins)** — fitted at the four arm-to-yoke prong junctions.
4. The yokes are mounted with their planes of prongs **mutually perpendicular**.

**Working:**

- The driving shaft's yoke rotates with it. The spider arms, carried in the prong holes of the driving yoke, cause the spider to rotate.
- The other two arms of the spider (in the perpendicular plane) carry the prongs of the driven yoke, causing it to rotate.
- The two yokes lie in perpendicular planes, so angular misalignment is accommodated.
- Mean speed is preserved (1:1) but **instantaneous velocity fluctuates** sinusoidally.

**Standard Proportions:**

$$D = 2d, \quad d_s = 0.55d, \quad L_h = 1.5d, \quad L_b = 2 \times \text{prong thickness} + 4 \text{ mm}$$

> 7 marks distribution: Sketch — 3 marks, Construction listing — 2 marks, Working — 1 mark, Proportions — 1 mark.

#### Model Solution to B(b)

**Step 1 — Apply the velocity ratio formula:**

$$\left(\frac{\omega_2}{\omega_1}\right)_{max} = \sec\alpha = \sec 20°$$

Computing:

$$\sec 20° = \frac{1}{\cos 20°} = \frac{1}{0.9397} = 1.0642$$

$$\omega_{2,\,max} = 1.0642 \times 1200 = 1277.0 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 2 — Minimum velocity:**

$$\left(\frac{\omega_2}{\omega_1}\right)_{min} = \cos\alpha = \cos 20° = 0.9397$$

$$\omega_{2,\,min} = 0.9397 \times 1200 = 1127.6 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 3 — Mean velocity:**

$$\overline{\omega_2} = \frac{1277.0 + 1127.6}{2} = 1202.3 \text{ rpm} \approx \omega_1 = 1200 \text{ rpm}$$

(Discrepancy is due to rounding.)

**Step 4 — Fluctuation:**

$$\text{Velocity fluctuation} = \omega_{2,\,max} - \omega_{2,\,min} = 1277.0 - 1127.6 = 149.4 \text{ rpm}$$

$$\text{Coefficient of fluctuation} = \frac{\omega_{2,\,max} - \omega_{2,\,min}}{\omega_1} = \frac{149.4}{1200} = 0.1245 = 12.45\%$$

**Step 5 — Qualitative sketch:** The driven shaft's velocity traces a **sinusoidal curve** as a function of driving-shaft rotation angle $\theta$. Peaks of $1277$ rpm occur at $\theta = 0°, 180°$ and troughs of $1127.6$ rpm at $\theta = 90°, 270°$.

> 7 marks distribution: Formula application — 3 marks, Numerical computation — 2 marks, Fluctuation analysis — 1 mark, Sketch description — 1 mark.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $\sec\alpha$ and $\cos\alpha$:** Many students interchange the max/min formulas. Remember: **MAX uses $\sec\alpha$ (greater than 1), MIN uses $\cos\alpha$ (less than 1)**.
> 2. **Not stating units in the final answer:** Always write "rpm" or "rad/s" with numerical values.
> 3. **Forgetting to label axes on the velocity fluctuation curve:** Always mark "$\omega_2$ (rpm)" on Y-axis and "$\theta$ (degrees)" on X-axis.
> 4. **In Oldham's coupling drawings:** Students often forget to show the **perpendicularity** between the two slot orientations. The slots must be drawn at **90° to each other** in the floating disc.
> 5. **In Bushed-pin coupling drawings:** Students frequently draw the **bushes and pins with the same diameter**; remember that the bush outer diameter must be **larger** than the pin diameter.
> 6. **In Universal joint theory:** Students forget to mention that the spider is a **single rigid cross** — it does not rotate about its own axis; it is constrained by the four bearings in 3D.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Points — Read this 3 times before the exam.**

### Oldham's Coupling — 5-Point Memory Grid
1. Used **only for parallel lateral misalignment** (no angular).
2. Has **3 parts**: Two flanged hubs + 1 floating disc.
3. Floating disc has **2 perpendicular tongues** engaging **2 perpendicular slots**.
4. Velocity ratio is **exactly 1:1**.
5. Disc translates (oscillates) in a small circular path during rotation; **sliding wear** is the main failure mode — disc is made of hardened steel or phosphor bronze.

### Bushed-Pin Flexible Coupling — 5-Point Memory Grid
1. Used for **multi-axis slight misalignment** + shock absorption.
2. Has **2 flanges, $n$ pins, $n$ rubber bushes**.
3. Power is transmitted via **shear in rubber bushes**.
4. Standard pins: $d_p = d/3$, $PCD = 3.5d$, $n = 6$ (typical).
5. **Limitation:** Generates heat in rubber; not suitable for very high speeds or temperatures above **80°C**.

### Universal Joint (Hooke's Joint) — 7-Point Memory Grid
1. Used for **angular misalignment** between intersecting shafts.
2. Has **2 yokes + 1 spider (cross) + 4 needle bearings**.
3. Yoke prongs are in **mutually perpendicular planes**.
4. **Max velocity ratio = $\sec\alpha$; Min velocity ratio = $\cos\alpha$.**
5. **Mean velocity = $\omega_1$** (preserved over one full cycle).
6. **Single Hooke's joint = fluctuating velocity; Double Hooke's joint = constant velocity.**
7. Maximum recommended angle: $\alpha = 30°$ to $45°$ for smooth operation.

### Cross-Cutting Concepts
- **Drawing conventions** for KTU: All sectional views use **$45°$ parallel hatch lines** spaced at **3 mm apart** for cast iron and **$60°$** for steel.
- **Centre lines** must extend **2 mm beyond** the contour.
- **Hidden lines** are short dashes; **visible lines** are thick continuous.
- The **BIS SP-46** code governs dimensioning style: arrowheads, extension lines, and placement.

### Quick-Recall Proportion Formulas (Save These!)
- Oldham: $D = 2.5d, \; t = 0.6d, \; T_d = 2t + 4$
- Bushed-Pin: $PCD = 3.5d, \; d_p = d/3, \; n = 6$
- Hooke's: $D = 2d, \; d_s = 0.55d, \; L_h = 1.5d$

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Strategy:** If a 14-mark question offers a choice between Oldham/Bushed-Pin and Universal Joint theory, **pick the Universal Joint** if you remember the $\sec\alpha$–$\cos\alpha$ formula — it is faster to write and consistently scores full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to CAD: Input and Output Devices, Drafting Software (AutoCAD), Basic Commands, Development of Simple 2D and 3D Drawings, Different File Formats for 3D Modelling (IGES, STL)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRL307 - MACHINE DRAWING AND SOLID MODELLING
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to CAD: Input and Output Devices, Drafting Software (AutoCAD), Basic Commands, Development of Simple 2D and 3D Drawings, Different File Formats for 3D Modelling (IGES, STL)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is CAD? (Formal Definition)

**Computer-Aided Design (CAD)** is the use of computer systems to assist in the creation, modification, analysis, and optimization of a design. It involves both software and sometimes special-purpose hardware. In the context of KTU's PCBRL307 syllabus, CAD is treated as the digital backbone of modern **Machine Drawing and Solid Modelling**, replacing the conventional drafting board, T-square, set-squares, and drafter with a graphical user interface (GUI) and a coordinate-based mathematical canvas.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** CAD is the gateway experiment to all subsequent solid modelling labs. Mastery of input/output devices, drafting commands, and file formats is mandatory before attempting Part Modelling, Assembly, and Drawing Generation modules.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **smart, infinite, mathematically perfect sheet of paper** that sits on your desk. On a conventional drawing board:
- The paper has a fixed size (A1, A2…).
- Drawing a straight line requires a T-square and steady hands.
- A circle requires a compass.
- Correcting a mistake means erasing and redrawing.

On a CAD canvas:
- The "paper" is **virtual and infinite**; you can zoom in to atomic precision or zoom out to see an entire ship.
- A line is a mathematical entity defined by two **coordinate points** $(x_1, y_1)$ and $(x_2, y_2)$.
- A circle is defined by a **center** $(c_x, c_y)$ and a **radius** $r$.
- Corrections are made by simply **deleting** the entity and re-issuing the command with new parameters.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Think of CAD as the difference between writing a letter by hand and typing it in MS Word. In handwriting, every "A" you draw is unique and slightly imperfect. In Word, every "A" is mathematically identical, perfectly aligned, and instantly re-sizable. CAD is the engineering version of that same leap.

## 1.3 Input Devices of a CAD Workstation

Input devices are the **hardware bridges** between the engineer's intent and the software's coordinate system. Every click, cursor move, or keystroke becomes a numerical input.

| Device | Function | Typical Specification (KTU standard) |
|---|---|---|
| **Mouse** | Pointing, selecting, clicking, dragging entities | Optical, 1000–1600 DPI |
| **Keyboard** | Typing commands, coordinate values, text annotations | 104-key standard |
| **Digitizer / Graphic Tablet** | Tracing existing paper drawings into digital format | Active area ≥ 12" × 12", 5080 LPI resolution |
| **Light Pen / Stylus** | Direct-on-screen drawing on CRT/LED displays | Pressure-sensitive (1024 levels) |
| **Touch Screen** | Direct finger/stylus input on monitor | Multi-touch capacitive |
| **Scanner** | Converts raster images of drawings to digital form | 600 DPI minimum for engineering drawings |
| **3D Scanner** | Captures physical 3D geometry as point clouds | Accuracy ≤ 0.1 mm for reverse engineering |
| **Joystick / Spaceball** | 6-DOF (six degrees of freedom) navigation for 3D models | Force feedback capable |

## 1.4 Output Devices of a CAD Workstation

Output devices convert the digital model back into a human-perceivable form (visual, printed, or physical).

> [!NOTE]
> **Display Standards (KTU Lab Standard):** A CAD monitor must support a minimum resolution of **1920 × 1080 pixels** (Full HD). For professional 3D modelling, a **4K (3840 × 2160)** monitor with **sRGB ≥ 100%** color gamut is recommended. The **refresh rate** should be at least **60 Hz** to avoid eye strain.

| Device | Output Type | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Monitor (CRT/LED/LCD)** | Soft copy, real-time visual | Primary CAD display |
| **Plotter** | Large-format hard copy on paper/film | A0, A1 engineering drawings |
| **Printer (Laser/Inkjet)** | Small-format hard copy | Reports, A4/A3 drawings |
| **3D Printer (Additive)** | Physical prototype (rapid prototyping) | PLA, ABS, resin output |
| **CNC Machine (Subtractive)** | Physical part from G-code | Milling, turning, EDM |
| **VR Headset** | Immersive 3D visualization | Design review, walkthroughs |

## 1.5 Drafting Software — AutoCAD (Formal Introduction)

**AutoCAD** is a commercial 2D and 3D Computer-Aided Design (CAD) software application developed by **Autodesk**. It has been the de facto industry-standard drafting tool since its release in **December 1982**. In KTU laboratories, AutoCAD is the recommended platform for PCBRL307 because of its standardized command structure, widespread industry adoption, and full compatibility with downstream CAM/CAE tools.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **File Extension Recognition (Essential for Exams):**
> - `.dwg` — Native AutoCAD drawing file (binary format).
> - `.dxf` — Drawing Exchange Format (ASCII text, vendor-neutral).
> - `.dwf` — Design Web Format (lightweight, web-viewable).
> - `.dwt` — Drawing Template (predefined standards).
> - `.bak` — Backup file (auto-saved by AutoCAD).

## 1.6 2D Drawings vs. 3D Drawings (Conceptual Distinction)

| Aspect | 2D Drawing | 3D Drawing |
|---|---|---|
| **Coordinate System** | $(x, y)$ on a flat plane | $(x, y, z)$ in 3D space |
| **Number of Views** | Requires multiple orthographic views (Top, Front, Side) | One isometric or perspective view shows all faces |
| **Mathematical Entity** | Lines, circles, polylines, arcs | Solids, surfaces, meshes |
| **Engineering Purpose** | Manufacturing drawings, dimensioned layouts | Conceptual design, visualization, simulation |
| **File Size** | Smaller (KB range) | Larger (MB range) |

## 1.7 File Formats for 3D Modelling (IGES & STL)

**IGES (Initial Graphics Exchange Specification)** — Pronounced "eye-jess". Defined by ANSI/USPRO/IPO-100, IGES is a **vendor-neutral** ASCII or binary file format used to transfer 3D geometry between different CAD/CAM systems (e.g., SolidWorks → AutoCAD → CATIA).

**STL (STereoLithography)** — The most widely used format for **3D printing**. It represents 3D surfaces as a **triangulated mesh** (a collection of small flat triangles). STL exists in two variants: **ASCII STL** (human-readable, large file) and **Binary STL** (compact, machine-readable).

> [!NOTE]
> **The Triangle Rule:** Every surface in an STL file is approximated by triangles. More triangles = higher surface accuracy = larger file size. A typical 3D printer reads an STL file and slices it into hundreds of horizontal layers, then prints each layer one on top of the other.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The CAD Workflow — From Intent to Output

The CAD process is not a single step; it is a **closed-loop engineering cycle**. The KTU board examiner expects students to identify each phase:

1. **Problem Definition** — Identify what must be designed.
2. **Conceptual Sketching** — Rough 2D layout to fix proportions.
3. **CAD Modelling** — Create precise 2D geometry or 3D solid.
4. **Analysis / Simulation** — Stress, thermal, fluid flow (FEA/CFD).
5. **Drafting / Detailing** — Generate dimensioned manufacturing drawings.
6. **Manufacturing Output** — Export to CNC (G-code) or 3D printer (STL/G-code).
7. **Feedback / Revision** — Iterate based on test results.

> [!NOTE]
> **Closed-Loop Justification:** The word "loop" is critical. CAD is not a one-shot operation; iterations of the model are expected. This is why modern CAD packages support **parametric modelling** — when you change one dimension, all dependent geometry updates automatically.

## 2.2 Coordinate System Theory

AutoCAD operates in the **World Coordinate System (WCS)** by default, with the origin at $(0, 0, 0)$. For each 2D drawing operation, the user specifies points in either:
- **Absolute Coordinates:** Direct entry, e.g., `LINE 0,0 100,50`.
- **Relative Coordinates:** Offset from the last point, prefixed with `@`, e.g., `@100,50`.
- **Polar Coordinates:** Distance and angle, e.g., `@100<45` (100 units at 45°).

The fundamental relationship between **Cartesian and Polar** coordinates in a 2D plane is given by:

$$
x = r \cos\theta
$$

$$
y = r \sin\theta
$$

where $r$ is the radial distance from the origin and $\theta$ is the angle measured counter-clockwise from the positive X-axis.

## 2.3 Basic AutoCAD Commands — KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

The following table is the **minimum command set** a KTU student must memorize for both lab viva and written exams. Commands are typed at the command line or activated from the Ribbon.

| Command | Shortcut | Function | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **LINE** | `L` | Draws straight line segments | Sketching edges, boundaries |
| **CIRCLE** | `C` | Draws a circle | Holes, shafts, fillets |
| **ARC** | `A` | Draws an arc | Rounded corners, fillets |
| **RECTANGLE** | `REC` | Draws a rectangle | Blocks, base plates |
| **POLYLINE** | `PL` | Connected line/arc segments as one entity | Complex profiles |
| **ERASE** | `E` | Deletes selected entities | Correcting mistakes |
| **COPY** | `CO` or `CP` | Copies entities | Repeated features |
| **MOVE** | `M` | Relocates entities | Repositioning |
| **ROTATE** | `RO` | Rotates entities about a base point | Angular features |
| **MIRROR** | `MI` | Creates a mirror image | Symmetric parts |
| **OFFSET** | `O` | Creates parallel copies at given distance | Concentric circles, walls |
| **TRIM** | `TR` | Trims entities at cutting edges | Removing excess lines |
| **EXTEND** | `EX` | Extends entities to meet boundaries | Completing lines |
| **FILLET** | `F` | Rounds sharp corners | Smooth transitions |
| **CHAMFER** | `CHA` | Bevels sharp corners | Edge preparation |
| **SCALE** | `SC` | Resizes entities uniformly | Enlarging/reducing |
| **ARRAY** | `AR` | Creates rectangular/polar patterns | Bolt holes, gear teeth |
| **HATCH** | `H` | Fills closed areas with patterns | Sectional views |
| **DIM** | `DIM` | Applies dimensions | Manufacturing drawings |
| **EXTRUDE** | `EXT` | Converts 2D to 3D solid (3D) | Bosses, ribs |
| **REVOLVE** | `REV` | Revolves 2D profile about an axis (3D) | Shafts, pulleys |
| **UNION** | `UNI` | Combines 3D solids | Assembly-level features |
| **SUBTRACT** | `SU` | Subtracts one solid from another | Holes, pockets |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Command Invocation Modes in AutoCAD:**
> 1. **Typing the full command** (e.g., `LINE`) in the command line.
> 2. **Typing the command alias** (e.g., `L` for LINE).
> 3. **Ribbon / Toolbar click** — clicking the corresponding icon.
> 4. **Dynamic Input** — typing values directly near the cursor.

## 2.4 2D Drawing Development — Step-by-Step Logic

A 2D engineering drawing in AutoCAD proceeds through these logical phases:

- **Step 1: Setup** — Set units (UNITS command), limits (LIMITS), grid (GRID), snap (SNAP), and ortho (ORTHO) modes.
- **Step 2: Sketching** — Use LINE, CIRCLE, ARC to build the geometry.
- **Step 3: Modification** — Use TRIM, EXTEND, OFFSET, FILLET, CHAMFER to refine.
- **Step 4: Layering** — Assign entities to layers (e.g., layer "Center" for centerlines).
- **Step 5: Dimensioning** — Apply linear, angular, and radial dimensions.
- **Step 6: Annotation** — Add text, hatching, title block.
- **Step 7: Plotting** — Print or plot the final drawing using the PLOT command.

## 2.5 3D Drawing Development — Step-by-Step Logic

A 3D solid model in AutoCAD proceeds through these logical phases:

- **Step 1: Workspace Switch** — Switch to the "3D Modelling" workspace.
- **Step 2: Viewport Setup** — Use isometric views (e.g., SE Isometric) to visualize 3D.
- **Step 3: 2D Profile** — Draw the 2D cross-section as a closed polyline.
- **Step 4: Solid Creation** — Apply EXTRUDE (linear), REVOLVE (rotational), SWEEP (along path), or LOFT (between profiles).
- **Step 5: Boolean Operations** — Use UNION, SUBTRACT, INTERSECT to combine/remove solids.
- **Step 6: Modification** — Use FILLET 3D, CHAMFER 3D, ARRAY 3D for refinement.
- **Step 7: Export** — Save as `.dwg` (native), `.iges` (exchange), or `.stl` (3D print).

## 2.6 IGES File Format — Theoretical Background

An IGES file is organized into **sections**, each starting with a specific letter code in column 1:

| Section | Code | Content |
|---|---|---|
| **Start** | `S` | Human-readable header |
| **Global** | `G` | Pre-processor information |
| **Directory Entry** | `D` | Index of each entity |
| **Parameter Data** | `P` | Geometric and topological data |
| **Terminate** | `T` | End of file marker |

The unit conversion inside IGES follows the simple linear relation:

$$
L_{\text{SI}} = L_{\text{IGES}} \times k
$$

where $k$ is a scaling factor stored in the Global section (e.g., $k = 25.4$ converts inches to millimetres).

## 2.7 STL File Format — Theoretical Background

An STL file describes a 3D surface as a **triangular mesh**. For each triangle, the file stores the **normal vector** $\hat{n}$ and the three vertex coordinates $V_1, V_2, V_3$. The normal vector is computed using the right-hand rule and the cross product:

$$
\hat{n} = \frac{(V_2 - V_1) \times (V_3 - V_1)}{\vert (V_2 - V_1) \times (V_3 - V_1) \vert}
$$

This formula guarantees that all triangles are consistently oriented, which is critical for 3D printers that need to know which side of a triangle is the **outer surface** versus the **inner cavity**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility of STL:** The entire 3D-printing industry — from hobbyist FDM printers to industrial SLA machines — relies on STL. Medical implants (hip joints, dental crowns), aerospace prototypes, and architectural models are all delivered as STL files before manufacturing.

## 2.8 Comparison of 2D Drafting vs. 3D Modelling Software Paradigms

| Criterion | 2D Drafting (AutoCAD Classic) | 3D Solid Modelling (AutoCAD 3D / Inventor) |
|---|---|---|
| **Data structure** | Vector entities on a plane | Boundary representation (B-Rep) of solids |
| **Primary use** | Manufacturing drawings, schematics | Conceptual design, simulation, 3D printing |
| **Modification ease** | Manual editing of each entity | Parametric; dimensions drive geometry |
| **Output to manufacturing** | 2D DXF/DWG sent to CAM | STL/STEP sent to 3D printer or CNC |
| **Learning curve** | Moderate | Steep |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Workflows & Modelling Procedures

This section presents the **complete, step-by-step analytical procedures** for the most common KTU exam scenarios on this topic. No steps are skipped.

## 3.1 Workflow A — Drawing a 2D Hexagonal Bolt Head (Analytical Construction)

A regular hexagon of circumscribed-circle radius $R$ has its vertices at angular intervals of $60°$ about the center. We will construct it using polar coordinates and then apply the **ROTATE** and **COPY** commands.

**Given:** Hexagon circumscribed-circle radius $R = 30 \text{ mm}$. Center at $(0, 0, 0)$.

**Step 1: Compute the first vertex using polar-to-Cartesian conversion.**

$$
x_1 = R \cos(0°) = 30 \cos(0°) = 30 \times 1 = 30
$$

$$
y_1 = R \sin(0°) = 30 \sin(0°) = 30 \times 0 = 0
$$

So $V_1 = (30, 0)$.

**Step 2: Compute the second vertex at $\theta = 60°$.**

$$
x_2 = 30 \cos(60°) = 30 \times 0.5 = 15
$$

$$
y_2 = 30 \sin(60°) = 30 \times 0.8660 = 25.98
$$

So $V_2 = (15, 25.98)$.

**Step 3: Compute the third vertex at $\theta = 120°$.**

$$
x_3 = 30 \cos(120°) = 30 \times (-0.5) = -15
$$

$$
y_3 = 30 \sin(120°) = 30 \times 0.8660 = 25.98
$$

So $V_3 = (-15, 25.98)$.

**Step 4: Continue for all six vertices** at $0°, 60°, 120°, 180°, 240°, 300°$. The remaining vertices are:

$$
V_4 = (-30, 0)
$$

$$
V_5 = (-15, -25.98)
$$

$$
V_6 = (15, -25.98)
$$

**Step 5: Build the hexagon using the AutoCAD POLYLINE command.**

Command sequence:
```
PL
Specify start point: 30,0
Specify next point: 15,25.98
Specify next point: -15,25.98
Specify next point: -30,0
Specify next point: -15,-25.98
Specify next point: 15,-25.98
Specify next point (or Close): C
```

**Step 6: Apply the CIRCLE command for the bolt-head outer circle (optional, for dimensional reference).**

Command sequence:
```
C
Specify center point: 0,0
Specify radius: 30
```

The hexagon is now drawn. The total perimeter $P$ of the hexagon, useful for chamfering or inspection, is:

$$
P = 6 \times s
$$

where $s$ is the side length. For a regular hexagon with circumradius $R$, the side length $s$ equals $R$. Therefore:

$$
P = 6 \times 30 = 180 \text{ mm}
$$

## 3.2 Workflow B — Converting a 2D Profile to a 3D Solid (Extrusion Procedure)

This is a **very high-yield KTU exam procedure**. We will extrude a 2D rectangle to a 3D block.

**Given:** A 2D rectangle with corners at $(0,0)$, $(50,0)$, $(50,30)$, $(0,30)$. Extrusion height $h = 20$ mm.

**Step 1: Draw the 2D rectangle using the RECTANGLE command.**

Command sequence:
```
REC
Specify first corner: 0,0
Specify other corner: 50,30
```

**Step 2: Switch to the 3D Modelling workspace.**

Navigate the top ribbon: **Workspace Switching → 3D Modelling**.

**Step 3: Set the visual style to "Conceptual" or "Realistic".**

Command: `VSCURRENT` → choose `Conceptual`.

**Step 4: Set the isometric view.**

Command: `VIEW` → select `SE Isometric`.

**Step 5: Apply the EXTRUDE command.**

Command sequence:
```
EXT
Select objects: (click the rectangle)
Select objects: 1 found, 1 total
Select objects: (press Enter to confirm)
Specify height of extrusion: 20
Specify angle of taper: 0
```

**Step 6: Calculate the resulting 3D volume for verification.**

The volume of the extruded block is:

$$
V = \text{Area}_{\text{base}} \times h
$$

$$
V = (50 \times 30) \times 20 = 1500 \times 20 = 30000 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Step 7: Apply a hole using the SUBTRACT command.**

Draw a 2D circle on the **top face** of the block at center $(25, 15)$ with radius $r = 5$ mm. Extrude this circle by the full block height (20 mm) into a **cylinder solid**. Then subtract:

```
SU
Select solids and regions to subtract from: (click block)
Select solids and regions to subtract from: (press Enter)
Select objects to subtract: (click cylinder)
Select objects to subtract: (press Enter)
```

**Step 8: Verify final volume.**

The new volume is:

$$
V_{\text{final}} = V_{\text{block}} - V_{\text{cylinder}} = 30000 - (\pi \times 5^2 \times 20)
$$

$$
V_{\text{final}} = 30000 - (\pi \times 25 \times 20) = 30000 - 500\pi
$$

$$
V_{\text{final}} = 30000 - 1570.80 = 28429.20 \text{ mm}^3
$$

## 3.3 Workflow C — Exporting a 3D Model to STL (Step-by-Step)

This workflow is essential for any student who wishes to 3D print their model.

**Step 1: Open the 3D solid model in AutoCAD.**

**Step 2: Type the EXPORT command.**

Command: `EXPORT` or `EXPORTSTL`.

**Step 3: Choose the file location and filename.**

File name: `MyBracket.stl`

**Step 4: Choose ASCII or Binary STL.**

For most 3D printers, **Binary STL** is preferred because it is **5–10× smaller** in file size.

**Step 5: Set the surface deviation (facet resolution).**

A higher facet resolution produces smoother curves but larger files. A typical good setting is:

$$
\text{Deviation} \leq 0.001 \times L_{\text{max}}
$$

where $L_{\text{max}}$ is the largest dimension of the model.

**Step 6: Verify the STL using a slicer.**

Open the file in **Cura** or **PrusaSlicer** to verify the mesh and slice it into layers.

## 3.4 Workflow D — Conversion Between IGES and Native AutoCAD Format

**Step 1:** To **import** an IGES file, use the command `IMPORT` and select the `.igs` / `.iges` file.

**Step 2:** AutoCAD will convert the IGES geometry into native AutoCAD entities (lines, arcs, 3D solids).

**Step 3:** To **export** to IGES, use the command `EXPORT` and select `IGES` from the file-type dropdown.

**Step 4:** Check the IGES Global section to confirm units. If the source model was in inches and the destination is in mm, the scaling factor $k$ must be applied:

$$
L_{\text{mm}} = L_{\text{inches}} \times 25.4
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Tolerance Check:** Most IGES importers in AutoCAD allow a coordinate tolerance setting. A value of $0.001$ mm is acceptable for general mechanical parts; tighter tolerances like $0.0001$ mm are needed for precision moulds and gauges.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Command Selection Strategy

The following decision matrix helps students select the right AutoCAD command for a given geometric requirement. This is a **common KTU viva question**.

| Geometric Requirement | Recommended Command | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Straight edge of length 50 mm | `LINE` | Simplest primitive |
| Curved edge with known radius | `ARC` or `CIRCLE` | Defined by center, radius, and sweep angle |
| Repeated bolt-hole pattern | `ARRAY` (polar) | Circular pattern in one command |
| Hatching a cross-section | `HATCH` | Fills closed area with pattern |
| Mirror-symmetric part (e.g., bracket) | `MIRROR` | Creates half the part, mirrors to get the full part |
| 3D solid from flat plate | `EXTRUDE` | Adds depth to 2D profile |
| 3D solid of axisymmetric part | `REVOLVE` | Rotates 2D profile about an axis |
| 3D solid along a curved path | `SWEEP` | Sweeps profile along a path curve |
| Smooth transition between two faces | `FILLET` (3D) | Adds rounded fillet to a solid edge |
| Combine two overlapping solids | `UNION` | Boolean addition |
| Drill a hole through a solid | `SUBTRACT` | Boolean subtraction |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CAD System Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

This diagram shows how a CAD workstation transforms user intent into a manufactured part. Each block represents a major functional stage, and the arrows show the **flow of data** through the system.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Intent] --> B[Input Devices]
    B --> C[CAD Software Engine]
    C --> D[Geometric Database]
    D --> E[Modelling Workspace]
    E --> F[Drafting Workspace]
    F --> G[Output Devices]
    G --> H[Manufacturing]
    H --> I[Physical Product]
    I --> J[Feedback to User]
    J --> A

    K[File Format Layer] --> C
    K --> F
    K --> G

    L[IGES] --> K
    M[STL] --> K
    N[DWG] --> K
    O[DXF] --> K
```

**Explanation of the architecture:**
- The **User Intent** (e.g., "I want a 50 mm shaft with a 10 mm hole") enters the system via **Input Devices** (mouse, keyboard, digitizer).
- The **CAD Software Engine** interprets the commands and updates the **Geometric Database**.
- The user toggles between **Modelling** (3D solid creation) and **Drafting** (2D drawing with dimensions) workspaces.
- The **Output Devices** (plotter, 3D printer, CNC) manufacture the part.
- The **Feedback Loop** ensures continuous design refinement.
- The **File Format Layer** handles translation between `.dwg`, `.dxf`, `.iges`, and `.stl`.

## 4.2 2D to 3D Modelling Workflow (Process Flow)

This diagram illustrates the **logical progression** from a 2D drawing to a 3D printed part. It is a high-yield visual aid for explaining the workflow in exams.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A2[2D Sketches] --> B2[Closed Polyline]
    B2 --> C2{Choose Solid Command}
    C2 --> D2[Extrude]
    C2 --> E2[Revolve]
    C2 --> F2[Sweep]
    C2 --> G2[Loft]
    D2 --> H2[3D Solid Model]
    E2 --> H2
    F2 --> H2
    G2 --> H2
    H2 --> I2[Boolean Operations]
    I2 --> J2[Final 3D Part]
    J2 --> K2[Export as STL]
    J2 --> L2[Export as IGES]
    J2 --> M2[Save as DWG]
    K2 --> N2[3D Print]
    L2 --> O2[Inter-CAD Transfer]
    M2 --> P2[Documentation]
```

**Explanation of the workflow:**
- A 2D sketch is converted into a **closed polyline** (a prerequisite for all 3D solid commands).
- The user chooses the appropriate **Solid Command** based on the part geometry.
- **Boolean operations** (Union, Subtract, Intersect) refine the solid.
- The final 3D part is exported in one of three formats depending on the **end goal** (3D print, CAD exchange, or documentation).

## 4.3 Input-Output Device Ecosystem (Block Diagram)

This diagram maps the **complete ecosystem** of input and output devices in a typical KTU CAD lab.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph InputDevices
        I1[Mouse]
        I2[Keyboard]
        I3[Digitizer Tablet]
        I4[3D Scanner]
        I5[Touch Screen]
    end

    subgraph ProcessingCore
        P1[CAD Software AutoCAD]
        P2[Geometric Database]
    end

    subgraph OutputDevices
        O1[Monitor]
        O2[Plotter]
        O3[3D Printer]
        O4[CNC Machine]
        O5[VR Headset]
    end

    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P1
    I3 --> P1
    I4 --> P1
    I5 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P1
    P1 --> O1
    P1 --> O2
    P1 --> O3
    P1 --> O4
    P1 --> O5
```

**Explanation of the ecosystem:**
- The **Input Devices** (left) feed user commands into the **Processing Core** (AutoCAD software and its geometric database).
- The **Output Devices** (right) display or manufacture the resulting design.
- The **bidirectional arrows** between the software and the database indicate that the database is continuously updated and queried during a session.

## 4.4 Command Execution Cycle in AutoCAD (Flowchart)

Every AutoCAD command follows a **predictable cycle**. This diagram explains the cycle, which is often asked in viva voce.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[User Types Command] --> S2[Command Parser Validates]
    S2 --> S3{Valid Command?}
    S3 -->|No| S4[Display Error Message]
    S4 --> S1
    S3 -->|Yes| S5[Display Prompts]
    S5 --> S6[User Provides Inputs]
    S6 --> S7[Command Executes Action]
    S7 --> S8[Update Geometric Database]
    S8 --> S9[Refresh Display]
    S9 --> S10[Return to Idle State]
    S10 --> S1
```

**Explanation of the cycle:**
- The user types a command (e.g., `CIRCLE`).
- The parser validates the command; if invalid, an error is shown.
- The command displays prompts asking for inputs (e.g., center point, radius).
- The user provides the inputs via mouse/keyboard.
- The command executes the geometric action, updates the database, and refreshes the display.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

This section contains exam-style questions calibrated to the **KTU 2024 Scheme assessment regulations** for PCBRL307. Marks are distributed as per the official ESE pattern.

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define CAD. List any four input devices used in a CAD workstation.
**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**

**Model Answer:**

CAD (Computer-Aided Design) is the use of computer hardware and software to create, modify, analyse, and optimize an engineering design. It replaces the manual drafting board with a digital, coordinate-based canvas that allows precise geometry creation, easy editing, and direct export to manufacturing.

**Four input devices:**
1. **Mouse** — for pointing, clicking, and selecting entities.
2. **Keyboard** — for typing commands and coordinate values.
3. **Digitizer / Graphic Tablet** — for tracing existing paper drawings into digital format.
4. **3D Scanner** — for capturing physical object geometry as point clouds for reverse engineering.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between IGES and STL file formats. State one engineering application of each.
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | IGES (Initial Graphics Exchange Specification) | STL (STereoLithography) |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Vendor-neutral 3D geometry exchange | 3D printing mesh format |
| **Structure** | Sectioned ASCII/binary file (S, G, D, P, T) | Triangulated mesh of 3D surfaces |
| **Geometric fidelity** | High (curves, surfaces, solids preserved) | Approximate (triangular facets) |
| **Typical use** | Transferring models between different CAD systems (e.g., CATIA to AutoCAD) | Direct input to 3D printers and slicers |

**Application:** IGES is used in **aerospace CAD-CAM data exchange** between design (CATIA) and manufacturing (Siemens NX). STL is used in **dental crown 3D printing**, where the digital scan is converted to STL and printed in resin.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module Experiment 2]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the difference between 2D and 3D drawings. Describe the basic AutoCAD commands required to draw and modify a 2D engineering sketch. List at least **eight** commands with their function.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat step-by-step procedure, explain how a 2D profile is converted into a 3D solid using the EXTRUDE command in AutoCAD. Compute the volume of an extruded block of base $60 \text{ mm} \times 40 \text{ mm}$ and extrusion height $25 \text{ mm}$. After a cylindrical hole of radius $8 \text{ mm}$ is drilled through the full height, compute the final volume.

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — [7 Marks]**

| Aspect | 2D Drawing | 3D Drawing |
|---|---|---|
| Coordinate system | 2D plane $(x, y)$ | 3D space $(x, y, z)$ |
| Views required | Multiple orthographic views | Single isometric or perspective view |
| Mathematical representation | Lines, circles, arcs, polylines | Solids, surfaces, meshes |
| Engineering purpose | Manufacturing drawings, dimensioned layouts | Conceptual design, simulation, 3D printing |

**Eight Basic AutoCAD Commands (with function):**

1. `LINE (L)` — Draws straight line segments between two points.
2. `CIRCLE (C)` — Draws a circle given the center and radius.
3. `ARC (A)` — Draws an arc given the start, end, and radius.
4. `RECTANGLE (REC)` — Draws a rectangle given two opposite corners.
5. `POLYLINE (PL)` — Draws connected segments as a single entity.
6. `ERASE (E)` — Deletes selected entities.
7. `COPY (CO)` — Copies entities to a new location.
8. `MIRROR (MI)` — Creates a mirror image of selected entities about a line.
9. `OFFSET (O)` — Creates parallel copies at a given distance.
10. `TRIM (TR)` — Trims entities at cutting edges.

**Part (b) — [7 Marks]**

**Step 1:** Draw the 2D rectangle using `REC` with corners $(0,0)$ and $(60, 40)$.

**Step 2:** Switch to the 3D Modelling workspace via the workspace switching button.

**Step 3:** Set the view to `SE Isometric` for proper 3D visualization.

**Step 4:** Invoke the EXTRUDE command:
```
EXT
Select objects: (click rectangle, press Enter)
Specify height of extrusion: 25
Specify angle of taper: 0
```

**Step 5:** The block solid is created. The volume of the original extruded block:

$$
V_{\text{block}} = \text{Area}_{\text{base}} \times h
$$

$$
V_{\text{block}} = (60 \times 40) \times 25 = 2400 \times 25 = 60000 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Step 6:** Draw a 2D circle at the center of the top face $(30, 20)$ with radius $8$ mm, and extrude it by the full height of $25$ mm to create a cylinder.

**Step 7:** Subtract the cylinder from the block using the `SUBTRACT` command.

**Step 8:** Final volume:

$$
V_{\text{cylinder}} = \pi r^2 h = \pi \times 8^2 \times 25 = \pi \times 64 \times 25 = 1600\pi
$$

$$
V_{\text{final}} = V_{\text{block}} - V_{\text{cylinder}} = 60000 - 1600\pi
$$

Using $\pi = 3.14159$:

$$
V_{\text{final}} = 60000 - 5026.55 = 54973.45 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Answer:** The final volume of the block with the cylindrical hole is $\mathbf{54973.45 \text{ mm}^3}$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module Experiment 2]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** List and explain the major input and output devices used in a CAD workstation. Briefly describe the role of a plotter and a 3D printer.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the IGES and STL file formats used in 3D modelling. Describe the structure of an ASCII STL file and mention the formula for the normal vector of a triangular facet.

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — [7 Marks]**

**Input Devices:**
- **Mouse:** Pointing device for selecting and clicking entities; optical mouse with 1000+ DPI is standard.
- **Keyboard:** Enters text commands, coordinates, and dimension values.
- **Digitizer:** A tablet with a stylus used to trace paper drawings into digital format.
- **3D Scanner:** Captures physical object geometry as a 3D point cloud.

**Output Devices:**
- **Monitor:** Displays the digital drawing in real time; minimum 1920 × 1080 resolution is recommended.
- **Plotter:** Produces large-format hard copies (A0, A1) of engineering drawings on paper or film.
- **3D Printer:** Creates a physical prototype from an STL file layer-by-layer (additive manufacturing).
- **CNC Machine:** Manufactures the part by subtractive milling based on a G-code file generated from the CAD model.

**Role of a Plotter:** A plotter draws vector graphics on paper using pens, knives, or other instruments. It is the primary output device for producing large engineering drawings in industry and design offices. Modern plotters are typically inkjet or electrostatic types.

**Role of a 3D Printer:** A 3D printer creates a physical object from a digital STL file by depositing material (plastic, resin, or metal) layer-by-layer. It enables **rapid prototyping**, allowing engineers to test a part's form, fit, and function before committing to expensive tooling.

**Part (b) — [7 Marks]**

**IGES (Initial Graphics Exchange Specification):**
IGES is a vendor-neutral file format developed in the USA in **1979** to enable the transfer of 2D and 3D CAD data between different software systems. It uses an **ASCII text structure** with five sections: Start (S), Global (G), Directory Entry (D), Parameter Data (P), and Terminate (T). The Global section contains pre-processor info such as units, file name, and date. IGES preserves curves, surfaces, and solid geometry with high accuracy.

**STL (STereoLithography):**
STL is the de facto standard file format for **3D printing**, originally developed by 3D Systems in **1987**. It describes a 3D surface as a triangulated mesh. There are two variants: **ASCII STL** (human-readable) and **Binary STL** (compact). Each triangle is defined by a **normal vector** $\hat{n}$ and three vertex coordinates $V_1$, $V_2$, $V_3$.

**Structure of an ASCII STL file:**

```
solid name
  facet normal nx ny nz
    outer loop
      vertex x1 y1 z1
      vertex x2 y2 z2
      vertex x3 y3 z3
    endloop
  endfacet
  ...
endsolid name
```

**Formula for the normal vector of a triangular facet:**

$$
\hat{n} = \frac{(V_2 - V_1) \times (V_3 - V_1)}{\vert (V_2 - V_1) \times (V_3 - V_1) \vert}
$$

The numerator is the cross product of two edge vectors, and the denominator normalizes the result to a **unit vector**. This normalization ensures that the magnitude of the normal is exactly 1, which is required by most 3D printer slicer software.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing the formula for normal vector** — Students often write $\hat{n} = (V_2 - V_1) \times (V_3 - V_1)$ without the normalization in the denominator. **Both parts are required** to get a unit normal.
> 2. **Mixing up the STL and IGES purposes** — STL is for **3D printing**, IGES is for **CAD-CAM data exchange**. Marks are deducted if these are swapped.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention the unit conversion** — When importing IGES, students must verify the units in the Global section. A 25.4 factor (inches to mm) is commonly tested.
> 4. **Using `|` in formulas** — Always use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. (Note: this is a writing-style warning, not a math error.)
> 5. **Omitting the ASCII STL structure** — Examiners specifically look for the words `solid`, `facet normal`, `outer loop`, `vertex`, `endloop`, `endfacet`, `endsolid` in the model answer.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> Use this list as a **last-minute revision sheet** before your KTU lab viva or written exam.

### Core Definitions
- **CAD** = Computer-Aided Design. Replaces manual drafting with a digital coordinate-based system.
- **AutoCAD** = Autodesk's flagship 2D/3D drafting software, released in 1982, industry standard.
- **WCS** = World Coordinate System, the default $(0, 0, 0)$ origin of any AutoCAD drawing.
- **IGES** = Initial Graphics Exchange Specification; vendor-neutral CAD data exchange format.
- **STL** = STereoLithography; triangulated mesh format for 3D printing.

### Input Devices to Memorize
- Mouse, Keyboard, Digitizer Tablet, 3D Scanner, Touch Screen, Light Pen, Joystick/Spaceball.

### Output Devices to Memorize
- Monitor, Plotter, Printer, 3D Printer, CNC Machine, VR Headset.

### Essential AutoCAD Commands (Must Memorize)
- **Drawing:** LINE, CIRCLE, ARC, RECTANGLE, POLYLINE.
- **Modification:** ERASE, COPY, MOVE, ROTATE, MIRROR, OFFSET, TRIM, EXTEND, FILLET, CHAMFER, SCALE, ARRAY.
- **Annotation:** HATCH, DIM.
- **3D:** EXTRUDE, REVOLVE, SWEEP, LOFT, UNION, SUBTRACT, INTERSECT.

### File Format Extensions (Must Memorize)
- `.dwg` (native), `.dxf` (exchange), `.dwf` (web), `.dwt` (template), `.iges` / `.igs` (vendor-neutral 3D), `.stl` (3D print mesh).

### Key Formulas
- Polar to Cartesian: $x = r\cos\theta$, $y = r\sin\theta$.
- Volume of extrusion: $V = A_{\text{base}} \times h$.
- STL normal vector: $\hat{n} = \dfrac{(V_2 - V_1) \times (V_3 - V_1)}{\vert (V_2 - V_1) \times (V_3 - V_1) \vert}$.
- Hexagon vertex: $V_i = (R\cos(i \cdot 60°), R\sin(i \cdot 60°))$ for $i = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$.
- IGES unit conversion: $L_{\text{SI}} = L_{\text{IGES}} \times k$.

### ASCII STL File Keywords (in order)
`solid` → `facet normal` → `outer loop` → `vertex` → `endloop` → `endfacet` → `endsolid`.

### 2D vs. 3D — Quick Recall
- 2D uses $(x, y)$ and requires multiple orthographic views.
- 3D uses $(x, y, z)$ and shows the entire model in one isometric view.

### 3D Solid Creation Methods
- **Extrude** — 2D profile pushed linearly.
- **Revolve** — 2D profile rotated about an axis.
- **Sweep** — 2D profile moved along a path.
- **Loft** — 3D solid formed between two or more 2D profiles.

### Boolean Operations
- **Union** = Addition, **Subtract** = Subtraction, **Intersect** = Common volume.

### Final Exam Tips
- Always write the **command sequence** (the actual AutoCAD command-line text) in your answer for full marks.
- When computing volumes, **show the substitution step** clearly.
- For IGES questions, mention the **five sections (S, G, D, P, T)**.
- For STL questions, mention **both ASCII and Binary** variants and the **triangulated mesh** concept.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CAD Operations: Drawing, Editing, Dimensioning, Plotting Commands, Layering Concepts, Matching, Detailing, and Detailed Drawings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRL307 - MACHINE DRAWING AND SOLID MODELLING
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** CAD Operations: Drawing, Editing, Dimensioning, Plotting Commands, Layering Concepts, Matching, Detailing, and Detailed Drawings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 CAD Operations — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **CAD (Computer-Aided Design)** refers to the use of computer systems to assist in the creation, modification, analysis, and optimization of a technical drawing or engineering design. In the context of KTU's PCBRL307, CAD operations encompass the **standardized command set** used to construct, edit, annotate, and output 2D engineering drawings using software such as **AutoCAD**, **SolidWorks**, or **CATIA**.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus**, *CAD Operations* under Module Experiment 2 specifically refer to the practical command-level workflow a student must master to produce **machine drawings** that conform to **BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards) SP:46** drafting conventions. The operations are grouped into eight functional clusters:

| Cluster | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Drawing Commands** | Create geometric primitives (Line, Circle, Arc, Polyline, Rectangle, Ellipse) |
| **Editing Commands** | Modify existing geometry (Move, Copy, Trim, Extend, Mirror, Rotate, Offset, Fillet, Chamfer) |
| **Dimensioning** | Add BIS-standard linear, angular, radial, and diametric dimensions |
| **Plotting Commands** | Output the digital drawing to paper/PDF (Layout, Viewport, Plot, Page Setup) |
| **Layering Concepts** | Organize drawing entities into named, color-coded, line-typed layers |
| **Matching (Properties Match)** | Transfer properties from one object to another (Matchprop / MA) |
| **Detailing** | Add sectional views, hatching, breaks, and auxiliary details |
| **Detailed Drawings** | Production of assembly and part drawings with full title block, BOM, and notes |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Architect's Digital Workshop"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition:** Think of a CAD software as a **digital drafting table equipped with infinite precision, an eraser that never smudges, and a photocopier that can duplicate any line in milliseconds**. Drawing commands are the **pens and compasses**, editing commands are the **erasers and correction tools**, layers are the **transparent overlay sheets** that draftsmen stack (one for dimensions, one for hidden lines, one for centerlines), and plotting is the **final blue-printing machine** that converts the digital sheet into a physical drawing.

In traditional manual drafting, an engineering drawing of a machine component (say, a **shaft** or a **flange coupling**) might take 4–6 hours. With CAD operations, the same drawing — with full dimensioning, multiple views, sectional cuts, and an **A2-size** plotted output — can be produced in 45–60 minutes with **far higher accuracy (typically ±0.01 mm)**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Drafting Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Constants to Remember:**
> - **Standard Sheet Sizes (BIS SP:46):** A0 = 841 × 1189 mm, A1 = 594 × 841 mm, A2 = 420 × 594 mm, A3 = 297 × 420 mm, A4 = 210 × 297 mm
> - **Drawing Scale (recommended):** 1:1 for CAD (full-size), or 1:2, 1:5 for plotted reductions
> - **Line Weights (BIS):** Thick lines (visible edges) = **0.7 mm**, Thin lines (dimension/hidden) = **0.35 mm**
> - **Text Height:** Title block text = **5 mm**, Dimension text = **2.5 mm**, General notes = **3.5 mm**
> - **Hatching (sectioning) spacing:** Typically **2–3 mm** between parallel hatch lines, angle **45°** for general sections

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Command Sheet

## 2.1 Drawing Commands — The Geometry Generators

Drawing commands are the **primitive construction tools**. Each command typically follows the pattern:

```
Command_Entry → Prompt for input → User specifies points/parameters → Entity created
```

### A. Line Command
- **Function:** Draws a straight line segment between two specified points.
- **Prompt sequence:** `Specify first point:` → `Specify next point or [Undo]:`
- **KTU Tip:** Use **ORTHO (F8)** mode to constrain lines to horizontal/vertical directions — essential for machine drawings.

### B. Circle Command
- **Function:** Draws a circle given center and radius, or center and diameter, or two/three points.
- **Six sub-options in AutoCAD:**
  1. Center, Radius (default)
  2. Center, Diameter
  3. 2-Point
  4. 3-Point
  5. Tangent, Tangent, Radius (TTR)
  6. Tangent, Tangent, Tangent (TTT)
- **KTU Application:** Circles appear in **holes, shafts, flanges, and gear blanks**.

### C. Arc Command
- **Function:** Draws an arc segment.
- **11 sub-options** in AutoCAD (3-point, Start-Center-End, Start-Center-Angle, etc.)
- **KTU Application:** **Fillet radii** in machine components, **chamfers**, and **involute gear tooth profiles**.

### D. Polyline (PLINE)
- **Function:** Creates a connected sequence of line and arc segments as a **single object**.
- **Why critical for KTU:** Machine parts like **flange outlines, key profiles, and rivet patterns** must remain a closed continuous boundary — polylines ensure this.

### E. Rectangle, Polygon, Ellipse, Spline
- **Rectangle:** Used for **bearing blocks, gland packing boxes**.
- **Polygon:** Used for **hexagonal nuts and bolt heads**.
- **Ellipse:** Used for **elliptical fillets, cam profiles**.
- **Spline:** Used for **fairing curves, blade profiles**.

## 2.2 Editing Commands — The Geometry Modifiers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Editing commands are invoked **more frequently** than drawing commands in a typical machine drawing. A 100-entity part drawing may require 200+ edit operations.

### Core Editing Commands (with keyboard shortcuts in AutoCAD):

| Command | Shortcut | Function | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Move | `M` | Relocates objects | Repositioning a view |
| Copy | `CO` | Duplicates objects | Multiple identical holes |
| Rotate | `RO` | Rotates around base point | Rotating section view |
| Mirror | `MI` | Creates mirror image | Symmetrical parts (flange) |
| Offset | `O` | Creates parallel copy | Concentric circles, parallel edges |
| Trim | `TR` | Cuts at intersecting edges | Removing excess line work |
| Extend | `EX` | Extends to boundary | Completing centerlines |
| Fillet | `F` | Rounds sharp corners | Fillet radii in shafts |
| Chamfer | `CHA` | Creates beveled edge | Chamfered edges of gears |
| Array | `AR` | Rectangular/Polar pattern | Bolt circle holes |
| Scale | `SC` | Resizes uniformly | Rare in production drawings |
| Stretch | `S` | Stretches selected portion | Lengthening a shaft |
| Break | `BR` | Breaks object at point | Breaking long centerlines |
| Erase | `E` | Deletes objects | Cleanup |

## 2.3 Dimensioning — The Communication Language

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Dimensioning is the process of **adding numerical values** to a drawing to define the **size, location, and geometric tolerance** of features. In KTU's PCBRL307, dimensions must strictly follow **BIS SP:46-2003** conventions.

### Dimension Types and Their Commands:

| Dimension Type | AutoCAD Command | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| Linear (Horizontal/Vertical) | `DLI` | Length, width, height of part |
| Aligned | `DAL` | Dimensions parallel to an inclined edge |
| Angular | `DAN` | Angle between two lines (e.g., chamfer angle) |
| Radius | `DRA` | Fillet radius |
| Diameter | `DDI` | Hole/shaft diameter |
| Baseline | `DBA` | Multiple dimensions from same origin |
| Continue | `DCO` | Chain of dimensions |
| Leader | `LE` | Notes, GD&T symbols |

### BIS Dimensioning Rules (SP:46):
1. **Dimensions are placed between the two views** (between front and top view) or outside the views.
2. **Aligned dimensions** for inclined features.
3. **Arrows** at both ends of dimension lines (or oblique strokes in older practice).
4. **Extension lines** start with a gap of **2 mm** from the object.
5. **Dimension text height** = 2.5 mm for visible, can be different for notes.

## 2.4 Layering Concepts — The Organization Engine

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Layer Definition (BIS-aligned):** A *layer* is a logical grouping of drawing entities that share common properties — **color, line type, and line weight**. Layers act like **transparent sheets** stacked on the drafting table, where each sheet contains one type of information.

### Standard Layer Convention for Machine Drawings:

| Layer Name | Color | Line Type | Line Weight | Contents |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `01_VISIBLE` | White/Black | Continuous | **0.7 mm** Thick | Visible edges, outlines |
| `02_HIDDEN` | Yellow | Dashed (Hidden) | **0.35 mm** Thin | Hidden edges of holes, internal features |
| `03_CENTER` | Red/Cyan | Center | **0.35 mm** Thin | Centerlines, axes of symmetry |
| `04_DIM` | Magenta/Green | Continuous | **0.35 mm** Thin | Dimension lines, extension lines, text |
| `05_HATCH` | Brown | Continuous | **0.35 mm** Thin | Section hatching, cut surfaces |
| `06_TEXT` | Green | Continuous | **0.35 mm** Thin | Notes, title block text |
| `07_BORDER` | White | Continuous | **0.5 mm** Medium | Sheet border, title block |
| `08_CONSTRUCT` | Gray | Continuous | **0.25 mm** Thin | Construction/reference lines |

### Layer Management Commands:
- **LAYER / LA** — Opens Layer Properties Manager
- **LAYOFF** — Turns off a layer (preserves data)
- **LAYON** — Turns on all layers
- **LAYISO** — Isolates a single layer
- **LAYLCK** — Locks a layer (visible but not selectable)
- **LAYULK** — Unlocks layers
- **LAYMRG** — Merges two layers

## 2.5 Matching (Properties Match / MATCHPROP / MA)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** The *Match Properties* command (`MA` or `MATCHPROP`) transfers the properties — **layer, color, linetype, lineweight, plot style, text style, dimension style** — from a *source object* to one or more *destination objects*. It is the CAD equivalent of "painting by sample."

**Workflow:**
1. Type `MA` → Enter
2. Select the **source object** (whose properties you want to copy)
3. Select the **destination objects** (one by one or via window selection)
4. Press Enter to complete

**KTU Application:** When drawing a section view, you select the visible edges on the front view as the source, then apply to all edges of the side view — ensuring uniform line weights across the entire drawing.

## 2.6 Detailing and Detailed Drawings

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Detailed Drawing Definition:** A *detailed drawing* (or *part drawing*) is a complete 2D representation of a **single machine component**, showing all views, sections, dimensions, tolerances, surface finish symbols, and a title block — sufficient for **manufacturing the part**.

**Components of a Complete Detailed Drawing:**
1. **Views** — Orthographic (Front, Top, Side) and/or Sectional
2. **Dimensions** — With tolerances where specified
3. **Sectional Hatching** — Indicating cut surfaces (angle = 45°, spacing = 2–3 mm)
4. **Surface Finish Symbols** — √, √√, etc., per BIS
5. **Geometric Tolerances** — GD&T (form, orientation, location, runout)
6. **Title Block** — Name, material, scale, drawn by, date, drawing number
7. **Notes** — General and specific manufacturing notes

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet — Dimensioning & Geometry Conversions

| Quantity | Formula / Rule | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Drawing Scale Factor | $S_f = \frac{\text{Dimension on drawing}}{\text{Actual size}}$ | dimensionless |
| Reduced Scale (1:2) | $L_{draw} = \frac{L_{actual}}{2}$ | mm |
| Enlarged Scale (2:1) | $L_{draw} = 2 \times L_{actual}$ | mm |
| Hatching Spacing | $d = 2 \text{ to } 3$ mm constant | mm |
| Dimension Text Height (KTU) | $h = 2.5$ mm standard | mm |
| Visible Line Weight | $w_{thick} = 0.7$ mm | mm |
| Thin Line Weight | $w_{thin} = 0.35$ mm | mm |
| Polygon (n-sided) circumscribed radius | $R = \frac{r}{\cos(\pi/n)}$ | mm |
| Bolt Circle Diameter | $D_{bc} = 1.5 \times d_{bolt}$ for $n=4$; $D_{bc} = 2.5 \times d_{bolt}$ for $n=6$ | mm |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Application:** The CAD operations covered in this experiment are the **foundation of digital manufacturing**. In industries such as **automotive (Tata Motors, Mahindra)**, **aerospace (HAL, ISRO)**, and **heavy machinery (L&T, BHEL)**, every part drawing is created using these exact commands, with **layering standards** that align with **ASME Y14.3** and **ISO 128**. A single error in dimensioning or layer assignment can lead to **manufacturing rework costing lakhs of rupees** and **weeks of delay**. Mastery of these commands is therefore not merely academic — it is a **pre-employment skill** for mechanical design engineers.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Step-by-Step: Drawing a Hexagonal Nut in CAD

To demonstrate the **integrated use** of Drawing, Editing, Dimensioning, and Layering commands, let us work through a complete KTU-style problem: *Draw the detailed drawing of an M20 hexagonal nut with detailed dimensions.*

### Given Specifications (BIS IS 1363):
- **Nominal diameter (d)** = 20 mm
- **Width across flats (s)** = 30 mm (for M20)
- **Width across corners (e)** ≈ 32.95 mm
- **Height (h)** = 16 mm
- **Thread pitch (p)** = 2.5 mm
- **Tapped hole diameter** = 18.376 mm (minor diameter, M20 coarse)

### Step 1: Calculate Derived Dimensions

**Step 1.1: Width across corners**

$$e = s \cdot \sec(30°) = \frac{s}{\cos(30°)}$$

$$e = \frac{30}{\cos(30°)} = \frac{30}{0.8660} = 34.64 \text{ mm}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The "width across corners" of a regular hexagon is the diameter of the circumscribed circle around the flat-to-flat polygon. Geometrically, the relation between inscribed and circumscribed radii for a regular n-gon is:
> $$R = \frac{r}{\cos(\pi/n)}$$
> For $n = 6$, $\cos(\pi/6) = \cos(30°) = \sqrt{3}/2 \approx 0.8660$.

**Step 1.2: Inscribed radius (apothem)**

$$r = \frac{s}{2} \cdot \tan(60°) = \frac{30}{2} \cdot 1.7321 = 25.98 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 1.3: Tapped hole minor diameter**

$$D_{minor} = d - 1.0825 \cdot p$$

$$D_{minor} = 20 - 1.0825 \times 2.5 = 20 - 2.706 = 17.294 \text{ mm}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The minor diameter of an ISO metric thread (external or internal) is derived from the fundamental triangle. The crest truncation for a 60° thread profile removes $H/8$ from the theoretical sharp crest, where $H = 0.866 \cdot p$. The result is the standard minor diameter formula.

### Step 2: Set Up the Layer System

| Step | Action | Command |
|---|---|---|
| 2.1 | Open Layer Properties Manager | `LA` |
| 2.2 | Create 6 layers | New Layer button |
| 2.3 | Assign colors per BIS convention | Click color swatch |
| 2.4 | Set line types (Continuous, Dashed, Center) | Load from acad.lin |
| 2.5 | Set line weights (0.7 mm, 0.35 mm) | Lineweight column |
| 2.6 | Make `01_VISIBLE` current | Double-click layer |

### Step 3: Draw the Front View (Sectional)

**Step 3.1: Draw the outer hexagon (top view)**

```
Command: POLYGON
Enter number of sides <4>: 6
Specify center of polygon: 0,0
Enter option [Inscribed in circle/Circumscribed about circle] <I>: I
Specify radius of circle: 17.32   (this is the inscribed radius from 1.2)
```

**Step 3.2: Draw the tapped hole (hidden lines in front view)**

```
Command: CIRCLE
Specify center point: 0,0
Specify radius: 8.647   (D_minor/2 from Step 1.3)
```

Switch to layer `02_HIDDEN` first.

**Step 3.3: Draw the front view rectangle (height = 16 mm)**

```
Command: PLINE
Specify start point: -15, 0
[Draw rectangle: 30 wide × 16 tall, 4 segments]
Specify next point: 15, 0
Specify next point: 15, 16
Specify next point: -15, 16
Specify next point: -15, 0
Close: Enter
```

**Step 3.4: Add the section cut**

- Draw a **centerline** through the front view (vertical, on layer `03_CENTER`).
- Apply **HATCH** command with **ANSI31 pattern**, scale = 2, angle = 0° (which renders as 45° in standard views).
- Hatch the cut area of the front view only (where material is "cut").

### Step 4: Apply Editing Commands

**Step 4.1: Mirror to create symmetry (if needed)**

```
Command: MIRROR
Select objects: (select the half-top view)
Specify first point of mirror line: 0,0
Specify second point of mirror line: 0,100
Delete source objects? [Yes/No] <N>: N
```

**Step 4.2: Fillet the corners (if chamfered)**

```
Command: FILLET
Select first object or [Undo/Polyline/Radius/Trim/Multiple]: R
Specify fillet radius: 1.5
Select first object: (click the corner edge)
Select second object: (click the adjacent edge)
```

**Step 4.3: Offset for parallel features**

```
Command: OFFSET
Specify offset distance: 2.5   (thread depth)
Select object to offset: (the major diameter circle)
Specify point on side to offset: (inside the hexagon)
```

### Step 5: Dimensioning (Switch to layer `04_DIM`)

| Dimension | Command | Value | Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| Width across flats (s) | `DLI` | 30 mm | Linear Horizontal |
| Height (h) | `DLI` | 16 mm | Linear Vertical |
| Thread M20 | Leader | "M20 × 2.5" | Leader Callout |
| Across corners (e) | `DAL` | 34.64 mm | Aligned |

**Step 5.1: Detailed dimensioning steps**

```
Command: DLI (Linear Dimension)
Specify first extension line origin: (left flat of hexagon)
Specify second extension line origin: (right flat of hexagon)
Specify dimension line location: (above the view)
Dimension text <30.00>: 30   (or press Enter to accept)
```

### Step 6: Add Title Block and Border

- Draw a **rectangle** of size **420 × 594 mm** (A2 sheet) on layer `07_BORDER`.
- Inside, create a **title block** of size 170 × 65 mm in the bottom-right corner.
- Use **TEXT** command with height = 5 mm for title, 3.5 mm for subtitle, 2.5 mm for small text.

## 3.2 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix — Command-to-Output Mapping

| Desired Output Feature | Primary Drawing Cmd | Primary Editing Cmd | Dimensioning Cmd | Layer Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Outer hexagonal profile | POLYGON | TRIM (cleanup) | DAL (across corners) | 01_VISIBLE |
| Tapped hole (front view, hidden) | CIRCLE | OFFSET (thread depth) | DDI (Ø) | 02_HIDDEN |
| Sectional hatching | BHATCH | HATCHEDIT | — | 05_HATCH |
| Centerline of bolt | LINE | — | — | 03_CENTER |
| Fillet radius (corner) | FILLET | — | DRA (R) | 01_VISIBLE |
| Chamfered edge | CHAMFER | TRIM | DAN (45°×1.5) | 01_VISIBLE |
| Bolt circle (PCD) | CIRCLE + OFFSET | — | DLI | 01_VISIBLE + 03_CENTER |
| Bolt holes (4× or 6× pattern) | CIRCLE | ARRAY (Polar) | DDI | 02_HIDDEN |
| Surface finish symbol | INSERT (Block) | — | — | 06_TEXT |
| Title block | LINE + PLINE | COPY, MOVE | — | 07_BORDER + 06_TEXT |
| Notes / tolerances | MTEXT | — | — | 06_TEXT |

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation: Plotting Scale Calculation

When a machine drawing is plotted from CAD to a physical sheet, the **plot scale** must compensate for sheet size and intended output reduction.

$$S_{plot} = \frac{L_{paper}}{L_{model} \times R_{viewport}}$$

Where:
- $L_{paper}$ = Length on the plotted paper (mm)
- $L_{model}$ = Length of the object in the CAD model space (mm, typically 1:1)
- $R_{viewport}$ = Viewport zoom scale factor (e.g., 0.5 for 1:2 reduction)

**Example:** To plot a **shaft of 200 mm length** at a 1:2 scale on **A2 paper** (594 mm):

$$S_{plot} = \frac{L_{paper}}{L_{model}} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5$$

So the shaft appears as 100 mm on the plotted paper. The viewport scale is set to **1:2** using the **Zoom → XP** command in the layout tab.

**Step-by-step AutoCAD Plot Workflow:**

1. **Step 1:** Switch to **Layout1** tab.
2. **Step 2:** Delete default viewport, insert a new viewport using **MV** command.
3. **Step 3:** Set viewport scale: `Z (Zoom) → 1/2XP` for 1:2.
4. **Step 4:** Type `PLOT` → select printer (e.g., DWG to PDF.pc3).
5. **Step 5:** Choose paper size (A2 = 420 × 594 mm).
6. **Step 6:** Set plot area = "Layout".
7. **Step 7:** Set plot scale = 1:2 (or 0.5).
8. **Step 8:** Set plot offset = Centered.
9. **Step 9:** Check "Plot with lineweights" → Yes.
10. **Step 10:** Click "Preview" → "Plot" to output.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define "Layer" in CAD and explain its significance in machine drawing as per BIS conventions. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> A **layer** in CAD is a logical grouping of drawing entities that share common properties such as **color, line type, and line weight**, analogous to transparent overlay sheets stacked on a drafting table. In machine drawing per **BIS SP:46**, layering is significant because it enables: (i) **organized drafting** — separate layers for visible edges, hidden lines, centerlines, dimensions, and hatching; (ii) **controlled visibility** — layers can be turned off/on to declutter complex assembly drawings; (iii) **standardized line weights** — automatic enforcement of 0.7 mm thick for visible and 0.35 mm thin for hidden/dimension lines; (iv) **easy editing** — selecting all items of a category (e.g., all dimensions) by selecting the layer; and (v) **plotting control** — selective plotting of layers.
> *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for analogy, 1 mark for significance)*

### Question 2: List any six editing commands used in CAD and state one specific application of each. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> Six editing commands and applications:
> 1. **TRIM (TR)** — Cutting excess line work at intersection of flange and shaft steps.
> 2. **OFFSET (O)** — Creating concentric circles for bolt-hole patterns and thread depth.
> 3. **MIRROR (MI)** — Generating the symmetric half of a flange coupling in a single operation.
> 4. **ARRAY (AR)** — Polar-arraying 6 bolt holes around a PCD (Pitch Circle Diameter).
> 5. **FILLET (F)** — Creating the rounded corner radius of a bearing housing.
> 6. **CHAMFER (CHA)** — Producing a 45° × 1.5 mm chamfer on a shaft end.
> *(0.5 mark per command — 3 marks total)*
