## 5.2 PART B — Full ESE Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks. **Internal choice** is mandatory — students attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A: [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]

**Draw the detailed drawing of a M20 × 2.5 Hexagonal Nut as per BIS SP:46 standards. The drawing must include:**
- (a) **All three views** (top view as hexagon, front view as sectional, side view) with proper line types and weights. (7 Marks)
- (b) **Complete dimensioning, section hatching, title block, and a thread callout** using a leader note. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution (a) — Views and Geometry (7 Marks)

**Given:** M20 hexagonal nut, thread pitch 2.5 mm.

**Calculations:**

$$s = 30 \text{ mm (width across flats)}$$

$$e = \frac{s}{\cos(30°)} = \frac{30}{0.8660} = 34.64 \text{ mm (across corners)}$$

$$h = 16 \text{ mm (height)}$$

$$d_{hole} = d - 1.0825 \cdot p = 20 - 1.0825(2.5) = 17.29 \text{ mm}$$

$$r_{hole} = \frac{17.29}{2} = 8.65 \text{ mm}$$

**Commands Executed:**

1. **Layer setup:** Create 6 layers (`01_VISIBLE`, `02_HIDDEN`, `03_CENTER`, `04_DIM`, `05_HATCH`, `07_BORDER`).
2. **Top view:** `POLYGON` command with 6 sides, inscribed in circle of radius **17.32 mm** (= 30/2 × tan 60° = 25.98... wait, recalculation: for inscribed circle radius of hexagon = $s/(2\tan 30°) = 30/(2 \times 0.5774) = 25.98$ mm. For circumscribed circle (corner-to-corner) = $s/(2\cos 30°) = 17.32$ mm).
3. **Front view (sectional):** Use `PLINE` to draw a rectangle of 30 × 16 mm. Apply `BHATCH` with `ANSI31` pattern, scale = 1.5, angle = 0°.
4. **Tapped hole (hidden):** On layer `02_HIDDEN`, draw two concentric circles — Ø17.29 (minor) and Ø20 (major, taps) using `CIRCLE` command.
5. **Centerlines:** Use `LINE` on layer `03_CENTER` with linetype `CENTER`.
6. **Hidden hole projection lines:** `OFFSET` the centerline by 8.65 mm, change to layer `02_HIDDEN` with linetype `HIDDEN`.

**Mark Distribution for (a):**
- Correct hexagonal geometry: 2 marks
- Front view sectional with hatching: 2 marks
- Hidden line representation: 1.5 marks
- Centerlines and projection: 1.5 marks

#### Model Solution (b) — Dimensioning, Title Block, and Callouts (7 Marks)

**Commands Executed:**

1. **Dimension width across flats (s = 30 mm):**
   - `DLI` → click left flat → click right flat → place above view → text auto: "30".
2. **Dimension across corners (e = 34.64 mm):**
   - `DAL` → click top-left corner → click bottom-right corner → place at 30° offset → text: "34.64".
3. **Dimension height (h = 16 mm):**
   - `DLI` (vertical) → click top edge → click bottom edge → place to the right.
4. **Thread callout (M20 × 2.5):**
   - `LE` (Leader) → start at threaded hole → place text "M20 × 2.5 DEPTH 16" with M-text style.
5. **Title block (bottom-right corner):**
   - Draw rectangle 170 × 65 mm with `PLINE`.
   - Divide into rows with `LINE` and `OFFSET`.
   - Add text using `MTEXT`: Title (5 mm height) = "HEXAGONAL NUT M20"; Material (3.5 mm) = "MS Grade 4"; Scale = "1:1"; Drawn by, Date, Drawing No.
6. **BIS border:** Outer rectangle of A2 size 420 × 594 mm on layer `07_BORDER`.

**Mark Distribution for (b):**
- Linear and aligned dimensions: 2 marks
- Thread callout and leader: 1.5 marks
- Section hatching: 1 mark
- Title block and border: 1.5 marks
- Overall neatness and BIS compliance: 1 mark

---

### Question B: [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam - May 2024] (Alternative Choice)

**Draw the detailed drawing of a Flange Coupling showing the front view (half in section) and side view, with complete dimensioning per BIS standards. The flange has 4 bolt holes on a PCD of 80 mm. The shaft diameter is 40 mm. (a) Views with sectioning (7 Marks), (b) Dimensioning, hatching, and title block (7 Marks).**

#### Model Solution (a) — Views (7 Marks)

**Calculations:**

$$D_{flange} = 3 \times d_{shaft} = 3 \times 40 = 120 \text{ mm (typical proportion)}$$

$$D_{PCD} = 80 \text{ mm (given)}$$

$$d_{bolt} = 0.5 \times d_{shaft} = 20 \text{ mm (M20 bolts)}$$

$$d_{bolt\text{-}hole} = 21 \text{ mm (clearance hole)}$$

$$T_{flange} = 0.5 \times d_{shaft} = 20 \text{ mm (thickness)}$$

$$H_{hub} = 0.75 \times d_{shaft} = 30 \text{ mm}$$

$$D_{hub} = 1.5 \times d_{shaft} = 60 \text{ mm}$$

**Commands Executed:**

1. **Setup layers** as per Section 2.4.
2. **Front view (half-section):** Draw a `PLINE` closed profile representing half the flange (one side in section).
3. **Side view:** Draw concentric circles using `CIRCLE`:
   - Outer flange: Ø120
   - PCD: Ø80 (centerline)
   - Hub: Ø60
   - Shaft: Ø40
   - 4 bolt holes: Ø21, at 0°, 90°, 180°, 270° on PCD
4. **Polar Array** for bolt holes: `AR` → Polar → 4 items → center at (0,0) → select bolt circle.
5. **Hatch the sectioned half:** `BHATCH` → ANSI31 → scale 2 → angle 0° → apply to the front view half.
6. **Keyway (optional):** `RECTANGLE` of 12 × 4 mm on the shaft, then `SUBTRACT`-equivalent via `TRIM`.

#### Model Solution (b) — Dimensioning, Hatching, Title Block (7 Marks)

1. **Diametric dimension of flange (Ø120):** `DDI` on outer circle with text prefix "Ø".
2. **Linear dimension of flange thickness (20 mm):** `DLI` vertical.
3. **PCD dimension (Ø80):** `DLE` (Diameter Dimension Leader) with text "PCD Ø80".
4. **Bolt size (4 × Ø21):** Leader note "4 HOLES Ø21 EQUALLY SPACED".
5. **Shaft Ø40:** `DDI` on shaft circle.
6. **Hatching:** `HATCHEDIT` to adjust pattern density.
7. **Title Block:** Name = "FLANGE COUPLING", Material = "CAST IRON FG 200", Scale = "1:2", Quantity = "1".

**Mark Distribution for (b):**
- Diametric and linear dimensions: 2 marks
- PCD and bolt pattern callouts: 1.5 marks
- Hatching and section symbols: 1.5 marks
- Title block with all required fields: 2 marks

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Line Type Confusion (–2 marks):** Students often draw *all* lines as continuous (solid), ignoring the difference between **visible (continuous thick)**, **hidden (dashed thin)**, and **center (long-short-long thin)** lines. Use `LINETYPE` command and the LAYER system — **never** skip this.
> 2. **Dimension Placement Violation (–1.5 marks):** Dimensions must be placed **between views** (front and top) per BIS SP:46, **not** inside the views or floating randomly. Repeated dimensions on the same feature are penalized.
> 3. **Missing Arrowheads or Extension Gaps (–1 mark):** Arrows must touch the extension lines, and there must be a **2 mm gap** between the object and the start of the extension line. Using `DIME` settings in AutoCAD (DIMEXO = 2) is essential.
> 4. **Forgetting the Section Plane Line (–1.5 marks):** When drawing a sectional view, the **section plane (cutting plane line A-A)** with arrows showing the viewing direction must be drawn on the parent view, or marks are deducted.
> 5. **Incorrect Hatching (–1 mark):** Hatching should be at **45°**, with **2–3 mm spacing**, and the same component in **different sections** must use **the same hatch pattern and direction**. Different components in the same section must be hatched in **opposite directions (45° and 135°)** or with different spacing.
> 6. **Missing Title Block Fields (–2 marks):** Every detailed drawing must have: **Title, Drawing Number, Scale, Drawn By, Checked By, Approved By, Date, Material, Quantity, Sheet Size, Projection Symbol (1st or 3rd angle)**. Missing 3+ fields = full marks deduction on title block.
> 7. **Plotting on Wrong Sheet Size (–1 mark):** If the problem says "A2 sheet" and the student plots on A3 or A4, marks are deducted.
> 8. **Not Using Layers (–2 marks):** Examiner checks the Layer Properties Manager. If everything is on Layer 0 (default), **2 marks are deducted** as it shows the student does not understand the organization principle.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU PCBRL307 Module Experiment 2:**

### A. Drawing Commands (Must Know)
- LINE, CIRCLE (6 sub-options), ARC (11 sub-options), PLINE, RECTANGLE, POLYGON, ELLIPSE, SPLINE, POINT, XLINE, RAY, MLINE
- **Ortho Mode (F8)** for horizontal/vertical constraints
- **Polar Tracking** for inclined lines at standard angles (30°, 45°, 60°)

### B. Editing Commands (Must Know)
- Core: **M, CO, RO, MI, O, TR, EX, F, CHA, AR, S, SC, BR, E**
- **Fillet radius** for machine components is typically **1, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 5 mm**
- **Chamfer sizes** for shafts: 1×1, 1.5×1.5, 2×2 mm
- **Array patterns** for bolt circles: **4 bolts (90°), 6 bolts (60°), 8 bolts (45°), 12 bolts (30°)**

### C. Dimensioning (BIS SP:46 Critical Rules)
- Text height = **2.5 mm**; Arrow size = **2.5 mm**; Extension line gap = **2 mm**
- Always dimension between views; never inside the view
- Ø symbol for diameters, R for radii, ⌴ for squares, ∠ for angles
- Leader callouts for threads, surface finish, materials

### D. Layering
- Minimum 6 layers: VISIBLE, HIDDEN, CENTER, DIM, HATCH, BORDER
- Visible = thick (0.7 mm); All others = thin (0.35 mm)
- **Color coding** is industry standard but not mandatory if line types are correct
- Always **lock** the BORDER layer before editing to prevent accidental modification

### E. Matching (MATCHPROP / MA)
- Use to maintain uniform line weights and styles
- Source first, then destination
- Press **S** inside the MA command to choose specific properties (e.g., only color, only linetype)

### F. Detailing & Detailed Drawings
- **Detailed drawing** = single part drawing
- **Assembly drawing** = shows how parts fit together
- Always include: Views, Dimensions, Section (if internal features), Hatching, Title block, Notes

### G. Plotting
- **Model space** = 1:1 drawing; **Layout** = paper space with viewports
- Plot scale = Viewport scale × Paper/Model conversion
- For A2 sheet at 1:2: viewport scale = 1:2
- Always enable **"Plot with lineweights"** to get correct thick/thin distinction

### H. KTU-Specific Tips for Exam
- Practice drawing: **Hexagonal nut, Flange coupling, Shaft, Bearing block, Plummer block, Knuckle joint, Screw jack, Coupling (protected/unprotected)**
- Memorize standard proportions (e.g., flange diameter = 3 × shaft diameter)
- Use **PLINE** for all closed boundaries — easier to hatch and dimension
- Set up the layer system **before** starting the drawing — saves time
- Use **BLOCK** and **INSERT** for repeated symbols (surface finish, weld symbols)

### I. Formulae to Memorize
- Across corners of hexagon: $e = s / \cos(30°)$
- Inscribed radius: $r = s / (2 \tan(30°))$
- Thread minor diameter: $D_{minor} = d - 1.0825 p$
- Hatching spacing: 2–3 mm
- Plot scale: $S_{plot} = L_{paper} / L_{model}$

> **END OF MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 NOTES**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on 3D Solid Modelling: Plummer Lock, Bearings, Guideways, Generating 2D from 3D
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRL307 - MACHINE DRAWING AND SOLID MODELLING
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on 3D Solid Modelling: Plummer Lock, Bearings, Guideways, Generating 2D from 3D

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of 3D Solid Modelling

**3D Solid Modelling** is a computer-aided design (CAD) technique that represents three-dimensional objects as solid volumes with well-defined mass, volume, surface area, centroid, and moments of inertia. Unlike wireframe or surface modelling, solid models are **informationally complete** — every geometric and topological property of the part is unambiguously known. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, exercises on Plummer Blocks, Bearings, and Guideways demand the creation of solid models using parametric feature-based modellers such as **SolidWorks, CATIA, NX, Creo (formerly Pro-E), or Autodesk Inventor**.

According to the **KTU 2024 syllabus for PCBRL307**, Module Experiment 2 expects the student to:
1. Construct solid models of standardised machine components (Plummer Block, Pedestal Bearing, Sliding/Guideway).
2. Apply Boolean operations (Union, Subtract, Intersect).
3. Apply feature operations (Extrude, Revolve, Sweep, Loft, Fillet, Chamfer).
4. Generate **2D orthographic/multiview drawings** automatically from the 3D solid model (a process called *Drawing Extraction* or *View Projection from 3D*).

> [!NOTE]
> **Solid vs Surface vs Wireframe — KTU Standard Distinction**
> - **Wireframe:** Only edges and vertices. Ambiguous (hidden line ambiguity). Not acceptable for KTU 3D modelling exercises.
> - **Surface:** Skin of zero thickness. No volume information.
> - **Solid:** Enclosed volume. Mass properties calculable. **Mandatory for KTU PCBRL307 experiments.**

## 1.2 Component-Wise Definition

### A) Plummer Block (Pillow Block)

A **Plummer Block** (also called a *Pillow Block Bearing Housing* or *Pedestal Bearing Housing*) is a fixed, self-contained bearing housing used to support a rotating shaft at intermediate points along its length. It typically encloses a **journal bearing (plain/sliding bearing)** or a **ball/roller bearing insert** inside a cast iron or fabricated steel body. It consists of a **base (pedestal)**, a **cap (top)**, **bolts**, **cap-screws/holding-down bolts**, **lubrication provisions (oil holes, grease nipples)**, and a **bearing liner/bush**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Terminology — "Plummer Lock":** In British Engineering convention (widely followed in KTU textbooks such as *N.D. Bhatt* and *P.S.G. Tech*), "Plummer Block" and "Plummer Lock" are sometimes used interchangeably. Strictly speaking, a *Plummer Lock* is the **split-bearing assembly with cap and adjusting screws**, while the entire housing assembly is the *Plummer Block*. For KTU purposes, treat them as synonymous.

### B) Bearings (Journal/Plain & Rolling Element)

A **Bearing** is a machine element that constrains relative motion between two parts to a desired type (rotational or linear) and **reduces friction** between moving surfaces. Two broad categories studied in KTU PCBRL307 are:

- **Sliding/Plain Bearings:** Two surfaces slide against each other (e.g., the shaft journal sliding inside the bush). Lubricated by oil/grease. Self-lubricating PTFE-based bearings also exist.
- **Rolling Element Bearings:** Balls or rollers carry the load. Lower starting torque. Sub-categorised as **Deep Groove Ball Bearings, Cylindrical Roller Bearings, Tapered Roller Bearings, Self-Aligning Bearings, Thrust Bearings, etc.**

### C) Guideways

**Guideways** are precision-machined surfaces (or pairs of surfaces) on a machine tool that **guide and constrain the linear motion of a moving slide, carriage, or table**. They must possess high **wear resistance**, **damping capacity**, and **geometric accuracy**. Common types studied in KTU:
- **Slide Guideways** — flat (planar) V or rectangular.
- **Sliding Guideways with liners** — PTFE (Teflon) strips or Turcite for stick-slip-free motion.
- **Rolling Element Guideways** — recirculating ball/roller bearing rails (e.g., Hiwin, THK, SKF).
- **Hydrostatic/Hydrodynamic Guideways** — pressurized oil film.

### D) Generating 2D from 3D (Drawing Extraction)

This is the process of **automatically projecting orthographic/multiview drawings (front, top, side, isometric, section views) from the 3D solid model** using the CAD software's drawing workbench. The software calculates projection planes, applies visibility logic (visible/hidden line removal), and produces **engineering drawings conforming to SP 46 (Bureau of Indian Standards)** or **ISO 128**.

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine you have a **chocolate truffle**. A wireframe model would be like drawing only the **silhouette and seams** with a pen — you know where the chocolate *might* be, but you can't tell if the inside is solid or hollow. A surface model is the **outer chocolate shell** — you see the boundary but have no idea about the truffle's volume or whether the filling has been poured in. A **solid model is the whole truffle** — chocolate shell *and* filling — you can compute its weight, slice it to see fillings, and 3D-print an exact replica.

Similarly, for a Plummer Block:
- The **base, cap, and bush** are features you *add* (Extrude, Revolve).
- The **bolt holes and oil hole** are features you *cut out* (Extrude-Cut, Revolve-Cut).
- When you slice the solid in your mind (section view), you *see* the internal bush and shaft.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Intuition for Bearing & Guideway:** A bearing is to a rotating shaft what a **caster wheel is to a shopping trolley** — it lets heavy loads move with minimal effort. A guideway is to a machine tool slide what a **railway track is to a train** — it constrains motion to a single straight line with high precision.

## 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

The following standard references must be memorised for KTU PCBRL307:

| Metric | Value | Used For |
|---|---|---|
| Density of Cast Iron | **7.2 g/cm³** | Plummer Block mass estimation |
| Density of Steel (Bearing Steel) | **7.85 g/cm³** | Shaft, bolts, inner/outer races |
| Young's Modulus, Grey Cast Iron | **100 – 150 GPa** | Housing stiffness check |
| Young's Modulus, Bearing Steel (AISI 52100) | **210 GPa** | Bearing rings |
| Standard Surface Roughness for sliding journal | **Ra 0.8 µm – Ra 1.6 µm** | Bearing liner finish |
| Standard Surface Roughness for rolling contact | **Ra 0.1 µm – Ra 0.4 µm** | Raceway finish |
| Standard fits for bearing on shaft | **k5 or m6 (inner ring)** | ISO 286 |
| Standard fits for bearing in housing | **H7 or J7 (outer ring)** | ISO 286 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of 3D Solid Modelling

Solid modelling rests on three mathematical foundations:

1. **Constructive Solid Geometry (CSG):** Combines *primitives* (box, cylinder, sphere, cone, torus) using Boolean operators (Union $\cup$, Difference $-$, Intersection $\cap$).
2. **Boundary Representation (B-Rep):** Stores topology (vertices, edges, faces, shells, loops) and geometry (curves, surfaces). Used by **SolidWorks, CATIA, Creo**.
3. **Sweep / Feature-based Parametric Modelling:** Most modern CAD systems use a hybrid. The user creates a 2D *sketch*, then applies a *feature* (Extrude, Revolve, Sweep, Loft) to generate 3D geometry. Parameters drive dimensions parametrically.

### The KTU-Standard Modelling Workflow

For every exercise in this module, follow this **7-step procedure**:

1. **Sketching:** Create 2D profiles on selected planes (Front, Top, Right, or custom).
2. **Dimensioning & Constraining:** Apply *geometric constraints* (Horizontal, Vertical, Tangent, Coincident, Equal, Symmetric) and *dimensional constraints* (linear, angular, radial).
3. **Feature Creation:** Apply Extrude Boss/Extrude Cut, Revolve Boss/Revolve Cut, Sweep, Loft.
4. **Modification Features:** Add Fillets (rounded edges), Chamfers (angled edges), Shells (hollow out), Draft (taper for casting).
5. **Pattern Operations:** Apply Linear Pattern, Circular Pattern, Mirror, or Sketch-driven Pattern to repeat features.
6. **Assembly (if multi-part):** Apply Mates (Coincident, Concentric, Distance, Angle, Tangent).
7. **Drawing Extraction:** Switch to Drawing Workbench → Insert Standard Views → Project → Add Dimensions → Export.

## 2.2 Deep Analysis — Plummer Block Construction

A typical Plummer Block consists of the following features (refer to Figure in SECTION 4):

- **Pedestal Base:** A rectangular block with through-holes for foundation bolts (foundation bolts M16 to M24 typically).
- **Cap:** A semi-cylindrical or semi-prismatic top that houses the bearing. Fastened to the base by **two cap bolts** (M12–M20) on either side.
- **Bearing Bush:** A cylindrical liner (often split) of bronze or white metal (Babbitt). Inner diameter matches the shaft diameter ($D_{bush\_inner} = D_{shaft}$).
- **Lubrication:** An oil hole drilled through the cap, terminating at the bearing surface. Sometimes a grease nipple (zerk fitting) is provided.
- **Holding-Down Bolts:** Two foundation bolts (M16 to M30) fasten the entire Plummer Block to the machine bed/foundation.

### Standard Proportions (KTU Reference — *N.D. Bhatt / PSG Design Data Book*)

Let **$D$** be the nominal shaft diameter.

| Parameter | Empirical Proportion | Engineering Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Centre height of shaft, **$H_c$** | $1.5 D$ to $2.0 D$ | Rigidity against bending moment |
| Total height, **$H_t$** | $3.0 D$ to $4.0 D$ | Cap + base thickness |
| Length of base, **$L_b$** | $3.0 D$ to $4.0 D$ | Foundation bolt spacing |
| Width of base, **$W_b$** | $2.0 D$ to $3.0 D$ | Lateral stability |
| Cap bolt centre distance, **$L_c$** | $1.5 D$ to $2.0 D$ | Tightening torque distribution |
| Cap bolt size, **$M_{cb}$** | $\approx 0.5 D$ (round up to standard) | M12 for D ≤ 25, M16 for D ≤ 40, M20 for D ≤ 60 |
| Bush wall thickness, **$t_b$** | $0.08 D$ to $0.12 D$ | Heat dissipation, strength |
| Bush length, **$L_{bush}$** | $1.0 D$ to $1.5 D$ | $L/D$ ratio for journal bearing |
| Oil hole diameter | $d_o \approx 0.05 D$ to $0.10 D$ | Capillary oil flow |

## 2.3 Deep Analysis — Bearing Modelling

For KTU purposes, two bearing types must be modelled:

### A) Sliding Journal Bearing (Plain Bearing inside a Plummer Block)

Key design parameters (Petroff / Raimondi-Boyd relations):

$$\text{Sommerfeld Number, } S = \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{\mu \, N}{P}$$

where:
- $r$ = journal radius
- $c$ = radial clearance ($c = D_{bush} - D_{shaft}$ over $2$)
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of lubricant
- $N$ = rotational speed in **rps**
- $P$ = bearing pressure (load per projected area)

**Coefficient of friction (Petroff's equation, lightly loaded assumption):**

$$f = 2\pi^2 \cdot \frac{\mu \, N}{P} \cdot \frac{r}{c}$$

### B) Rolling Element Bearing Life (ISO 281:2007 / KTU Standard)

The **basic rating life** (in millions of revolutions) of a rolling element bearing is:

$$L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p$$

where:
- $C$ = **Basic Dynamic Load Rating (N)** — supplied by manufacturer (e.g., SKF, FAG)
- $P$ = **Equivalent Dynamic Load (N)** acting on the bearing
- $p$ = **3** for ball bearings, **10/3** for roller bearings

**Converted to operating hours:**

$$L_{10h} = \frac{10^6}{60 \cdot n} \cdot \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p$$

where $n$ = rotational speed in **rpm**.

### Equivalent Dynamic Load (most common case: radial + axial combined load)

$$P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$$

where $X$ = radial factor, $Y$ = axial factor (lookup tables from manufacturer based on $F_a / F_r$ ratio and $e$).

## 2.4 Deep Analysis — Guideway Design Principles

A guideway must satisfy **four engineering functions simultaneously**:
1. **Locate** the slide accurately (geometric accuracy).
2. **Support** the load without deformation (stiffness).
3. **Guide** motion along the intended axis with minimum friction.
4. **Damp** vibrations (damping capacity).

For **sliding guideways**, the *stick-slip phenomenon* must be avoided — this requires a low and stable coefficient of friction. The empirical rule:

$$\mu_{static} \leq \mu_{kinetic}$$

**Static friction coefficient for cast-iron on cast-iron (lubricated):** $\mu_s \approx 0.05$ to $0.10$.
**Static friction coefficient for PTFE-Turcite on cast-iron:** $\mu_s \approx 0.02$ to $0.04$.

For **rolling guideways**, the load-deflection is governed by Hertzian contact theory. The Hertz contact stress between a ball of radius $R$ and a flat raceway under normal load $Q$ is:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{1}{\pi} \cdot \sqrt[3]{6 \cdot Q^2 \cdot k^2 \cdot E^{*2} \cdot \frac{1}{R^2}}$$

where $E^{*}$ is the effective elastic modulus and $k$ is the contact geometry factor (1 for sphere-on-flat).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use $\vert$ (written as `\vert`) for absolute values to avoid breaking the markdown table structure.

| Formula | Expression | Application / KTU Use |
|---|---|---|
| Shaft Diameter from Empirical Design | $D = K \cdot \sqrt[3]{P \cdot kW}$ | Initial shaft sizing |
| Basic Rating Life (rolling bearing) | $L_{10} = \left(C \vert P^{-1}\right)^p$ | Bearing life in millions of revs |
| Bearing Life in Hours | $L_{10h} = \frac{10^6}{60 n} \cdot \left(C \vert P^{-1}\right)^p$ | KTU standard for life calculation |
| Equivalent Dynamic Load | $P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$ | Combined radial + axial loads |
| Sommerfeld Number | $S = (r/c)^2 \cdot (\mu N / P)$ | Journal bearing characterisation |
| Petroff Friction Coefficient | $f = 2\pi^2 \cdot (\mu N / P) \cdot (r/c)$ | Lightly loaded plain bearing |
| Hertz Contact Stress | $\sigma_{max} = \frac{1}{\pi}\sqrt[3]{6 Q^2 k^2 E^{*2} R^{-2}}$ | Rolling contact design |
| Clearance Ratio (Journal) | $\psi = c/r$ | Typical values $0.001$ to $0.002$ |
| Standard Fits (Bearing inner ring on shaft) | $k_5$, $m_6$, $n_6$ | ISO 286 |
| Standard Fits (Bearing outer ring in housing) | $H_7$, $J_7$, $K_7$ | ISO 286 |
| Plummer Block Centre Height | $H_c = (1.5 \text{ to } 2.0) \cdot D$ | Empirical proportion |
| Plummer Block Base Length | $L_b = (3.0 \text{ to } 4.0) \cdot D$ | Empirical proportion |
| Plummer Block Bush Wall Thickness | $t_b = (0.08 \text{ to } 0.12) \cdot D$ | Empirical proportion |
| Engineering Drawing Scale | $1:1$, $1:2$, $1:5$, $1:10$ | SP 46 / ISO 5455 |
| Linear Tolerance Grade (IT) | IT6 to IT11 (general) | ISO 286 |

## 2.6 Engineering Utility in Industry

Solid modelling of machine components is the **digital backbone** of modern manufacturing. In industries such as automotive, aerospace, heavy machinery (L&T, Tata Motors, BHEL) and CNC machine tool building (HMT, Ace, BFW), the engineer creates a 3D solid model first, then automatically extracts:
- **2D engineering drawings** for shop-floor manufacturing
- **STL/STEP files** for 3D printing or CNC machining
- **FEA inputs** for stress/thermal analysis (ANSYS, HyperWorks)
- **Animation files** for kinematic simulation

The Plummer Block, being a *canonical machine design example*, teaches the student to handle **boolean subtraction** (drilling holes), **revolve features** (cylindrical bush), **split bodies** (cap-base), and **patterns** (bolt holes) — all essential CAD skills.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Empirical Standard Proportions of a Plummer Block

**Given:** Shaft diameter $D = 60$ mm (a typical medium-duty application).
**Find:** All standard dimensions of the Plummer Block using empirical proportions.

**Step 1 — Centre Height of Shaft**

$$H_c = 1.75 \cdot D = 1.75 \times 60 = 105 \text{ mm}$$

Explanation: The centre of the shaft should be $1.5D$ to $2.0D$ above the bed for adequate rigidity. Choose $1.75D$ as a balanced design point.

**Step 2 — Base Length**

$$L_b = 3.5 \cdot D = 3.5 \times 60 = 210 \text{ mm}$$

Explanation: Sufficient base length accommodates two foundation bolts ($M_{fb} \approx 0.4D \approx M24$) with adequate edge distance ($\geq 1.5 d_{bolt}$).

**Step 3 — Base Width**

$$W_b = 2.5 \cdot D = 2.5 \times 60 = 150 \text{ mm}$$

Explanation: Wider than the cap for lateral stability.

**Step 4 — Cap Bolt Centre Distance**

$$L_c = 1.75 \cdot D = 1.75 \times 60 = 105 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Cap Bolt Size**

$$M_{cb} = 0.5 \cdot D = 0.5 \times 60 = 30$$

Round to nearest standard ISO metric bolt: **M30** (acceptable; could also use M27 for cost saving).

**Step 6 — Bush Wall Thickness**

$$t_b = 0.10 \cdot D = 0.10 \times 60 = 6 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 7 — Bush Outer Diameter**

$$D_{bush,outer} = D + 2 t_b = 60 + 12 = 72 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 8 — Bush Length**

$$L_{bush} = 1.25 \cdot D = 1.25 \times 60 = 75 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 9 — Total Plummer Block Height**

$$H_t = H_c + 0.5 \cdot D_{bush,outer} + t_{cap} + t_{base}$$

Taking $t_{cap} = t_{base} = 30$ mm each:

$$H_t = 105 + 36 + 30 + 30 = 201 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 10 — Oil Hole Diameter**

$$d_o = 0.07 \cdot D = 0.07 \times 60 = 4.2 \text{ mm}$$

Round to standard drill size: **$\phi 5$ mm**.

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — Rolling Element Bearing Life

**Problem:** A deep groove ball bearing is to operate on a shaft at $n = 1450$ rpm. The radial load is $F_r = 4000$ N, axial load is negligible. The chosen bearing has a basic dynamic load rating $C = 25000$ N. Find the basic rating life $L_{10}$ (in hours) and check if it is suitable for an application requiring $L_{10h} \geq 20000$ hours.

**Given:**
- $n = 1450$ rpm
- $F_r = 4000$ N
- $F_a = 0$ (pure radial)
- $C = 25000$ N
- $p = 3$ (ball bearing)

**Step 1 — Equivalent Dynamic Load (pure radial)**

Since $F_a = 0$, the manufacturer tables give $X = 1, Y = 0$:

$$P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a = 1 \cdot 4000 + 0 \cdot 0 = 4000 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Basic Rating Life (millions of revolutions)**

$$L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p = \left(\frac{25000}{4000}\right)^3 = (6.25)^3$$

$$L_{10} = 6.25 \times 6.25 \times 6.25$$

$$L_{10} = 39.0625 \times 6.25$$

$$L_{10} = 244.140625 \text{ million revolutions}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Operating Hours**

$$L_{10h} = \frac{10^6}{60 \cdot n} \cdot L_{10} = \frac{10^6}{60 \times 1450} \cdot 244.140625$$

$$L_{10h} = \frac{10^6}{87000} \cdot 244.140625$$

$$L_{10h} = 11.4943 \cdot 244.140625$$

$$L_{10h} \approx 2805 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 4 — Verdict**

Since $L_{10h} \approx 2805$ hours $\ll 20000$ hours required, the bearing **fails** the design criterion. The student must either:
- (a) Choose a bearing with a higher $C$ rating, or
- (b) Reduce the applied load.

**Step 5 — Required C for desired life**

$$L_{10h} = 20000 \text{ hrs}, \quad L_{10,req} = \frac{60 \cdot n \cdot L_{10h}}{10^6} = \frac{60 \times 1450 \times 20000}{10^6}$$

$$L_{10,req} = \frac{1.74 \times 10^9}{10^6} = 1740 \text{ million revs}$$

$$C_{req} = P \cdot \sqrt[3]{L_{10,req}} = 4000 \cdot \sqrt[3]{1740}$$

$$C_{req} = 4000 \times 12.04 \approx 48160 \text{ N}$$

So a bearing with $C \geq 48160$ N is required. The next standard bearing (e.g., SKF 6312 has $C = 55{,}300$ N) would be selected.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Component Feature-to-Operation Mapping

This matrix tells you **which CAD feature to use** when modelling each part:

| Component Feature | 3D Feature Operation | Required Boolean Step | Mermaid ID (Ref) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pedestal Base block | Extrude Boss | None | baseA |
| Bearing Bush (cylindrical liner) | Revolve Boss | Union with base (cylindrical boss) | bushB |
| Bore for bush | Extrude Cut or Revolve Cut | Subtract | boreC |
| Cap | Extrude Boss (split horizontally) | Union | capD |
| Foundation Bolt Holes (2) | Extrude Cut + Linear Pattern | Subtract | boltE |
| Cap Bolt Holes (2) | Extrude Cut | Subtract | cboltF |
| Oil Hole | Extrude Cut | Subtract | oilG |
| Fillet on bore edges | Fillet Feature | Modify | filletH |
| Chamfer on base edges | Chamfer Feature | Modify | chamferI |
| Drawing Extraction | View Projection → Drawing Workbench | None | drwgJ |
| Isometric View | Standard 3D View (NW Isometric) | None | isoK |
| Section View | Cut by Section Plane → Project | None | sectL |
| Sectional dimensions | Auto-dimension + Manual SP-46 | None | dimM |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Procedure for Generating 2D from 3D (Drawing Extraction)

The student must follow these steps in a CAD workbench (SolidWorks Drawing / CATIA Drafting / Creo Drawing):

**Step 1 — Create a New Drawing File.** Choose an appropriate drawing template (e.g., A3 sheet for Plummer Block, A2 for larger assemblies).

**Step 2 — Set Drawing Standards.** Configure → ISO, Third angle projection (as per SP 46), Drawing scale (1:1 or 1:2).

**Step 3 — Insert Standard 3D View.** Place the isometric or trimetric view of the 3D part in the title block area.

**Step 4 — Insert Orthographic Views.** Use "Projected View" command to automatically generate Front, Top, and Right side views from the base view. Software handles projection lines internally.

**Step 5 — Insert Section View.** Define a cutting plane → software generates the sectional view, applies hatching (cross-hatching) automatically using ANSI/ISO hatch patterns.

**Step 6 — Dimension Insertion.** Use "Auto-Dimension" or "Smart Dimension" — software extracts 3D dimensions directly. The student **edits** these to follow KTU SP 46 conventions (e.g., prefer dimensioning in the front view; avoid redundant dimensions).

**Step 7 — Add Geometric Tolerances and Surface Finish.** Use GD&T symbols (perpendicularity, cylindricity) and surface roughness (Ra values).

**Step 8 — Add Title Block, Bill of Materials (BOM), and Notes.** Standard KTU template includes part name, drawing number, scale, material, units, drawn/checked/approved fields.

**Step 9 — Plot or Export to PDF.** Output the final 2D drawing for submission.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always draw **Sectional Front View** of the Plummer Block (showing the bush and shaft in section). Always include a **TOP view without section**. Always show **hidden lines as dashed (short dashes)**, and **centre lines as long-dash short-dash chain lines**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The KTU-Standard 3D Modelling Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Start: Read Problem Statement] --> A2[Identify Standard Proportions from D]
    A2 --> A3[Select Modelling Software and Units mm]
    A3 --> A4[Create Part File and Choose Front Plane]
    A4 --> A5[Sketch 2D Profile of Base]
    A5 --> A6[Apply Geometric and Dimensional Constraints]
    A6 --> A7[Extrude Boss to Create Pedestal Base]
    A7 --> A8[Sketch Bush Profile on Top Plane]
    A8 --> A9[Revolve Boss 360 degrees to Create Cylindrical Bush]
    A9 --> A10[Extrude Cut Bore Through Bush]
    A10 --> A11[Extrude Cut Foundation Bolt Holes]
    A11 --> A12[Linear Pattern Foundation Holes 2X]
    A12 --> A13[Mirror Copy to Create Cap Geometry]
    A13 --> A14[Extrude Cut Cap Bolt Holes]
    A14 --> A15[Extrude Cut Oil Hole]
    A15 --> A16[Apply Fillet and Chamfer Features]
    A16 --> A17[Validate Solid and Check Mass Properties]
    A17 --> A18[Switch to Drawing Workbench]
    A18 --> A19[Insert Standard Orthographic Views]
    A19 --> A20[Insert Sectional Front View]
    A20 --> A21[Auto Dimension and Edit per SP 46]
    A21 --> A22[Add GD&T and Surface Finish]
    A22 --> A23[Add Title Block and BOM]
    A23 --> A24[Plot or Export PDF]
    A24 --> A25[End: Submit 3D Model and 2D Drawing]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Plummer Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Foundation Bolts] --> P2[Base Pedestal]
    P2 --> P3[Bore Surface]
    P3 --> P4[Bearing Bush Liner]
    P4 --> P5[Rotating Shaft Journal]
    P5 --> P6[Oil Film Lubrication]
    P2 --> P7[Cap]
    P7 --> P8[Cap Bolts M30]
    P7 --> P9[Oil Hole and Grease Nipple]
    P9 --> P6
    P4 --> P10[Babbitt or Bronze Layer]
    P10 --> P5
    P2 --> P11[Dowel Pin for Alignment]
    P2 --> P12[Foundation Bed or Frame]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — 2D Drawing Extraction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[3D Solid Model in Part File] --> S2[Drawing Workbench Activated]
    S2 --> S3[Sheet Format A2 or A3 Selected]
    S3 --> S4[Base View Inserted Isometric]
    S4 --> S5[Projected Views Front Top Right]
    S5 --> S6[Section Line Defined Through Centre]
    S6 --> S7[Sectional View Generated with Hatching]
    S7 --> S8[Auto Dimensions Extracted]
    S8 --> S9[Manual Dimension Cleanup per SP 46]
    S9 --> S10[GDT Symbols Added]
    S10 --> S11[Surface Roughness Ra Values Added]
    S11 --> S12[Title Block Filled with Material and Scale]
    S12 --> S13[BOM Table Added for Assembly if Any]
    S13 --> S14[Final Drawing Plotted]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Bearing Selection Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[What is the Application] --> Q2{Load Magnitude}
    Q2 --> Q3[Light Load under 5000 N] --> R1[Choose Ball Bearing]
    Q2 --> Q4[Heavy Load above 5000 N] --> R2[Choose Roller Bearing]
    Q2 --> Q5[Combined Radial and Axial] --> R3[Choose Tapered Roller or Angular Contact]
    Q1 --> Q6{Alignment Error Present}
    Q6 --> Q7[Yes Misalignment more than 0.001 rad] --> R4[Choose Self Aligning Bearing]
    Q6 --> Q8[No Alignment Rigid Shaft] --> R5[Choose Deep Groove Ball]
    Q1 --> Q9{Pure Axial Thrust}
    Q9 --> R6[Choose Thrust Ball or Thrust Roller]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Guideway Type Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G1[Guideway Type] --> G2[Sliding Contact Guideway]
    G1 --> G3[Rolling Contact Guideway]
    G1 --> G4[Fluid Film Guideway]
    G2 --> G2a[Cast Iron on Cast Iron Plain]
    G2 --> G2b[PTFE or Turcite Lined Low Friction]
    G2 --> G2c[Polymer Composite Lined]
    G3 --> G3a[Linear Recirculating Ball Rail]
    G3 --> G3b[Crossed Roller Way]
    G3 --> G3c[Needle Roller Way]
    G4 --> G4a[Hydrostatic with External Pump]
    G4 --> G4b[Hydrodynamic Self Generated Oil Film]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Q: Define solid modelling. How is it different from surface and wireframe modelling?**

**Model Answer (Concise Definition + 3 Differentiating Points):**

**Solid modelling** is a CAD technique that represents a 3D object as a fully enclosed volume with unambiguous mass, surface, and topological properties.

**Key Differences (3 points for 3 marks):**

| Aspect | Wireframe | Surface | Solid |
|---|---|---|---|
| Information | Edges only | Skin (no volume) | Complete volume |
| Hidden line ambiguity | Yes | Sometimes | No |
| Mass properties | Not computable | Not computable | Computable |

(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for differentiating table.)

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: List any three standard proportions used in designing a Plummer Block.**

**Model Answer (3 Proportions, 1 mark each):**

1. **Centre height** of the shaft above the bed: $H_c = 1.5D$ to $2.0D$.
2. **Length of the base**: $L_b = 3.0D$ to $4.0D$.
3. **Bush wall thickness**: $t_b = 0.08D$ to $0.12D$.

(where $D$ = shaft diameter)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]`

**Q: A Plummer Block has to be designed to support a shaft of diameter $D = 80$ mm carrying a radial load of $12$ kN at $720$ rpm.**

**(a)** Compute all the standard dimensions of the Plummer Block using empirical proportions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the step-by-step procedure for modelling this Plummer Block in a 3D solid modeller and generating its 2D engineering drawing. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Centre Height** (1 mark)

$$H_c = 1.75 \cdot D = 1.75 \times 80 = 140 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Base Length** (1 mark)

$$L_b = 3.5 \cdot D = 3.5 \times 80 = 280 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Base Width** (1 mark)

$$W_b = 2.5 \cdot D = 2.5 \times 80 = 200 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Bush Outer Diameter and Length** (1 mark)

$$t_b = 0.10 \cdot D = 0.10 \times 80 = 8 \text{ mm}$$

$$D_{bush,outer} = 80 + 2(8) = 96 \text{ mm}$$

$$L_{bush} = 1.25 \cdot D = 1.25 \times 80 = 100 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Cap and Base Plate Thicknesses** (1 mark)

$$t_{cap} = t_{base} = 30 \text{ mm (typical, since } D = 80 \text{ mm medium-heavy)}$$

**Step 6 — Total Height** (1 mark)

$$H_t = H_c + 0.5 \cdot D_{bush,outer} + t_{cap} + t_{base} = 140 + 48 + 30 + 30 = 248 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 7 — Bolt Sizes** (1 mark)

Cap bolt: $0.5D = 40$ → round to **M36** (or downgrade to M30 with washers). Foundation bolt: $0.4D = 32$ → **M30**.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**3D Modelling Procedure (5 marks):**

1. **Create new Part file**, set units to mm, choose Front Plane. (1 mark)
2. **Sketch the base profile** as a rectangle $280 \times 200$ mm. Apply dimensional and geometric constraints. **Extrude Boss** by 60 mm (combining cap + base thicknesses). (1 mark)
3. **Sketch bush profile** as a rectangle on Top Plane: width = bush length 100 mm, height = bush outer radius 48 mm. **Revolve Boss** 360° around the centreline to create the cylindrical bush. (1 mark)
4. **Bore** the bush — sketch a circle of diameter 80 mm on the bush face, **Extrude Cut** through all. (1 mark)
5. **Foundation bolt holes** — sketch 2 circles of diameter 31 mm (clearance for M30 bolt) on the base; **Extrude Cut** through base. **Linear Pattern** to place 2 holes. **Cap bolt holes** similarly. **Oil hole** of diameter 6 mm drilled. Apply **Fillet** $R = 5$ mm on the bore edges. (1 mark)

**2D Drawing Extraction (2 marks):**

6. Switch to Drawing Workbench. Insert Front, Top, Right side orthographic views and an Isometric view on A2 sheet, scale 1:2.
7. Insert a Sectional Front View (cutting plane through shaft centreline, hatched at 45°, ANSI metal hatch). Apply SP 46 dimensioning. Add title block with material "Grey Cast Iron FG 260" and surface roughness Ra 1.6 µm on bearing surface.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q: A rolling element deep groove ball bearing has the following specifications: shaft speed $n = 960$ rpm, radial load $F_r = 6$ kN, axial load $F_a = 1.5$ kN, basic dynamic load rating $C = 35$ kN. Take $X = 0.56$, $Y = 1.45$, $e = 0.30$ for this bearing.**

**(a)** Calculate the basic rating life $L_{10}$ in hours and comment on suitability for a 25000-hour application. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Briefly explain the standard features of (i) a self-aligning bearing and (ii) a hydrostatic guideway. State two advantages and one limitation of each. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Verify which row of manufacturer table applies.** (1 mark)

Check $F_a / F_r = 1500 / 6000 = 0.25$. Since $0.25 < e = 0.30$, use $X = 1, Y = 0$.

**Step 2 — Equivalent Dynamic Load** (1 mark)

$$P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a = 1 \cdot 6000 + 0 \cdot 1500 = 6000 \text{ N} = 6 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Life in Million Revolutions** (2 marks)

$$L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^3 = \left(\frac{35000}{6000}\right)^3 = (5.8333)^3$$

$$(5.8333)^2 = 34.0278, \quad \times 5.8333 = 198.495$$

$$L_{10} \approx 198.5 \text{ million revolutions}$$

**Step 4 — Life in Hours** (2 marks)

$$L_{10h} = \frac{10^6}{60 \cdot n} \cdot L_{10} = \frac{10^6}{60 \times 960} \times 198.5$$

$$L_{10h} = \frac{10^6}{57600} \times 198.5 = 17.361 \times 198.5 \approx 3446 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 5 — Verdict** (1 mark)

Since $3446 \text{ hrs} \ll 25000 \text{ hrs}$, the bearing is **NOT suitable**. Required $C$ is approximately:

$$C_{req} = P \cdot \sqrt[3]{\frac{60 n \cdot L_{10h,req}}{10^6}} = 6000 \cdot \sqrt[3]{\frac{60 \cdot 960 \cdot 25000}{10^6}}$$

$$C_{req} = 6000 \cdot \sqrt[3]{1440} = 6000 \cdot 11.28 \approx 67680 \text{ N} = 67.7 \text{ kN}$$

Recommend a bearing with $C \geq 70$ kN.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**(i) Self-Aligning Bearing — 3.5 marks:**

A self-aligning bearing has **two rows of balls** with a **spherical outer raceway**. This permits the inner ring (with shaft and balls) to tilt by 1.5° to 3° relative to the outer ring without inducing harmful internal stresses. Used where shaft deflection or misalignment is unavoidable (e.g., long shafts, cardan shafts).

- **Advantages:** (1) Tolerates misalignment. (2) Reduces maintenance frequency in flexible shaft applications.
- **Limitation:** Lower load capacity compared to a deep groove ball bearing of the same bore due to the spherical raceway geometry; not suitable for high-speed precision applications.

**(ii) Hydrostatic Guideway — 3.5 marks:**

A hydrostatic guideway uses a **pressurized oil film** injected through orifices (or capillary restrictors) between the slide and the bed. The oil pressure lifts the slide, creating a virtually frictionless separation of the metal surfaces.

- **Advantages:** (1) No metal-to-metal contact — extremely low wear and infinite life. (2) Excellent damping due to oil film compliance, ideal for precision grinding machines.
- **Limitation:** Requires a constant external high-pressure hydraulic pump and filtration system — high initial cost and maintenance complexity.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks (Per KTU Valuation Key Patterns):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to check $F_a/F_r < e$ before using $X=0.56, Y=1.45$ in bearing life problems.** Loss: 1 to 2 marks. Always compute $F_a/F_r$ first.
> 2. **Mixing up $C/P$ with $P/C$.** The basic rating life formula is $(C/P)^p$, NOT $(P/C)^p$. Loss: 1 mark.
> 3. **Wrong power in Petroff equation** — students often write $4\pi^2$ instead of $2\pi^2$. Loss: 1 mark.
> 4. **3D model not watertight** — if the student leaves a gap between the cap and base or fails to use Boolean Union, the solid is invalid and Section view will fail to render. Loss: 2 marks.
> 5. **Missing hatching or wrong hatch angle (45°)** in Sectional Front View of Plummer Block. Loss: 1 mark.
> 6. **Dimensions on both Front and Sectional view (redundant dimensioning).** Loss: 1 mark.
> 7. **Forgetting to add Centre lines (chain dashed) for cylindrical features.** Loss: 1 mark.
> 8. **Using Wireframe or Surface instead of Solid.** Loss: 2 to 3 marks — entire problem considered incomplete.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision List — Read This Before the Exam**

- [ ] 3D **Solid** model is mandatory — wireframe/surface models are **not** accepted.
- [ ] Plummer Block = **Pedestal Base + Cap + Bush + Foundation Bolts + Cap Bolts + Oil Hole + Grease Nipple**.
- [ ] Standard empirical proportions (remember with respect to shaft diameter $D$): $H_c = 1.5D$–$2.0D$, $L_b = 3.0D$–$4.0D$, $W_b = 2.0D$–$3.0D$, $L_{bush} = 1.0D$–$1.5D$, $t_b = 0.08D$–$0.12D$.
- [ ] Cap bolts are typically **$0.5D$** rounded up to standard ISO metric size (M12, M16, M20, M24, M30, M36).
- [ ] Bearing life formula: $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$; convert to hours using $L_{10h} = (10^6 / 60n) \cdot (C/P)^p$.
- [ ] $p = 3$ for **ball bearings**, $p = 10/3$ for **roller bearings**.
- [ ] Petroff friction equation: $f = 2\pi^2 \cdot (\mu N / P) \cdot (r/c)$.
- [ ] Always **check $F_a / F_r$ vs $e$** before choosing $X$ and $Y$ for equivalent load.
- [ ] Standard fits: bearing inner ring on shaft → $k_5 / m_6 / n_6$; bearing outer ring in housing → $H_7 / J_7$.
- [ ] Self-aligning bearing → two-row balls with **spherical outer race** → tolerates misalignment up to ~3°.
- [ ] Hydrostatic guideway → external pressurized oil film → zero metal-to-metal contact → used in precision grinding.
- [ ] Rolling guideways → recirculating ball/roller rails (Hiwin, THK) → high precision, low friction.
- [ ] Sliding guideways → risk of **stick-slip** → use PTFE/Turcite liners to stabilise $\mu$.
- [ ] Drawing extraction is done in the **Drawing Workbench** of the CAD software.
- [ ] Sectional Front View of Plummer Block is mandatory — use hatching at **45°** for cast iron / steel.
- [ ] Always include **centre lines** (long-dash short-dash chain) on all cylindrical features.
- [ ] Always include **hidden lines** (short dashes) on all hidden features in non-sectioned views.
- [ ] Standard drawing scales (SP 46 / ISO 5455): **1:1, 1:2, 1:5, 1:10, 1:20, 1:50**.
- [ ] Use ISO third-angle projection for all KTU submissions.
- [ ] Cast iron density = $7.2 \text{ g/cm}^3$; bearing steel (AISI 52100) = $7.85 \text{ g/cm}^3$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assembly Drawing of Mechanical Engineering Parts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRL307 - MACHINE DRAWING AND SOLID MODELLING
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Assembly Drawing of Mechanical Engineering Parts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Assembly Drawing** is a technical engineering drawing that depicts a complete mechanical unit or machine by showing **all its component parts in their correct assembled positions**, with their inter-relationships, mating features, and the sequence in which they fit together. It serves as the master graphical document that communicates the **form, fit, function, and fabrication** of every individual part that constitutes the assembly.

In the KTU 2024 PCBRL307 syllabus context, the term *Mechanical Engineering Parts* refers to standardized machine elements such as:
- **Bearings** (Plummer Block, Footstep Bearing, Pedestal Bearing)
- **Couplings** (Protected / Unprotected Flange Coupling, Muff Coupling)
- **Power Transmission Devices** (Screw Jack, Tailstock, Lathe Chuck)
- **Valves** (Globe Valve, Stop Valve, Non-Return Valve)
- **Holding Devices** (Machine Vice, Clamps)
- **Joints and Fastening Assemblies** (Knuckle Joint, Cotter Joint)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Reference:** Per IS 1503 (Indian Standard for Assembly Drawings) and Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) conventions, an assembly drawing must show: (1) All parts in assembled condition, (2) Center lines and reference dimensions, (3) A parts list / Bill of Materials (BOM), (4) Title block with scale, projection symbol, and sheet number.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Jigsaw Metaphor

Imagine you have purchased a **flat-pack wooden table from IKEA**. Inside the box, you receive many pieces of wood, screws, allen keys, and a one-page *exploded pictorial view* showing how all the parts fit together. That single page is, in essence, an **assembly drawing**.

- The **wooden planks** = Individual part drawings
- The **screws and dowels** = Standard parts (bolts, nuts, keys)
- The **exploded view on the instruction sheet** = Assembly drawing
- The **numbered list of items** on the back of the sheet = Bill of Materials (BOM)

Now imagine the inverse: an engineer is given the exploded pictorial and the *list of all parts* (with their individual shop drawings). Their job is to **reconstruct the assembly drawing** so that a fitter in the workshop can use it to assemble the product. This reverse-engineering task — *going from individual part drawings to a single composite assembly* — is the **core skill** evaluated in KTU Module 2.

## 1.3 Why Assembly Drawings Are Indispensable

| Industrial Function | Real-World Utility |
|---|---|
| **Manufacturing Communication** | Instructs the assembly line worker on the precise stacking order of parts |
| **Quality Control** | Acts as the *reference master* against which the physical product is inspected |
| **Maintenance & Servicing** | Exploded variants help the technician disassemble, repair, and reassemble |
| **Design Validation** | Reveals interferences, accessibility issues, and assembly sequence bottlenecks |
| **Procurement** | The BOM drives the purchase of raw castings, bought-out standard parts, and fasteners |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Anchor (KTU Board Examiner Trick):** Whenever the question says *"prepare the assembly drawing from the given part drawings"*, the examiner is checking your ability to **identify, orient, and mate** the parts correctly — not your artistic skill. Precision and standardization win marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Assembly Drawings

Assembly drawings are classified along **two orthogonal axes**: (a) the *level of detail* shown, and (b) the *stage of design* at which they are produced.

### 2.1.1 Classification by Level of Detail

1. **General Assembly Drawing** — Shows all parts in their fully assembled position. Includes overall dimensions, key mating dimensions, centerlines, and a complete BOM. This is the *most common type* asked in KTU examinations.
2. **Sub-Assembly Drawing** — Shows only a sub-group of related parts that form an independent functional unit within a larger assembly (e.g., the *valve-disc-stem* subassembly of a globe valve, separated from the *body-bonnet* subassembly).
3. **Detailed Assembly Drawing** — A general assembly that *also* shows enough small features (undercuts, fillets, hidden internal features) so that each part can be manufactured directly from it without referring to individual part drawings. Rare in KTU but valuable conceptually.

### 2.1.2 Classification by Stage of Design

1. **Design Assembly Drawing** — Produced during the *conceptual / preliminary design* stage. Shows only the rough outline, the spatial arrangement, and the critical functional relationships. Dimensions are *not* shown — only overall envelope and key interface dimensions.
2. **Working Assembly Drawing** — Produced after the design is frozen. Shows the *final, dimensioned* assembly that drives production.
3. **Installation Assembly Drawing** — Shows the assembly as it would be installed on its foundation or on the mating machine, including foundation bolt locations, alignment dimensions, and clearance for service.
4. **Exploded Assembly Drawing (Pictorial)** — Shows all parts pulled apart along the assembly axis with *leader lines* (or "screamer lines") indicating where each part goes. Used in service manuals and instruction sheets.

## 2.2 Standard Conventions Used in Assembly Drawings (IS 1503 / IS 696)

The Bureau of Indian Standards mandates the following conventions. Mastering these is **non-negotiable** for KTU marks.

### 2.2.1 Conventional Representation of Standard Parts

| Standard Part | Conventional Representation Rule |
|---|---|
| **Bolts, Nuts, Studs, Screws** | Shown *in assembled position* but not sectioned. Drawn in their *external* view only — internal threads, shank details, and slot depths are omitted. |
| **Bearings (Ball / Roller)** | Shown in *section* only when they need to clarify function. Ball bearings are shown with circles and the cage as a simplified outline. |
| **Springs (Helical)** | Shown in *section* as a series of dashed parallel lines (coils on the far side) and solid lines (coils on the near side). No detailed coil profile. |
| **Gears, Sprockets, Pulleys** | Teeth are *not* drawn individually. Shown as a simple outline with a *phantom line* (chain-dotted) representing the *pitch circle*. The tooth profile is replaced by a continuous circle. |
| **Shafts, Pins, Spindles** | *Not sectioned* even when the cutting plane passes through them. Drawn in external view with center lines. |
| **Keys, Cotters** | Shown in *section* when used to lock a hub onto a shaft. |
| **Castings** | Shown in *section* with **uniform 45° parallel hatching** spaced approximately **3 mm apart** on standard A3 / A2 sheets. |
| **Different adjacent parts in section** | Hatched at *different angles* (typically 45° and 135°) and/or *different spacings* to visually separate them. Same part, even if sectioned at multiple locations, must have hatching at the *same angle and spacing*. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Rule:** The single most violated convention is the **shaft-not-sectioned rule**. Students often draw section lines through a solid shaft, which the examiner penalises heavily. A solid shaft is *always* shown in external view, with no section hatching.

### 2.2.2 Sectioning Strategy

The **front view** of an assembly drawing is almost always a *full-section* or *half-section*. KTU favourites are:

- **Full Section (A-A)** — The cutting plane line passes completely through the assembly. All cut material is hatched. The cutting plane is identified by capital letters (A-A) and arrows showing the viewing direction.
- **Half Section** — One half is shown in section and the other half in external view. Used when the assembly is **symmetric** about a vertical center line (typical for valves, couplings).
- **Aligned Section** — Cutting plane is bent at 90° to pass through features that are not aligned. Used when holes are arranged on different axes.

### 2.2.3 Dimensioning Philosophy

Assembly drawings follow the **functional dimensioning** principle: dimensions are placed on the parts that *carry the function*, not distributed arbitrarily.

| Dimension Category | Example | Where It Goes |
|---|---|---|
| **Overall Dimensions** | Length × Width × Height of the whole assembly | Extreme edges of the front and top views |
| **Key Mating Dimensions** | Bearing bore, shaft diameter at the coupling seat | On the *mating parts* (e.g., the bearing inner race *and* the shaft journal) |
| **Performance Dimensions** | Centre height of a pedestal bearing from the base | Critical for shaft alignment — must be shown |
| **Assembly Reference Dimensions** | Distance between bolt centers on a flange | Shown with a *bracket* (square bracket) or in parentheses to indicate it is *reference only* |

> [!NOTE]
> **Do NOT** show every dimension of every part on the assembly drawing. Each *individual* part drawing carries its own manufacturing dimensions. The assembly drawing shows only the dimensions needed to **assemble the parts correctly**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Drawing Scale | $S = \dfrac{L_{\text{drawing}}}{L_{\text{actual}}}$ | ratio (dimensionless) | High |
| 2 | Standard Scales (IS 1503) | Assembly: 1:2, 1:5, 1:10 (reducing); 1:1, 2:1 (enlarging) | — | High |
| 3 | Overall Dimension on Drawing | $D_d = D_a \times S$ | mm | High |
| 4 | Limit Dimensions (Shaft-Hole Fit) | $H = N - h$ (allowance), $T_h + T_s = F$ (fundamental deviation sum) | mm (microns in precision) | Medium |
| 5 | Clearance Fit | $C = H_s - h_h$ where $H_s$ = max hole, $h_h$ = min shaft | mm | Medium |
| 6 | Interference Fit | $I = h_s - H_h$ where $h_s$ = max shaft, $H_h$ = min hole | mm | Medium |
| 7 | Transition Fit Boundary | $\vert H_s - h_h \vert \leq \vert h_s - H_h \vert$ for guaranteed interference | mm | Low |
| 8 | Surface Roughness Grade | $R_a$ value in $\mu m$ (1 N-grade ≈ 2× previous grade) | micrometres ($\mu m$) | Medium |
| 9 | Ball Bearing Pitch Diameter | $D_p = \dfrac{D + d}{2}$ | mm | High (for Plummer block) |
| 10 | Keyway Depth on Shaft | $t_1 = 0.75 \times h$ (rectangular key per IS 2292) | mm | Medium |
| 11 | Keyway Depth on Hub | $t_2 = 0.5 \times h$ | mm | Medium |
| 12 | Bolt Circle Diameter (BCD) | $D_{BCD} = \dfrac{D_{center} \times \sin(180°/n)}{\text{related ratio}}$ — simpler: $D_{BCD} = \dfrac{p}{\sin(180°/n)}$ for $n$ bolts on pitch $p$ | mm | Medium |
| 13 | Spring Free Length Indicator | $L_f = L_s + \delta$ where $\delta$ is deflection | mm | Low |
| 14 | BOM Item Numbering | Sequential integers, leader lines with filled circles or arrows | — | High |
| 15 | Hatching Spacing | Minimum **3 mm**, maximum **6 mm** apart on the actual sheet | mm | High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 3 mm hatching rule** is one of the most common reasons students lose 1–2 marks. Hatching too close (looks like a black blob) or too far (looks like an error) is penalised. Aim for roughly 4 mm spacing on A3 / A2 sheets.

## 2.4 The Bill of Materials (BOM) — Anatomy

The BOM is a *tabular block* placed in the **upper-right corner** of the assembly drawing sheet. It contains the following columns (per IS 1503):

1. **Item No.** — Sequential integer (1, 2, 3, …) matching the *leader-line bubble* in the drawing
2. **Description** — Part name (e.g., "Body", "Bolt M12 × 60", "Gasket")
3. **Material** — Grade of material (e.g., "Cast Iron FG 200", "EN8 Steel", "Bearing Steel SAE 52100")
4. **Quantity** — Number of pieces of that item used in *one* assembly
5. **Part No. / Remarks** — Optional: drawing reference, supplier code, etc.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Pattern:** Standard bought-out items (bolts, nuts, washers, keys) are usually listed *without individual part drawings*. They are identified by standard reference (e.g., "Hex Bolt M16 × 80 IS 1363"). Only the *custom-manufactured* parts are detailed in separate part drawings.

# 3. Step-by-Step Methodology for Preparing an Assembly Drawing

This section presents the **exhaustive step-by-step procedure** that the KTU examiner expects a student to follow. Each step is logically justified.

## 3.1 The Seven-Stage Procedure

### Stage 1 — Inventory and Identification of Parts

**Objective:** Enumerate every part drawing supplied and establish the *master part* (the body / housing) and the *fastener stack* (shaft, bearings, caps, bolts, keys, etc.).

**Procedure:**
1. Lay out all the supplied part drawings on a clean table.
2. Group them into three logical buckets:
   - **Casting / Housing Parts** — Body, cover, cap, base. These are the *stationary* structural elements.
   - **Rotating / Moving Parts** — Shaft, disc, spindle, piston.
   - **Standard / Bought-Out Parts** — Bolts, nuts, washers, keys, bearings, seals, O-rings, springs.

**Why this matters:** KTU questions often hide the assembly logic by giving parts in random order. The student must first recognise that, e.g., in a Plummer Block, the *pedestal* is the master, the *bearing* and *shaft* sit inside it, and the *cap* closes it from the top.

### Stage 2 — Selection of Views and Section Plane

**Objective:** Decide which two views (front + top, or front + side) will best communicate the assembly.

**Procedure:**
1. Identify the **principal axis** of the assembly. For rotating assemblies (bearings, couplings), the *shaft axis* is principal. For reciprocating assemblies (screw jack), the *screw axis* is principal.
2. Orient the assembly so the *maximum number of internal features* is visible in the front view.
3. Choose the **cutting plane** to pass through:
   - The shaft centerline (so the shaft's mating with bores is visible)
   - One or more bolt axes (to show the bolting arrangement)
   - Any internal cavity (e.g., the port of a valve)

**General Rule of Thumb:**
$$\text{Number of cut features} = \sum(\text{shafts}) + \sum(\text{bolts on plane}) + \sum(\text{internal cavities on plane})$$

### Stage 3 — Establishment of Reference Geometry (Center Lines and Axis Lines)

**Objective:** Draw the *invisible skeleton* of the assembly before any solid lines.

**Procedure:**
1. Locate the **main center line** (CL) along the shaft / screw axis — drawn as a *chain-dotted line* (long-short-long).
2. Mark the **center heights** of all mating bores (e.g., the bearing seat center, the bolt-hole pitch circle center).
3. Mark the **overall envelope** as a light construction rectangle (length × width) to bound the drawing.
4. Draw the **bolt pitch circles** (BCDs) using:
   $$D_{BCD} = 2R_{bolt}$$
   where $R_{bolt}$ is the radial distance of each bolt from the assembly centerline.

### Stage 4 — Blocking Out the Master Part (Housing)

**Objective:** Sketch the housing/casting in light pencil, then ink the *permanent lines* (Type A: 0.6 mm for visible edges).

**Procedure:**
1. Draw the *outer profile* of the body in both views.
2. Draw the *mating bores* (bearing seat, bolt holes) using center lines and dimension markers.
3. Apply **45° hatching** to the cut sections, spaced approximately **4 mm apart** on the actual sheet.
4. Ensure the hatching for the *body* is at one angle (say 45° N-E) and the hatching for any *adjacent different part* on the cutting plane (e.g., a key, a gland) is at a *different* angle (say 135°) or different spacing.

### Stage 5 — Insertion of Mating Parts in Sequence

**Objective:** Add the remaining parts in their *assembly sequence*, which is from largest to smallest and from inside to outside.

**Sequence Rules:**
1. **Inside first** — Shaft, then bearings, then seals, then caps, then bolts. This is the *fitter's actual sequence*.
2. **Larger features first** — The main bore is drawn before the small bolt holes.
3. **Section conventions** — Apply *shaft-not-sectioned*, *bolt-not-sectioned*, *bearing-in-section-or-simplified* as per IS 1503.

**Example for a Plummer Block (Assembly Sequence):**
1. Pedestal (master casting)
2. Shaft (drawn in external view, NOT sectioned)
3. Bearing (drawn in simplified section or external view with phantom circle for the pitch diameter)
4. Cap (top half of the bearing housing)
5. Cap bolts (M-grade bolts in external view, with proper socket / hex head outline)
6. Set screws (against the shaft, drawn in section)
7. Oil hole / grease nipple

### Stage 6 — Application of Dimensions and Part Numbers

**Objective:** Add only the *necessary* dimensions and assign item numbers to every part.

**Procedure:**
1. Add the **overall dimensions** (L × W × H) on the extreme edges of the views.
2. Add the **key mating dimensions**: bearing bore diameter, shaft length, bolt center distance.
3. Add **performance dimensions**: pedestal center height from the base (critical for shaft alignment).
4. For each part, draw a **leader line** terminating in a *filled circle* (or arrow) carrying the item number. The leader line should point to the part, not to a void or a center line.
5. Cross-check: every item in the drawing must appear in the BOM, and every item in the BOM must be visible in the drawing.

**Item Numbering Convention:**
$$I_n \in \{1, 2, 3, \ldots, n\}$$
where $n$ is the total number of distinct parts in the assembly.

### Stage 7 — Drawing the Title Block and BOM, and Final Check

**Objective:** Complete the documentation.

**Procedure:**
1. Draw the **title block** in the lower-right corner with: Assembly name, scale, projection symbol (1st-angle for KTU), drawing number, name of the drafter, date.
2. Draw the **BOM** in the upper-right corner with the five columns: Item No., Description, Material, Qty, Remarks.
3. Verify the **alignment** of all views: every horizontal line in the front view must align with the corresponding feature in the top view (projection lines are mandatory).
4. Verify the **no-section rules** for shafts, bolts, nuts, keys, pins, rivets, and balls/rollers of bearings.
5. Verify the **hatching consistency** — same angle and spacing for the *same* part; different angle or spacing for *different* parts.

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example — Dimensioning Logic of a Plummer Block

A Plummer block is a pillow-block bearing assembly supporting a rotating shaft. A KTU 2024 Part B question typically gives 6–8 part drawings (pedestal, cap, cap bolts, bearing, shaft, set screws, oil plug) and asks for the assembly.

Let us compute the **key dimensions** that must appear on the assembly drawing.

**Given (typical KTU data sheet):**
- Shaft diameter $d = 50 \text{ mm}$
- Bearing bore $D = 50 \text{ mm}$ (matches the shaft with a transition fit, e.g., H7/k6)
- Pedestal base length $L_b = 280 \text{ mm}$
- Pedestal base width $W_b = 80 \text{ mm}$
- Pedestal height $H_p = 130 \text{ mm}$ (from base bottom to shaft centerline)
- Cap bolt center distance $P_b = 150 \text{ mm}$ (center-to-center between the two cap bolts)
- Bearing overall width $B = 60 \text{ mm}$

**Derived Dimensions:**

1. **Pedestal bearing seat center height** (from base to shaft CL):
$$H_p = 130 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(given — must appear on the front view as a vertical dimension)}$$

2. **Bearing pitch diameter** (used for the conventional circle on the bearing outline):
$$D_p = \frac{D + d}{2} = \frac{50 + 50}{2} = 50 \text{ mm}$$
Wait — for a Plummer block the bearing is a *journal (sliding) bearing*, not a rolling-element bearing. The pitch diameter concept does not apply. The relevant dimension is the **journal bearing bore** $D = 50 \text{ mm}$ which equals the shaft diameter (with a clearance of roughly $0.05$ to $0.1 \text{ mm}$).

3. **Bearing length-to-diameter ratio** (L/D rule for journal bearings):
$$\frac{L}{D} = \frac{B}{D} = \frac{60}{50} = 1.2$$
This is within the typical safe range of $0.5 \leq L/D \leq 1.5$ for a Plummer-block journal bearing. The KTU examiner will not ask you to compute this, but knowing it confirms your design understanding.

4. **Cap bolt tightening torque reference** (not asked on the drawing, but useful):
$$T = k \cdot d_b \cdot F$$
where $k \approx 0.2$ (nut factor for steel-on-steel), $d_b$ is the bolt nominal diameter, $F$ is the preload. The KTU question does not require this calculation, but mentioning it in your exam answer shows *applied understanding* — examiner's pet.

5. **Hatching angle for the cap vs. pedestal**: They are *the same cast part family* but the cap is a *separate piece*. Convention: hatch the cap at the *same angle* (45° N-E) as the pedestal, but with *opposite direction* (45° N-W) OR *different spacing* (4 mm vs. 3 mm). The KTU preferred convention is **mirror hatching** — the cap hatching is mirrored across the mating plane to show that the two are *bolted together*, not a single monolithic casting.

**Visualisation of the Mirror-Hatching Rule:**
$$\theta_{\text{cap}} = 180° - \theta_{\text{pedestal}}$$
where $\theta$ is measured counter-clockwise from the horizontal axis.

## 3.3 Common Interference Identification

A high-yield KTU trap is the *interference check*. Before finalising, mentally simulate the assembly:

- Does the **shaft** clear the **keyway** at the full length of the hub?
- Does the **bearing inner race** seat fully on the **shaft journal**?
- Do the **cap bolts** have clearance for the spanner / socket?
- Does the **oil hole** align with the **bearing cavity** in both the body and the cap?

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Forgetting the **oil hole alignment** between the pedestal and the cap is a classic 1-mark deduction. Always draw the oil hole as a continuous line in the front section view, showing it aligned through the cap and into the bearing seat.

## 3.4 Projection Convention — First Angle vs. Third Angle

KTU follows the **First Angle Projection** convention (per IS 1503 and Indian Standard practice). In first-angle:

- The **Top view** is placed *below* the front view
- The **Left side view** is placed to the *right* of the front view
- The symbol is a truncated cone with the small end facing the viewer (a frustum icon)

$$\text{Symbol} = \text{Frustum (truncated cone) with small end toward the viewer}$$

In third-angle (US convention), the symbol is a frustum with the *large* end toward the viewer, and the views are arranged opposite to first-angle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Mark Lifesaver:** If the KTU question specifies *"Use First Angle Projection"*, you must place the top view *below* the front view. Mixing the two conventions is an automatic **2-mark penalty** in the valuation key.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Mermaid Block Architecture)

Since the KTU topic is fundamentally a *visual drafting* discipline, the Mermaid diagrams below present the **functional architecture and methodology** rather than attempting to render the actual 2D orthographic drawings.

## 4.1 Workflow Architecture — From Part Drawings to Assembly Drawing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Receive Individual Part Drawings] --> stepB[Step 1: Identify Master Casting and Fastener Stack]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 2: Determine Principal Assembly Axis]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 3: Choose Front and Top or Front and Side Views]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 4: Decide Section Plane A-A Location]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 5: Draw Reference Center Lines and Bolt Pitch Circles]
    stepF --> stepG[Step 6: Block Out Master Part in Light Pencil]
    stepG --> stepH[Step 7: Ink Permanent Visible Edges Type A 0.6 mm]
    stepH --> stepI[Step 8: Insert Shaft in External View No Section]
    stepI --> stepJ[Step 9: Insert Bearing in Simplified Section]
    stepJ --> stepK[Step 10: Insert Cap Cap Bolts Set Screws in Sequence]
    stepK --> stepL[Step 11: Apply Hatching with Mirror Rule for Cap]
    stepL --> stepM[Step 12: Add Overall and Key Mating Dimensions]
    stepM --> stepN[Step 13: Assign Item Numbers with Leader Lines]
    stepN --> stepO[Step 14: Draw Title Block in Lower Right]
    stepO --> stepP[Step 15: Draw BOM in Upper Right]
    stepP --> stepQ[Step 16: Final Verification View Alignment No-Section Rules]
    stepQ --> stepR[End: Completed Assembly Drawing]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Plummer Block Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph topTier["TOP TIER BOLTING AND CAP"]
        item8[Item 8 Hex Nut M16]
        item7[Item 7 Cap Bolt M16 x 100]
        item6[Item 6 Cap Top Half]
        item9[Item 9 Plain Washer]
    end
    subgraph midTier["MID TIER BEARING AND LUBRICATION"]
        item4[Item 4 Journal Bearing Liner]
        item10[Item 10 Grease Nipple]
        item11[Item 11 Oil Hole Through Cap and Body]
    end
    subgraph bottomTier["BOTTOM TIER PEDESTAL AND SHAFT"]
        item1[Item 1 Pedestal Base Casting]
        item2[Item 2 Shaft]
        item3[Item 3 Bearing Seat Bore]
        item5[Item 5 Set Screw M10]
    end
    item1 --> item3
    item3 --> item4
    item4 --> item2
    item6 --> item4
    item7 --> item6
    item7 --> item1
    item8 --> item7
    item9 --> item7
    item5 --> item2
    item10 --> item11
    item11 --> item4
```

## 4.3 Classification Tree of Assembly Drawings

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[Assembly Drawings of Mechanical Parts] --> branchB[By Level of Detail]
    rootA --> branchC[By Design Stage]
    branchB --> leafB1[General Assembly]
    branchB --> leafB2[Sub Assembly]
    branchB --> leafB3[Detailed Assembly]
    branchC --> leafC1[Design Assembly]
    leafC1 --> leafC1a[Concept Stage No Dimensions]
    leafC1 --> leafC1b[Preliminary Stage Key Dimensions Only]
    leafC1 --> leafC1c[Final Stage Fully Dimensioned]
    branchC --> leafC2[Working Assembly]
    leafC2 --> leafC2a[Production Ready]
    leafC2 --> leafC2b[Includes BOM and All Mating Dimensions]
    branchC --> leafC3[Installation Assembly]
    leafC3 --> leafC3a[Foundation Bolts and Alignment]
    leafC3 --> leafC3b[Clearance for Service Tools]
    branchC --> leafC4[Exploded Assembly Pictorial]
    leafC4 --> leafC4a[Service Manuals and Instruction Sheets]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram of the No-Section Convention Rule

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ruleA[KTU No Section Convention IS 1503] --> partB[Components That Must NOT Be Sectioned]
    ruleA --> partC[Components That MAY Be Sectioned]
    partB --> list1[Solid Shafts]
    partB --> list2[Bolts Nuts Screws Studs]
    partB --> list3[Keys Pins Rivets Spindles]
    partB --> list4[Balls and Rollers of Bearings]
    partB --> list5[Spokes of Pulleys and Gears]
    partB --> list6[Webs of Ribs]
    partC --> list7[Castings Body Cap Pedestal]
    partC --> list8[Hub and Flange Thickness]
    partC --> list9[Springs in Helical Form]
    partC --> list10[Bearing Outer and Inner Rings If Functionally Required]
    ruleA --> ruleD[Rationale Avoid Confusing the Reader with Internal Features of Standard Parts]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid Block Diagrams and Not Real Drawings?** Real assembly drawings require *exact geometric proportions, hidden-line removal, hatching patterns, and dimension chains* that cannot be expressed in a flowchart syntax. Mermaid here serves as the **methodological blueprint** — the cognitive map a student should hold in mind before putting pencil to paper. The actual drawing must be done by hand on the answer sheet (or in CAD software) using the *blocks* defined above.
