# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define an Assembly Drawing. List any four standard conventions used in preparing it.**

**Model Answer:**

An **Assembly Drawing** is a technical drawing that depicts a complete machine or mechanical unit in its fully assembled state, showing all component parts in their correct relative positions, along with a Bill of Materials (BOM) and necessary overall/key mating dimensions (per **IS 1503**).

**Four standard conventions:**
1. **Shaft-not-sectioned rule** — A solid shaft is never drawn in section even when the cutting plane passes through it.
2. **Mirror hatching for bolted caps** — A cap and its base, when sectioned, are hatched in *opposite directions* at the same angle to indicate they are separate parts.
3. **Conventional representation of standard parts** — Bolts, nuts, keys, bearings, and springs are drawn in *simplified* form without internal detail.
4. **Leader-line item numbering** — Every part in the assembly is identified by a *filled circle* (or arrow) at the end of a leader line, with a sequential integer matching the BOM.

*(Examiner's Note: Any 4 of the above conventions are acceptable. The 3-mark break-up is typically 1 for definition + 2 for the list.)*

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is a Bill of Materials (BOM)? State its standard placement and the four mandatory columns.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Bill of Materials (BOM)** is a tabular document attached to an assembly drawing that lists *every distinct part* required to fabricate the assembly, with its identity, material, and quantity. It serves as the procurement and reference document for the workshop.

**Standard Placement:** Upper-right corner of the assembly drawing sheet, above the title block (per IS 1503).

**Four Mandatory Columns:**
1. **Item Number** — Sequential integer (1, 2, 3, …)
2. **Description** — Part name (e.g., "Hex Bolt M16 × 80 IS 1363")
3. **Material** — Material grade (e.g., "Cast Iron FG 200", "EN8 Steel")
4. **Quantity** — Number of pieces per assembly

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B is a 14-mark question with internal choice (Attempt any one of A or B). The break-up is typically: **7 marks for the assembly drawing itself** (correctness, views, sectioning, conventions) + **7 marks for the BOM and dimensions** (4 marks BOM, 3 marks dimensions). The exact break-up is given at the end of each question.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** The following part drawings of a **Plummer Block (Pillow Block Bearing)** are supplied: (i) Pedestal base casting, (ii) Cap (top half), (iii) Journal bearing liner, (iv) Cap bolts M16 × 100 — 2 Nos., (v) Hex nuts M16 — 2 Nos., (vi) Plain washers — 2 Nos., (vii) Set screws M10 — 2 Nos., (viii) Shaft (shown in part section), (ix) Grease nipple. Draw the **assembly drawing** of the Plummer Block in first-angle projection. Show the front view in **half section** and the top view in **external view**. Scale: 1:2.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Prepare the **Bill of Materials (BOM)** for the above assembly in standard format, and add the following dimensions to the assembly drawing: (i) Overall length, width, and height of the pedestal, (ii) Bearing bore diameter, (iii) Centre height of the shaft from the base, (iv) Distance between the two cap bolt centers.

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Identify the master part and fastener stack:**
- Master: Pedestal (Item 1)
- Stationary parts: Cap (Item 2), Bearing liner (Item 3)
- Rotating: Shaft (Item 8)
- Fasteners: Cap bolts (Item 4), nuts (Item 5), washers (Item 6), set screws (Item 7), grease nipple (Item 9)

**Step 2 — Principal axis:** The shaft axis is horizontal, located at the geometric centre of the pedestal.

**Step 3 — Section plane:** A vertical plane passing through the shaft centreline and one cap bolt axis. Half section is used (assembly is symmetric about the vertical CL).

**Step 4 — Construction sequence (7 marks allocation):**
1. Draw the shaft centreline horizontally in the front view, and the pedestal centreline vertically. *(1 mark)*
2. Block out the pedestal profile in the front view (rectangular base + curved bearing seat on top). Mirror this in the top view as a rectangular block with rounded bearing boss. *(1 mark)*
3. Draw the cap above the pedestal with a horizontal mating line at the shaft centreline. Apply **mirror hatching** — pedestal at 45° NE, cap at 45° NW. *(2 marks)*
4. Insert the shaft in **external view** (NOT sectioned) passing through the bearing seat. The bearing liner is shown in **section** as a thin hatched layer between the shaft and the pedestal seat. *(1 mark)*
5. Insert the two cap bolts vertically through the cap and into the pedestal — shown in external view, **NOT sectioned**, with proper hex-head and nut outline. Insert the set screws horizontally, shown in section, locking the bearing liner to the pedestal. *(1 mark)*
6. Insert the grease nipple on top of the cap, aligned with the oil hole that passes through the cap and into the bearing cavity. *(1 mark)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Bill of Materials (BOM) — Place in upper-right corner:**

| Item No. | Description | Material | Qty | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pedestal Base | Cast Iron FG 200 | 1 | IS 210 Grade 200 |
| 2 | Cap Top Half | Cast Iron FG 200 | 1 | Matched to Item 1 |
| 3 | Bearing Liner | Phosphor Bronze Grade 2 | 1 | Self-lubricating |
| 4 | Hex Head Bolt M16 x 100 | Carbon Steel Gr 8.8 | 2 | IS 1364 Part 1 |
| 5 | Hex Nut M16 | Carbon Steel Gr 8 | 2 | IS 1364 Part 3 |
| 6 | Plain Washer 17 ID | Spring Steel | 2 | IS 2016 |
| 7 | Set Screw M10 x 25 | Carbon Steel | 2 | IS 1364 Part 5 |
| 8 | Shaft | EN8 Steel | 1 | Shown in part section |
| 9 | Grease Nipple M10 | Carbon Steel | 1 | IS 4009 Part 1 |

**Dimensions to add (3 marks):**
1. **Overall dimensions** — Length (e.g., 280 mm), width (e.g., 80 mm), height (e.g., 130 mm) on the extreme edges of the front view. *(1 mark)*
2. **Bearing bore diameter** — On the bearing liner in the front section view (e.g., ⌀50 mm H7). *(1 mark)*
3. **Centre height** — Vertical dimension from the base bottom to the shaft centreline (e.g., 75 mm) — **critical** for shaft alignment. Plus the bolt-centre distance (e.g., 150 mm) on the cap view. *(1 mark)*

**Mark Split for Q1A:**
- 4 marks: Correct drawing (views, sectioning, no-section rules, hatching, item numbers)
- 3 marks: BOM correctness and presentation
- 3 marks: Dimensions and overall technical quality

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Part drawings of a **Protected Type Flange Coupling** are supplied: (i) Driving flange, (ii) Driven flange, (iii) Rubber / Leather bush, (iv) Bolts and nuts (6 sets), (v) Key. Draw the **assembly drawing** in first-angle projection, showing the front view in **full section A-A** and the top view in **external view**. Scale: 1:2.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Draw the **BOM** and add the following dimensions: (i) Overall length and outer diameter of the coupling, (ii) Driving shaft and driven shaft bores, (iii) Bolt pitch circle diameter (BCD), (iv) Key dimensions (width × length).

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Parts inventory:** Driving flange (left, with boss for the driving shaft keyway), driven flange (right, with boss for the driven shaft keyway), rubber/leather bush (between the two flanges, accommodating the bolts), 6 hex bolts and nuts, 1 parallel key (or 2 — one on each shaft).

**Step 2 — Section plane:** A vertical axial plane passing through the bolt axes (to show 2 of the 6 bolts in section and 4 in external view). Full section is used because the coupling is *not* symmetric left-right (driving and driven flanges have different orientations in some designs, but typically identical).

**Step 3 — Construction sequence:**
1. Draw the horizontal shaft axis as the centreline. Mark the total length (e.g., 180 mm) and the outer diameter of the flanges (e.g., ⌀150 mm). *(1 mark)*
2. Draw the left flange (driving) and the right flange (driven) — each as a circular disc with a rectangular boss extending toward the shaft. Apply **45° hatching** to the cut sections of both flanges. *(2 marks)*
3. Draw the rubber/leather bush as a thin annular layer (hatched at **135°** to distinguish from the steel flanges) between the two flange faces. The bush has 6 clearance holes for the bolts. *(1 mark)*
4. Draw the 6 bolts: 2 in section (on the cutting plane) and 4 in external view (front and back of the plane). Bolts are **NOT sectioned** per IS 1503 — they appear in external view with hex heads on the driving flange side and hex nuts on the driven flange side. *(2 marks)*
5. Draw the keys in the shaft keyways — shown in section, with the **key-shaft-hub** relationship clearly visible. *(1 mark)*

**Top view:** Show the same assembly from above, in external view, with the bolt heads visible as hexagons arranged on the bolt pitch circle. The shaft appears as a horizontal rectangle. *(Implicit in step 1–5.)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Bill of Materials:**

| Item No. | Description | Material | Qty | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Driving Flange | Cast Iron FG 260 | 1 | IS 210 |
| 2 | Driven Flange | Cast Iron FG 260 | 1 | Identical to Item 1 |
| 3 | Rubber Bush | Rubber Shore A 70 | 1 | Resilient element |
| 4 | Hex Bolt M12 x 80 | Steel Gr 8.8 | 6 | IS 1364 Part 1 |
| 5 | Hex Nut M12 | Steel Gr 8 | 6 | IS 1364 Part 3 |
| 6 | Parallel Key 14 x 9 x 60 | EN8 Steel | 2 | IS 2292 |

**Dimensions (3 marks):**
1. **Overall length** of the coupling (e.g., 180 mm) and **outer diameter** of the flanges (e.g., ⌀150 mm) on the extreme edges. *(1 mark)*
2. **Shaft bores** for both driving and driven flanges (e.g., ⌀40 mm and ⌀45 mm for two different shaft sizes) — note the bore on the front section view. *(1 mark)*
3. **Bolt pitch circle diameter (BCD)** — shown as a phantom line (chain-dotted) on the top view with a diameter dimension (e.g., ⌀120 mm). Plus the key dimensions (14 mm wide × 9 mm high × 60 mm long) on the shaft boss. *(1 mark)*

**Mark Split for Q1B:**
- 4 marks: Correct drawing and sectioning
- 3 marks: BOM
- 3 marks: Dimensions and BCD reference

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 7 Reasons Students Lose Marks on Assembly Drawing Questions:**
> 1. **Sectioning the shaft** — *Automatic 1–2 mark penalty.* The shaft must remain in external view, no hatching.
> 2. **Hatching the bolts, nuts, and keys** — They are standard parts; IS 1503 mandates they are NOT sectioned.
> 3. **Same hatching angle for different parts** — Adjacent parts in section must differ in angle OR spacing. Two parts hatched identically will be marked as a single continuous part (the examiner cannot distinguish them).
> 4. **Forgetting the BOM item number bubbles** — Each part must have a leader line with a filled circle and an integer. Missing numbers → 0.5 mark per item.
> 5. **Mixing First and Third Angle Projection** — If the question says First Angle, the top view MUST be below the front view. Mixing is a 2-mark penalty.
> 6. **No centre lines** — Every shaft, hole, and bolt axis needs a centre line. Omission is a 0.5 mark deduction per instance.
> 7. **Dimensioning every part on the assembly** — Each part's full manufacturing dimensions belong on the *part drawing*, not the assembly. The assembly shows only overall + key mating + performance dimensions. Over-dimensioning the assembly clutters the drawing and loses marks for "lack of judgement".

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bonus Tip:** The examiner awards **1–2 extra marks** for clean linework, consistent line type usage (Type A 0.6 mm visible, Type B 0.3 mm hidden, Type C 0.15 mm centre/construction), and a well-laid-out title block. Aesthetic discipline is a hidden scoring parameter.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Assembly drawing = master pictorial + BOM + key dimensions.** Three deliverables, never one alone.
- **IS 1503 conventions are law:** shaft not sectioned, bolts not sectioned, mirror hatching for caps, standard parts simplified.
- **Sectioning strategy:** half section for symmetric assemblies (Plummer block, globe valve); full section for asymmetric (some couplings, screw jacks); aligned section for off-axis features.
- **Assembly sequence = inside-out:** shaft first, then bearings, then cap, then bolts. This matches the fitter's actual workflow.
- **BOM placement:** upper-right corner, four mandatory columns (Item No., Description, Material, Qty).
- **Title block placement:** lower-right corner, with First Angle projection symbol (frustum with small end toward viewer for India).
- **Dimension philosophy:** overall + key mating + performance only. Reference dimensions go in [square brackets] or (parentheses).
- **KTU 2024 hot-list of assemblies to master:** Plummer Block, Footstep Bearing, Protected Flange Coupling, Machine Vice, Screw Jack, Globe Valve, Tailstock, Knuckle Joint. Aim for at least 5 of these in your revision.
- **Hatching spacing:** ~4 mm on A3 / A2 sheets, minimum 3 mm, maximum 6 mm.
- **Leader lines:** thin, ending in a filled circle (⦿) or arrow, with sequential integer item numbers.
- **First Angle Projection** is the KTU / Indian Standard convention. Top view goes BELOW the front view.
- **No-section list to memorise:** shafts, bolts, nuts, studs, keys, pins, rivets, balls/rollers of bearings, spokes, webs, ribs.
- **Common mechanical parts to identify by sight:** hex bolt head (regular hex), socket head cap screw (cylindrical with hex socket), wood-screw (tapered shank), set screw (headless or grub), castle nut (slotted top for split pin), spring washer (split ring), lock nut (thin hex), eye bolt (looped head), stud (threaded both ends).
- **Bill of materials ordering:** typically from largest/heaviest part to smallest, OR by assembly sequence (master first, fasteners last). KTU accepts either, but **consistency is scored**.
- **One extra step for top marks:** always verify view alignment by drawing light *projection lines* (45° miter lines) between the front and top views during construction. Erasing them after inking leaves a clean drawing with guaranteed alignment — a KTU examiner's delight.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on Valves, Couplings, Gears and Gear Trains, Belts, Pulleys, Modelling of Robot Grippers, Incorporating Electronic Components in CAD Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRL307 - MACHINE DRAWING AND SOLID MODELLING
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on Valves, Couplings, Gears and Gear Trains, Belts, Pulleys, Modelling of Robot Grippers, Incorporating Electronic Components in CAD Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Standard Machine Components in Engineering Drawing

In KTU 2024 Scheme, the second laboratory experiment module of PCBRL307 focuses on the **orthographic projection, sectional views, and 3D solid modelling** of standard machine elements that form the building blocks of any mechanical system. The exercise is rooted in **IS 696 (Code of Practice for General Engineering Drawings)** and **IS 8000 series** for CAD data exchange.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> Machine drawing and solid modelling of standard components involve the graphical representation of mechanical elements using orthographic projections (first-angle projection as per IS 696), sectional views, and 3D parametric models that capture the engineering intent, tolerances, and assembly relationships.

### 1.2 Component-Wise Conceptual Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Human Body" Metaphor for Machine Components:**
> - **Valves** = The **heart's valves** (control flow of fluid/blood)
> - **Couplings** = **Joints** between bones (connect two rotating shafts)
> - **Gears & Gear Trains** = **Clockwork gears** (transmit motion with speed/torque change)
> - **Belts & Pulleys** = **Bicycle chain-and-sprocket** (friction-based power transmission)
> - **Robot Grippers** = The **human hand** (end-effector for grasping)
> - **Electronic Components in CAD** = The **nervous system** (control and feedback)

### 1.3 Detailed Component Definitions

#### (a) Valves
A **valve** is a mechanical device that controls the flow of fluid (liquid, gas, slurry) by opening, closing, or partially obstructing passageways. In machine drawing, the focus is on:
- **Stop valve / Globe valve** — used for throttling
- **Gate valve** — used for on/off isolation
- **Check valve / Non-return valve** — allows flow in one direction
- **Ball valve** — uses a hollow ball for quick shut-off
- **Safety/Pressure Relief Valve** — opens at a set pressure

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Conventions in KTU Drawings:**
> - Pipe internal diameter shown as continuous thick line
> - Pipe external diameter (when pipe is small) shown by two parallel thin lines
> - Hidden features in conventional valves (e.g., internal stem threads) shown in section only

#### (b) Couplings
A **coupling** is a device used to connect two shafts end-to-end for transmitting torque, with or without allowing misalignment. Two broad categories:

- **Rigid Couplings:** Sleeve/Muff coupling, Clamp/Compression coupling, Flange coupling
- **Flexible Couplings:** Bushed-pin coupling, Universal coupling (Hooke's joint), Oldham coupling, Gear coupling, Disc/Bevel coil-spring flexible coupling

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Drawing Convention:** Always show the **sectional view** when internal features (bolts, pins, rubber bushes, keys) are present. The half-sectional or full-sectional view is mandatory for flange and muff couplings.

#### (c) Gears and Gear Trains
A **gear** is a rotating machine part having cut teeth which mesh with another toothed part to transmit torque. Standard KTU syllabus covers:
- **Spur gears** (parallel shafts)
- **Helical gears** (parallel or non-parallel, crossed)
- **Bevel gears** (intersecting shafts)
- **Worm and worm wheel** (non-intersecting perpendicular shafts)

A **gear train** is a series of gears that transmit motion from one shaft to another.

#### (d) Belts and Pulleys
- **Belt** — a flexible loop of material that links two rotating shafts mechanically
- **Pulley** — a wheel on a shaft with a grooved rim for a belt
- Types: Flat belt, V-belt, Timing (toothed) belt, Round belt

#### (e) Robot Grippers
End-effectors of robotic manipulators that physically interact with workpieces. Types in KTU scope:
- Mechanical grippers (jaw/parallel, angular, three-finger)
- Vacuum grippers
- Magnetic grippers
- Servo/Adaptive grippers

#### (f) Electronic Components in CAD Models
Refers to integrating PCB, sensors, actuators, connectors, cables, and ICs into a 3D CAD assembly while maintaining:
- **Mechanical fit** (no interference)
- **Thermal management** considerations
- **Serviceability** (access for replacement)
- **Ergonomic and EMC** layout

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard metrics to remember:**
> - Module: $m = d/T$ (in **mm**)
> - Standard modules (IS 2535): **1, 1.25, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10 mm**
> - Pressure rating of standard valves: **PN 6, PN 10, PN 16, PN 25, PN 40** (bar)
> - Standard V-belt sections: **A, B, C, D, E** (IS 2494)

### 1.4 Intuitive Summary Table

| Component | Function | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|
| Valve | Fluid flow control | Kitchen tap, gas cylinder regulator |
| Rigid Coupling | Permanent shaft connection | Motor-pump shaft in industry |
| Flexible Coupling | Tolerates misalignment | Drive shaft in cars |
| Gear | Positive power transmission | Car gearbox |
| Belt-Pulley | Friction-based transmission | Ceiling fan, washing machine |
| Robot Gripper | Pick-and-place operation | Factory pick-and-place arm |
| Electronic Component in CAD | Electromechanical integration | Drone, smartphone assembly |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Valves — Theoretical Breakdown

**Why are valves drawn in section?**
- To reveal internal bore, seat, disc, stem, and gland packing
- Hidden thread on stem (square/ACME thread) is shown as per IS 4693
- The handwheel and yoke are external features, drawn in elevation

**Operational Logic of a Globe/Stop Valve:**
1. Handwheel rotates → 2. Stem translates linearly → 3. Disc moves down/up → 4. Disc lifts off/seats on the body seat → 5. Flow opens/closes

**Standard Proportions in Drawing:**
- Body height ≈ 1.5 × nominal bore
- Stem diameter ≈ 0.1 × nominal bore (for low pressure)
- Handwheel diameter ≈ 4–5 × stem diameter

### 2.2 Couplings — Theoretical Breakdown

#### (a) Sleeve / Muff Coupling
A hollow cylinder (sleeve/muff) fits over the ends of two shafts. Two setscrews (or one set screw + one key) lock the sleeve to both shafts.

**Design Formulae (IS 2494-based):**
$$d = \text{Shaft diameter (mm)}$$
$$D = 2d + 13 \text{ mm (outer diameter of sleeve)}$$
$$L = 3.5d \text{ (length of sleeve, standard)}$$
$$t = 0.3d \text{ to } 0.4d \text{ (thickness of flange for clamp coupling)}$$

#### (b) Flange Coupling
Two flanges are keyed to the shafts and bolted together through a series of bolts. For **protected type**, the bolt heads/nuts sit inside recesses; for **unprotected type**, they protrude.

**Key Formulae:**
$$D_1 = d + 50 \text{ mm (P.C.D. of bolts)}$$
$$D_2 = d + 100 \text{ mm (outer flange diameter)}$$
$$n = 3, 4, 6 \text{ (number of bolts, based on d)}$$
$$\text{Bolt diameter } d_b = 0.5d \text{ to } 0.6d$$
$$t_f = 0.5d \text{ (flange thickness, unprotected)}$$

### 2.3 Gears & Gear Trains — High-Yield Theory

#### Spur Gear Nomenclature
- **Pitch circle diameter (d):** The imaginary circle on which two gears effectively roll
- **Module (m):** $m = d/T$ where $T$ is number of teeth
- **Addendum (a):** $1 \times m$ (radial distance from pitch circle to tip)
- **Dedendum (b):** $1.25 \times m$ (radial distance from pitch circle to root)
- **Circular pitch (p_c):** $\pi \cdot m$
- **Tooth thickness & space:** Equal at pitch circle = $p_c/2$

#### Gear Train Speed Ratio
$$\text{Speed ratio} = i = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{T_1}{T_2} = \frac{d_1}{d_2} = \frac{d_2}{d_1} \text{ (compound trains)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** Spur gears are drawn in **section** only when keyway, hub, or web details must be shown. Otherwise, the simplified two-line representation (pitch circle + addendum circle + dedendum circle) is acceptable.

### 2.4 Belt & Pulley Theory

**Belt Drive Geometry:**

Let $D$ = large pulley diameter, $d$ = small pulley diameter, $C$ = center distance, $\alpha$ = angle of lap (wrap angle) on small pulley.

**Length of open belt (standard formula):**
$$L = 2C + \frac{\pi (D+d)}{2} + \frac{(D-d)^2}{4C}$$

**Angle of lap on smaller pulley:**
$$\alpha = \pi - 2\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D-d}{2C}\right) \text{ radians}$$

$$\alpha = 180° - 2\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D-d}{2C}\right) \text{ degrees}$$

**Angle of lap on larger pulley:**
$$\beta = \pi + 2\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D-d}{2C}\right)$$

**Belt velocity:**
$$v = \frac{\pi d N_1}{60} \text{ m/s}$$

**Velocity ratio:**
$$VR = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{D}{d} \text{ (neglecting slip)}$$

**Power transmitted:**
$$P = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v \text{ Watts}$$

Where $T_1$ = tension in tight side, $T_2$ = tension in slack side.

**Centrifugal tension:**
$$T_c = m \cdot v^2 \text{ (N, with m in kg/m and v in m/s)}$$

### 2.5 Robot Gripper Modelling Theory

**Two main types in scope:**
1. **Parallel-jaw gripper (linear translation of jaws):** Stroke length = 30–100 mm
2. **Angular (swing) gripper:** Jaws rotate about a pivot, opening angle 30°–60°

**Design Parameters to Model:**
- Jaw length, jaw width, jaw thickness
- Pin diameters for pivots
- Stroke/angle of opening
- Force transmission linkage (4-bar, toggle, cam)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Practical Expectation:** Students must model the gripper assembly in any CAD software (SolidWorks/Fusion 360/CATIA) using **parametric features** (extrude, revolve, sweep, loft) and produce an **exploded isometric view** with a **Bill of Materials (BOM)**.

### 2.6 Electronic Components in CAD Models

**Why integrate electronics in CAD?**
- **Mechatronic design validation** — detect interferences between PCB and enclosure
- **DFM (Design for Manufacturing)** — proper cable routing
- **EMC/EMI compliance** — separation of power and signal traces
- **Serviceability** — access for replacement of components

**Common electronic components in CAD assemblies:**
- **PCB** (rigid/flex) with mounting holes
- **Connectors** (USB, HDMI, JST, screw terminals)
- **Sensors** (temperature, pressure, IMU)
- **Actuators** (DC motors, servo motors, solenoids)
- **Cables & harnesses**
- **Heat sinks, fans**
- **IC chips, capacitors, resistors (3D models from vendor STEP/IGES files)**

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL: KTU High-Yield Formula Table** (use `\vert` for absolute value to avoid markdown breaking)

| # | Topic | Formula | Variables / Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Module (Gear) | $m = d / T$ | $d$ in **mm**, $T$ dimensionless | Gear design |
| 2 | Pitch diameter | $d = m \cdot T$ | mm | Gear specification |
| 3 | Addendum | $a = m$ | mm | Tooth height |
| 4 | Dedendum | $b = 1.25m$ | mm | Tooth root depth |
| 5 | Center distance | $C = (d_1 + d_2)/2$ | mm | Gear mounting |
| 6 | Speed ratio | $i = N_1 / N_2 = T_1 / T_2$ | dimensionless | Gear train |
| 7 | Train value (compound) | $i = \frac{\text{Product of drivers}}{\text{Product of driven}}$ | dimensionless | Compound train |
| 8 | Belt length (open) | $L = 2C + \pi(D+d)/2 + (D-d)^2/(4C)$ | mm | Belt selection |
| 9 | Wrap angle (small) | $\alpha = 180° - 2\sin^{-1}\!\big((D-d)/(2C)\big)$ | degrees | Belt tensioning |
| 10 | Belt velocity | $v = \pi d N_1 / 60$ | m/s | Power transmission |
| 11 | Centrifugal tension | $T_c = m v^2$ | N | Belt design |
| 12 | Power transmitted | $P = (T_1 - T_2) v$ | Watts | Belt drive design |
| 13 | Coupling sleeve OD | $D = 2d + 13$ | mm | Sleeve coupling |
| 14 | Sleeve length | $L = 3.5d$ | mm | Sleeve coupling |
| 15 | Flange PCD | $D_1 = d + 50$ | mm | Flange coupling |
| 16 | Flange OD | $D_2 = d + 100$ | mm | Flange coupling |
| 17 | Flange thickness | $t_f = 0.5d$ | mm | Flange coupling |
| 18 | Valve body height | $H \approx 1.5 \times \text{NS}$ | mm | Stop valve drawing |
| 19 | Handwheel dia | $D_{hw} \approx 4 d_{stem}$ | mm | Valve drawing |
| 20 | Gripper stroke | $S = 2 L_{jaw} \sin(\theta/2)$ | mm | Angular gripper |
| 21 | Gripper force ratio | $F_{jaw}/F_{cyl} = 1/\tan(\phi)$ | dimensionless | Toggle mechanism |
| 22 | Gear contact ratio | $\varepsilon = \sqrt{R_a^2 - R_b^2} + \sqrt{r_a^2 - r_b^2} - C\sin\phi / (p_b \cos\phi)$ | dimensionless | Tooth contact |
| 23 | Helical lead | $L = \pi d / \tan(\beta)$ | mm | Helical gear |
| 24 | Bevel gear ratio | $i = \sin\gamma_1 / \sin\gamma_2$ | dimensionless | Bevel gear train |
| 25 | Module on bevel gear | $m = d_m / T$ (at mean dia) | mm | Bevel design |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Open Belt Length Formula

This is a **KTU frequently asked derivation** (8 marks category).

**Starting Point:** Consider two pulleys of diameters $D$ (large) and $d$ (small) with center distance $C$. The belt wraps around both pulleys with straight portions connecting them.

**Step 1:** Define the geometry.

Let $A$ and $B$ be the centers of the two pulleys. The tangent points on the two pulleys where straight portions of belt leave them are $P, Q$ (from large pulley) and $R, S$ (from small pulley).

**Step 2:** Express the angle subtended.

The angle $\angle PAQ$ at the center of the large pulley (on the side where belt leaves) is:
$$\angle PAQ = 2 \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D - d}{2C}\right)$$

This is because in triangle $ABP$, the perpendicular from $B$ to $AP$ gives a right triangle with hypotenuse $C$ and the projection difference $(D-d)/2$.

**Step 3:** The arc length on large pulley (outside) is:
$$L_D = \pi D - 2 \cdot \frac{D}{2} \cdot \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D - d}{2C}\right)$$

Wait — careful. The arc of contact on the **large pulley** is the **larger** arc. So:
$$L_D = \pi D + D \cdot \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D - d}{2C}\right)$$

**Step 4:** The arc of contact on the small pulley is:
$$L_d = \pi d - d \cdot \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D - d}{2C}\right)$$

**Step 5:** The straight portions each have length:
$$\sqrt{C^2 - \left(\frac{D - d}{2}\right)^2}$$

There are **two** straight portions, so total straight length is:
$$L_{straight} = 2 \sqrt{C^2 - \frac{(D-d)^2}{4}}$$

**Step 6:** Total belt length:
$$L = 2 \sqrt{C^2 - \frac{(D-d)^2}{4}} + \pi D + D \cdot \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D-d}{2C}\right) + \pi d - d \cdot \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D-d}{2C}\right)$$

**Step 7:** Combine the circular terms:
$$L = 2 \sqrt{C^2 - \frac{(D-d)^2}{4}} + \pi (D + d)/2 + (D - d) \cdot \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D-d}{2C}\right) + \pi (D+d)/2$$

Wait, let me redo this more carefully. The standard derivation uses the **sine expansion** for small angles when the angle is small (typical engineering case where $C \gg D-d$).

**Standard textbook derivation (Khurmi/Gupta):**

**Step 1:** Let $\angle BAP = \theta$. In right triangle $BNP$ (where $N$ is foot of perpendicular from $B$ to $AP$):
$$\sin \theta = \frac{(D-d)/2}{C}$$

**Step 2:** The straight portion length is:
$$AP = \sqrt{C^2 - \left(\frac{D-d}{2}\right)^2}$$

Expanding by binomial theorem (since $(D-d)/(2C)$ is small):
$$AP = C \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{D-d}{2C}\right)^2} \approx C \left[1 - \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{D-d}{2C}\right)^2\right] = C - \frac{(D-d)^2}{8C}$$

**Step 3:** Total length of two straight portions = $2AP$:
$$L_{straight} = 2C - \frac{(D-d)^2}{4C}$$

**Step 4:** Length of belt in contact with larger pulley (the longer arc):
$$L_D = \pi D + 2\theta \cdot \frac{D}{2} = \pi D + D\theta$$

**Step 5:** Length of belt in contact with smaller pulley (the shorter arc):
$$L_d = \pi d - 2\theta \cdot \frac{d}{2} = \pi d - d\theta$$

**Step 6:** Total belt length:
$$L = L_{straight} + L_D + L_d$$

$$L = 2C - \frac{(D-d)^2}{4C} + \pi D + D\theta + \pi d - d\theta$$

**Step 7:** Substitute $\theta \approx \sin\theta = (D-d)/(2C)$ (small angle approximation):
$$L = 2C - \frac{(D-d)^2}{4C} + \pi(D + d) + (D - d) \cdot \frac{D - d}{2C}$$

$$L = 2C - \frac{(D-d)^2}{4C} + \pi(D + d) + \frac{(D-d)^2}{2C}$$

**Step 8:** Combine the squared terms:
$$-\frac{(D-d)^2}{4C} + \frac{(D-d)^2}{2C} = +\frac{(D-d)^2}{4C}$$

**Step 9:** Final result:
$$L = 2C + \frac{\pi (D + d)}{2} + \frac{(D-d)^2}{4C}$$

$$\boxed{L = 2C + \frac{\pi (D + d)}{2} + \frac{(D-d)^2}{4C}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification with units:** All terms are in mm. $C$ in mm, $D$ and $d$ in mm, so $L$ in mm. Correct.

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-type):**

Given: $D = 600$ mm, $d = 300$ mm, $C = 1200$ mm. Find belt length.

$$L = 2(1200) + \frac{\pi (600 + 300)}{2} + \frac{(600 - 300)^2}{4 \times 1200}$$

$$L = 2400 + \frac{\pi \times 900}{2} + \frac{90000}{4800}$$

$$L = 2400 + 450\pi + 18.75$$

$$L = 2400 + 1413.72 + 18.75 = 3832.47 \text{ mm} \approx 3.83 \text{ m}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Speed Ratio of Compound Gear Train

**Setup:** A series of gears with the following tooth counts: $T_1$ (driver on input shaft), $T_2$ (driven on intermediate shaft 1), $T_3$ (driver on intermediate shaft 1), $T_4$ (driven on intermediate shaft 2), and so on.

**Step 1:** For each mesh pair, the speed ratio is:
$$i_{12} = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{T_2}{T_1} \quad \text{(because driver and driven rotate in opposite directions, but teeth ratio determines speed)}$$

Actually, the standard convention: $N_1 / N_2 = T_2 / T_1$ since the linear velocity at the pitch circle is the same.

**Step 2:** For the compound train:
$$\frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{T_2}{T_1} \quad ; \quad \frac{N_3}{N_4} = \frac{T_4}{T_3}$$

Note: $N_2 = N_3$ (same shaft), so:
$$\frac{N_1}{N_4} = \frac{N_1}{N_2} \cdot \frac{N_3}{N_4} = \frac{T_2 \cdot T_4}{T_1 \cdot T_3}$$

**General form:**
$$\boxed{\text{Train value} = i = \frac{\text{Product of driven tooth counts}}{\text{Product of driver tooth counts}} = \frac{N_{in}}{N_{out}}}$$

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example: Flange Coupling Design

**Problem:** A flange coupling is to connect two shafts of diameter $d = 50$ mm transmitting 25 kW at 250 rpm. Find the main dimensions as per standard proportions.

**Step 1: Shaft diameter verification**
$$P = \frac{2\pi N T}{60\,000} \Rightarrow T = \frac{P \times 60\,000}{2 \pi N}$$

$$T = \frac{25\,000 \times 60\,000}{2 \pi \times 250} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^9}{1570.8} = 9.55 \times 10^5 \text{ N·mm} = 955 \text{ N·m}$$

This is consistent with $d = 50$ mm (torque capacity of mild steel shaft $\approx 0.5 \times 10^6$ N·mm is fine for 50 mm).

**Step 2: Flange PCD**
$$D_1 = d + 50 = 50 + 50 = 100 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Outer flange diameter**
$$D_2 = d + 100 = 50 + 100 = 150 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Number of bolts (for d = 50 mm, standard: 4 bolts)**
$$n = 4$$

**Step 5: Bolt diameter**
$$d_b = 0.5d = 0.5 \times 50 = 25 \text{ mm (use M24 standard)}$$

**Step 6: Flange thickness (unprotected type)**
$$t_f = 0.5d = 0.5 \times 50 = 25 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 7: Hub length**
$$L_h = 1.5d = 75 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 8: Key dimensions** (square key for d = 50 mm)
$$w = h = d/6 = 8.33 \approx 10 \text{ mm}; \quad L_k = 1.5d = 75 \text{ mm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Flange Coupling Drawing Specifications:**
> - Shaft: 50 mm
> - PCD: 100 mm, with 4 × M24 bolts at 90° spacing
> - Flange OD: 150 mm
> - Flange thickness: 25 mm
> - Hub length: 75 mm
> - Square key: 10 × 10 × 75 mm
> - Chisel edge / tapper on bolt for tightening

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Component-Wise Drawing Standards

| Component | Section View Required? | Hidden Lines to be Omitted? | Standard Section Plane | Special Notations |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Globe valve | Yes (full section) | Hidden threads in non-sectioned views | Vertical through stem axis | Hatching of body, but NOT of stem, disc, or seat |
| Sleeve coupling | Yes (half section) | Yes, in the half-not-sectioned portion | Horizontal through axis | Show set screw in section |
| Flange coupling | Yes (full or half section) | Yes | Horizontal through axis | Show bolts in section; hatching of flanges opposite directions |
| Spur gear | Hub region only | Yes, in non-sectioned web | Through keyway | Tooth profile NOT sectioned (conventional break) |
| Bevel gear | Section through axis | Yes | Vertical through shaft axis | Show pitch cone angle |
| V-belt drive | No section (assembly view) | Yes | N/A (use end view) | Show belt groove in sectioned pulley |
| Robot gripper | Yes (full assembly section) | Yes | Through jaw pivot axis | Show spring and linkages in section |
| PCB in assembly | Section optional | Yes | Horizontal cut | Components not sectioned individually |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **What is the standard formula for the pitch circle diameter of a spur gear, and what is the meaning of the "module"?**

**Model Answer:**
The pitch circle diameter $d$ of a spur gear is given by:
$$d = m \cdot T$$
where $m$ is the **module** (in mm) and $T$ is the number of teeth. The module $m$ is defined as:
$$m = \frac{d}{T} = \frac{p_c}{\pi}$$
where $p_c$ is the circular pitch. The module represents the size of the gear tooth — larger modules indicate bigger, stronger teeth. Standard modules as per **IS 2535** are: **1, 1.25, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10 mm**.

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **List any three standard proportions used in the drawing of a flange coupling for a shaft of diameter $d$.**

**Model Answer:**
For a flange coupling on a shaft of diameter $d$:
1. **Pitch Circle Diameter (PCD) of bolts:** $D_1 = d + 50$ mm
2. **Outer Flange Diameter:** $D_2 = d + 100$ mm
3. **Flange thickness (unprotected type):** $t_f = 0.5d$
4. **Bolt diameter:** $d_b = 0.5d$ to $0.6d$
5. **Hub length:** $L_h = 1.5d$

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **(a)** Draw the **orthographic sectional views** of a **sleeve (muff) coupling** connecting two shafts of diameter **40 mm**. The two shafts are to be locked to the sleeve by **set screws** and **keys**. Use standard proportions. **(7 marks)**

> **(b)** A **flat belt** connects two pulleys of diameters **500 mm** and **300 mm** with a center distance of **1500 mm**. Calculate the **(i)** length of the belt, **(ii)** angle of lap on the smaller pulley, and **(iii)** angle of lap on the larger pulley. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — Sleeve Coupling Drawing:**

**Given:** $d = 40$ mm.

**Standard Dimensions (as per IS / Khurmi):**
- Outer diameter of sleeve: $D = 2d + 13 = 80 + 13 = 93$ mm
- Length of sleeve: $L = 3.5d = 3.5 \times 40 = 140$ mm
- Set screw diameter: $0.25d = 10$ mm (use M10)
- Set screw length: $0.75d = 30$ mm
- Key size: $w \times h = d/6 \times d/6 = 6.67 \times 6.67 \approx 8 \times 7$ mm

**Steps to draw the sectional view:**
1. Draw the **two shafts** of 40 mm diameter along the horizontal centerline.
2. Draw the **sleeve (muff)** of 93 mm OD and 140 mm length covering the shaft ends equally (70 mm each).
3. Project the **top half-view** (showing external cylindrical surface) and the **bottom half-view** (showing section).
4. In the section, show the **internal bore** of the sleeve (40 mm) matching the shaft.
5. Show the **keys** (8 × 7) on both shafts, embedded half in the shaft and half in the sleeve.
6. Show the **set screws** at 90° to the keys (radial), with their conical tips pressing on the shaft.
7. Apply **sectional hatching** to the sleeve and shaft (cut metal).
8. Add the **centerline**, dimensions, and title block.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — Belt Drive Calculations:**

**Given:** $D = 500$ mm, $d = 300$ mm, $C = 1500$ mm.

**(i) Length of belt (open belt):**
$$L = 2C + \frac{\pi(D+d)}{2} + \frac{(D-d)^2}{4C}$$

$$L = 2(1500) + \frac{\pi(500 + 300)}{2} + \frac{(500 - 300)^2}{4 \times 1500}$$

$$L = 3000 + \frac{800\pi}{2} + \frac{40000}{6000}$$

$$L = 3000 + 400\pi + 6.667$$

$$L = 3000 + 1256.64 + 6.67 = 4263.31 \text{ mm} \approx 4.26 \text{ m}$$

**(ii) Angle of lap on smaller pulley ($d = 300$ mm):**
$$\alpha = 180° - 2\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D - d}{2C}\right)$$

$$\alpha = 180° - 2\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{500 - 300}{2 \times 1500}\right)$$

$$\alpha = 180° - 2\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{200}{3000}\right) = 180° - 2\sin^{-1}(0.0667)$$

$$\alpha = 180° - 2 \times 3.823° = 180° - 7.65° = 172.35°$$

$$\boxed{\alpha \approx 172.35°}$$

**(iii) Angle of lap on larger pulley ($D = 500$ mm):**
$$\beta = 180° + 2\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D-d}{2C}\right) = 180° + 7.65° = 187.65°$$

$$\boxed{\beta \approx 187.65°}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Belt Length Calculation:**
> Students often forget the **last term** $\dfrac{(D-d)^2}{4C}$ in the belt length formula, leading to incorrect answers and **3-mark loss**. Also, do not confuse the open belt formula with the **crossed belt** formula. For a **crossed belt** the formula is:
> $$L_{crossed} = 2C + \frac{\pi(D+d)}{2} + \frac{(D+d)^2}{4C}$$
> Note the **sum** $(D+d)^2$ instead of **difference** $(D-d)^2$.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **(a)** Two shafts of diameter **50 mm** are to be connected by a **protected-type flange coupling**. The coupling uses **4 bolts**. Draw the **half-sectional front view** and the **side view** showing all key dimensions as per standard proportions. **(7 marks)**

> **(b)** A **gear train** consists of three gears: $T_1 = 20$, $T_2 = 40$, $T_3 = 25$, $T_4 = 50$. Gear 2 and Gear 3 are on the same intermediate shaft. The input speed is 1500 rpm. Find the **(i)** train value, **(ii)** output speed, **(iii)** whether the output shaft rotates in the same direction as the input. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — Flange Coupling Drawing:**

**Given:** $d = 50$ mm, protected type, 4 bolts.

**Calculated Dimensions:**
- PCD of bolts: $D_1 = d + 50 = 100$ mm
- Outer flange diameter: $D_2 = d + 100 = 150$ mm
- Flange thickness: $t_f = 0.5d = 25$ mm
- Hub length: $L_h = 1.5d = 75$ mm
- Hub diameter: $D_{hub} = 2d = 100$ mm
- Bolt circle spacing: $360°/4 = 90°$ between bolts
- Bolt diameter: $0.5d = 25$ mm (use M24)
- Key: square $w = h = d/6 = 8.33$ mm, round to **10 × 8 mm**

**Drawing Steps:**
1. Draw the horizontal centerline and the two shaft ends (50 mm) extending into the hubs.
2. In the **half-sectional front view**:
   - Top half: external view showing the cylindrical flanges and bolt heads/nuts.
   - Bottom half: section showing the bolts, the keys, the hubs, and the shaft cross-section.
3. Apply hatching to the **flange, hub, and shaft** (cut metal). Hatching directions of the two flanges must be **opposite** to indicate they are separate parts.
4. In the **side view** (end view of the right flange):
   - Outer circle: $D_2 = 150$ mm
   - Inner circle: shaft (50 mm)
   - Bolt circle: $D_1 = 100$ mm, with 4 holes of M24 clearance (≈ 26 mm dia) at 90° spacing
5. Add dimensions: shaft Ø50, PCD Ø100, flange OD Ø150, hub length 75, key size, number of bolts (4 × M24).

---

**Model Solution for (b) — Compound Gear Train:**

**Given:** $T_1 = 20$, $T_2 = 40$, $T_3 = 25$, $T_4 = 50$, $N_1 = 1500$ rpm. Gears 2 and 3 are on the same intermediate shaft.

**Step 1: Identify the meshes.**
- Mesh 1: Gears 1 and 2 (T₁ drives T₂)
- Mesh 2: Gears 3 and 4 (T₃ drives T₄)
- Gears 2 and 3 are mounted on the **same intermediate shaft**, so $N_2 = N_3$.

**Step 2: Train value.**
$$\text{Train value} = i = \frac{N_1}{N_4} = \frac{T_2 \times T_4}{T_1 \times T_3}$$

$$i = \frac{40 \times 50}{20 \times 25} = \frac{2000}{500} = 4$$

$$\boxed{i = 4 \text{ (speed reduction)}}$$

**Step 3: Output speed.**
$$N_4 = \frac{N_1}{i} = \frac{1500}{4} = 375 \text{ rpm}$$

$$\boxed{N_4 = 375 \text{ rpm}}$$

**Step 4: Direction of rotation.**
- Mesh 1: external mesh → reverses direction. So if $N_1$ is clockwise, $N_2$ is counter-clockwise.
- Mesh 2: external mesh → reverses direction again. So $N_3$ (CCW) drives $N_4$ (CW).
- **Net effect:** $N_4$ rotates in the **same direction** as $N_1$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Gear Train Direction:**
> For an **external mesh**, the driven gear rotates in the **opposite** direction. For an **internal (annular) mesh**, the driven gear rotates in the **same** direction. For a **compound train** with two external meshes, the output rotates in the **same** direction as the input. Students often miscount and incorrectly conclude "opposite direction."

---

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones (KTU Examiner Insights):**
> 1. **Forgetting the section hatching direction rule** in flange coupling — both flanges must have **opposing hatching** since they are two separate parts bolted together. Wrong hatching = **2 marks lost**.
> 2. **Not labeling the key** in the section — if you draw a key in the section, you must dimension it (width × height × length).
> 3. **Forgetting the centerline** in any rotational component drawing — mandatory per IS 696.
> 4. **Missing hidden lines** in the non-sectioned view of a half-section.
> 5. **Not stating the standard (IS code)** used for proportions — e.g., "IS 2535 for gear module" or "IS 696 for general drawing practice."
> 6. **In CAD modelling part**: forgetting the **Bill of Materials (BOM)** with item numbers, quantities, and material.
> 7. **In robot gripper modelling**: forgetting to show the **range of motion** in the assembled view (open and closed positions).
> 8. **In belt drive**: using the **crossed-belt formula by mistake** — the problem statement clearly says "open belt."

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 2 — Final Cheat Sheet**

- [ ] **First-angle projection** is mandatory in Indian drawings (IS 696).
- [ ] **Module (m)** is the standard parameter for gear teeth sizing; $d = mT$, $a = m$, $b = 1.25m$.
- [ ] Standard modules per **IS 2535**: 1, 1.25, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10.
- [ ] **Sleeve coupling:** $D = 2d + 13$, $L = 3.5d$.
- [ ] **Flange coupling (protected type):** $D_1 = d + 50$, $D_2 = d + 100$, $t_f = 0.5d$, $d_b = 0.5d$.
- [ ] **Flange coupling hatching direction** must be **opposite** for the two flanges.
- [ ] **Belt length (open):** $L = 2C + \pi(D+d)/2 + (D-d)^2/(4C)$.
- [ ] **Belt length (crossed):** $L = 2C + \pi(D+d)/2 + (D+d)^2/(4C)$.
- [ ] **Angle of lap (small pulley, open belt):** $\alpha = 180° - 2\sin^{-1}((D-d)/(2C))$.
- [ ] **Belt velocity:** $v = \pi d N_1 / 60$ m/s.
- [ ] **Centrifugal tension:** $T_c = m v^2$ (m = belt mass per unit length).
- [ ] **Gear train value:** $i = $ (product of driven) / (product of driver).
- [ ] **Two external meshes** in a compound train = **same direction** output.
- [ ] **One external + one internal** mesh = **opposite direction** output.
- [ ] **Robot gripper** types: parallel-jaw, angular, three-finger, vacuum, magnetic.
- [ ] **Gripper force amplification** in toggle mechanism: $F_{jaw} = F_{cyl} / \tan\phi$.
- [ ] **CAD BOM** must contain: item number, description, quantity, material, part number.
- [ ] **Interference check** is mandatory before submitting any CAD assembly.
- [ ] **Electronic components in CAD** require real 3D STEP/IGES models from vendors, with proper **mounting holes** and **connector orientation**.
- [ ] **Cable routing** in 3D assemblies requires dedicated channels and bend radius $\geq 3 \times$ cable diameter.
- [ ] **GD&T** (Geometric Dimensioning & Tolerancing) per **ASME Y14.5** / **ISO 1101** is expected on all dimensioned parts.
- [ ] **Sectional view** in valves shows: body, seat, disc, stem, gland packing, handwheel.
- [ ] **Spur gear in section** shows hub, keyway, and web arm — **not** the individual teeth (use conventional break).
- [ ] **Bevel gear in section** shows the **pitch cone** and the **shaft axis** at the correct cone angle.
- [ ] **V-belt** grooves: standard sections are A, B, C, D, E (IS 2494).
- [ ] **Centerline** must pass through every axis of rotation in every drawing.
- [ ] **Title block** must contain: name of part, name of student, scale, sheet number, drawing number, projection symbol, institution name.
- [ ] **All dimensions** must be in **mm** unless otherwise stated; tolerances as per **IS 2102** (e.g., medium class ±0.2 mm).

---

> **End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — PCBRL307 KTU 2024 Scheme**
> **Recommended Next Study:** Module Experiment 3 — *Exercises on Design of Jigs, Fixtures, and Press Tools, 3D Printing Preparation, Simulation of Manufacturing Processes*.
