topics.title: Creating Assembly Models of Socket and Spigot Joint, Knuckle Joint, Rigid Flange Couplings, Bushed Pin Flexible Coupling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRL307 - MACHINE DRAWING AND SOLID MODELLING
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Creating Assembly Models of Socket and Spigot Joint, Knuckle Joint, Rigid Flange Couplings, Bushed Pin Flexible Coupling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Assembly Modelling in CAD

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *Assembly Modelling* is the process of combining multiple individual 3D part models into a single, functionally accurate virtual product by applying geometric relationships called **mates** (constraints). The resulting model preserves the relative degrees of freedom (DOF) of all components as defined by the designer.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PCBRL307**, Assembly Modelling is the **Core Activity** of Module Experiment 2. Students are expected to take pre-modelled standard machine components and assemble them using a parametric CAD platform (typically **SolidWorks**, **CATIA**, **Creo Parametric**, or **Fusion 360**).

### Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Origami to Jigsaw" Shift.**
> When you build a 3D part, you are essentially folding a single sheet of paper into a shape (Origami). When you perform *Assembly Modelling*, you shift from origami to a **jigsaw puzzle**. Each piece (part) is independently created, and the puzzle (assembly) only becomes meaningful when you snap the pieces together using their **edges, faces, and axes** — in CAD, these snaps are called *Mates*. A socket-spigot joint is like inserting a pen cap (spigot) into a bottle neck (socket) — they must be **concentric** and **coaxial**.

### Standard Industrial Metrics for Machine Joints

| Parameter | Unit | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Standard Shaft Diameter ($d$)** | **mm** | The base dimension from which all joint proportions are derived |
| **Tolerances (ISO 286)** | **µm** | Dimensional limits for fit (e.g., H7/g6 for clearance) |
| **Surface Finish (Ra)** | **µm** | Roughness value for sliding/screwed surfaces |
| **Drafting Standard** | **SP 46 (BIS) / ISO 128** | Line types, dimensioning, and sectioning conventions |

## 1.2 Overview of the Four Joints / Couplings in Experiment 2

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** All four assemblies belong to the family of **permanent or semi-permanent mechanical joints** that transmit **rotational power** or **axial force** between machine elements.

1. **Socket and Spigot Joint** — A pipe/cylindrical fitting where one member (spigot) is inserted into another (socket) and locked by a caulking or welding operation. Used in **steam, water, and air pipelines**.

2. **Knuckle Joint** — A pin-and-eyelet linkage used to transmit **axial tensile or compressive loads** with the ability to swivel. Used in **tie rods, valve linkages, and lever arms**.

3. **Rigid Flange Coupling** — A two-flange (disc) coupling bolted together, used to **rigidly connect two aligned shafts** for transmitting torque. Used in **pumps, compressors, and line shafts**.

4. **Bushed Pin Flexible Coupling** — A modified flange coupling where **rubber or leather bushes** are placed over the pins, providing **misalignment tolerance and shock absorption**. Used in **motors, generators, and machine tool drives**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Assembly Modelling Workflow (Universal CAD Pipeline)

Every assembly model in KTU lab examinations is created following this **5-Stage Workflow**:

1. **Part Creation (Part Module):** Each component is modelled as a separate `.sldprt` / `.ipt` / `.fcstd` file using 2D sketches + features (Extrude, Revolve, Fillet, Chamfer, Hole Wizard).
2. **File Insertion (Assembly Module):** All individual parts are inserted into the assembly file (`.sldasm` / `.iam` / `.f3z`).
3. **Mate Application (Constraint Solving):** Mates are applied sequentially to establish relative positions.
4. **Interference Check (Validation):** The CAD engine verifies that no part geometry overlaps illegally.
5. **Exploded View & BOM (Documentation):** An exploded view is generated, and a **Bill of Materials (BOM)** is extracted for the drawing sheet.

> [!NOTE]
> **Degrees of Freedom (DOF) Concept:** A free body in 3D space has **6 DOF** (3 translations: X, Y, Z and 3 rotations: roll, pitch, yaw). Each standard mate (Coincident, Concentric, Coincident, etc.) removes specific DOF. A fully constrained assembly has **0 DOF** for all parts (this is the KTU lab validation target).

## 2.2 Standard Mates (Constraints) Used in the Four Assemblies

| Mate Type | DOF Removed | Used In (Examples) | Visual Intuition |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **Coincident** | 2 translational | Flange faces meeting | Two flat surfaces fuse like pressed paper |
| **Concentric** | 2 translational | Shaft-in-hole, pin-in-boss | Two circles share a common center axis |
| **Distance / Offset** | 0 (defines gap) | Pin protrusion length | Acts like a measuring calliper |
| **Angle** | 1 rotational | None here (mostly for mechanisms) | Sets a specific angle like a clock hand |
| **Tangent** | 1 translational | None here | Used for gears and belt drives |
| **Lock / Fix** | 6 (Full lock) | Ground/base component | Anchors the assembly to origin |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Standard Proportions for Joints & Couplings

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas use **$d$** as the nominal shaft diameter (in mm) — the KTU-recommended base parameter for all proportional design.

### 2.3.1 Socket and Spigot Joint (Cast Iron / Mild Steel Standard)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Length of socket } (L) &= 1.5\,d \quad \text{to} \quad 2.0\,d \\
\text{Wall thickness of socket } (t_s) &= 0.05\,d + 5\,\text{mm} \\
\text{Wall thickness of spigot } (t_{sp}) &= 0.04\,d + 4\,\text{mm} \\
\text{Diameter of socket flange } (D) &= d + 2(t_s) + \text{flange allowance} \\
\text{Number of rivets in flange } (n) &= \text{Per design load; commonly } 4 \text{ to } 8
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.3.2 Knuckle Joint (Unwin's Empirical Proportions)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Diameter of pin } (d_p) &= d \\
\text{Outer diameter of eye } (d_o) &= 2.0\,d_p \\
\text{Thickness of single eye } (t) &= 1.0\,d_p \\
\text{Thickness of fork } (t_f) &= 0.75\,d_p \\
\text{Diameter of rod (knuckle)} (d) &= d_p \\
\text{Width of the joint head } (b) &= 1.5\,d_p \quad \text{to} \quad 2.0\,d_p \\
\text{Crown height } (h_c) &= 0.7\,d_p \quad \text{(approximate)} \\
\text{Collar thickness } &= 0.5\,d_p
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note (Unwin's Formula):** The empirical factor for knuckle joint comes from **W.C. Unwin's** standard rule: $d_p = \sqrt{ (4 \times W) / (\pi \times \sigma_t) }$ for tensile load $W$ and permissible tensile stress $\sigma_t$. For exam problems, the proportion $d_p = d$ is assumed.

### 2.3.3 Rigid Flange Coupling

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pitch circle diameter of bolts } (D_1) &= 3.0\,d \quad \text{to} \quad 3.5\,d \\
\text{Number of bolts } (n) &= 4,\,6,\,8 \text{ (even number, symmetric)} \\
\text{Bolt diameter } (d_b) &= 0.5\,d \quad \text{to} \quad 0.6\,d \\
\text{Flange thickness } (t_f) &= 0.5\,d \quad \text{to} \quad 0.75\,d \\
\text{Flange outer diameter } (D) &= D_1 + 2(d_b + 2\,\text{mm clearance}) \\
\text{Hub diameter } (D_h) &= 2.0\,d \quad \text{to} \quad 2.5\,d \\
\text{Hub length } (L_h) &= 1.5\,d \quad \text{to} \quad 2.0\,d \\
\text{Key proportion} (w \times h) &= d/4 \times d/6
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.3.4 Bushed Pin Flexible Coupling

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pitch circle of pins } (D_p) &= 3.0\,d \quad \text{to} \quad 4.0\,d \\
\text{Number of pins } (n) &= 4,\,6,\,8,\,10 \\
\text{Pin diameter } (d_{pin}) &= d/3 \quad \text{to} \quad d/2 \\
\text{Flange thickness } (t_f) &= 0.5\,d \quad \text{to} \quad 0.75\,d \\
\text{Bush outer diameter } &= d_{pin} + 2t_b,\quad t_b \approx 2 \text{ to } 3\,\text{mm} \\
\text{Flange outer diameter } (D) &= D_p + 2(d_{pin} + 5\,\text{mm}) \\
\text{Flange width (axial)} (W_f) &= 1.5\,d \quad \text{to} \quad 2.0\,d
\end{aligned}
$$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these joints matter in the field:**
> - **Socket-Spigot:** Still the default for cast iron municipal water mains (ISO 2531 / EN 545).
> - **Knuckle Joint:** Found in **tractor trailer hitches**, **ship steering linkages**, and **valve gear mechanisms** in steam locomotives.
> - **Rigid Flange Coupling:** Industry standard for **centrifugal pump-to-motor** connections where shafts are precisely aligned.
> - **Bushed Pin Flexible Coupling:** The **Hiren / Pin-Bush coupling** is a workhorse in **machine tool spindles** where vibration damping is critical.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Proportions & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Component Inventory per Assembly

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is the **most high-yield content for KTU viva and lab records**. Examiners frequently ask: *"How many parts did you model?"* and *"Name the mates used."*

| Assembly | Component Parts | Key Mates | Material (Typical) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Socket & Spigot** | Socket body, Spigot pipe end, (Optional) Caulking ring | Concentric (axis), Coincident (end face) | Cast Iron / Mild Steel |
| **Knuckle Joint** | Rod 1 (single eye), Rod 2 (fork, 2 halves), Pin, Collar / Taper pin | Concentric (pin), Coincident (fork faces), Distance (pin protrusion) | Mild Steel (EN8) |
| **Rigid Flange** | Shaft 1, Flange 1, Key, Bolts (n), Nuts (n), Shaft 2, Flange 2, Key | Concentric (shaft-flange), Coincident (flange faces), Concentric (bolts in holes) | Cast Iron / MS |
| **Bushed Pin** | Flange 1, Shaft 1, Pin, Bush (rubber/leather), Flange 2, Shaft 2 | Concentric (pin-bush-shaft pairs), Coincident (flange faces), Distance (pin head protrusion) | MS + Natural Rubber |

## 3.2 Exhaustive Proportional Derivation — Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam-Type Problem:** *"Design the proportions of a Rigid Flange Coupling for two shafts of nominal diameter $d = 40$ mm."*

**Given:** $d = 40\,\text{mm}$

**Step 1 — Pitch Circle Diameter of Bolts ($D_1$)**

We choose the lower bound of the standard range for cost-effective design:
$$D_1 = 3.0 \times d = 3.0 \times 40 = 120\,\text{mm}$$

**Step 2 — Number and Diameter of Bolts**

For moderate duty couplings, $n = 4$ bolts is standard. The bolt diameter is:
$$d_b = 0.5 \times d = 0.5 \times 40 = 20\,\text{mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, the next higher standard bolt size (M22 or M24) is chosen for availability. For exam simplification, the proportional value is accepted.

**Step 3 — Flange Thickness ($t_f$)**

$$t_f = 0.5 \times d = 0.5 \times 40 = 20\,\text{mm}$$

**Step 4 — Flange Outer Diameter ($D$)**

$$D = D_1 + 2 \times (d_b + 2) = 120 + 2 \times (20 + 2) = 120 + 44 = 164\,\text{mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This accommodates the bolt head (or nut) diameter plus a 2 mm clearance all around the bolt hole circle.

**Step 5 — Hub Dimensions ($D_h$ and $L_h$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_h &= 2.0 \times d = 2.0 \times 40 = 80\,\text{mm} \\
L_h &= 1.5 \times d = 1.5 \times 40 = 60\,\text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Key Proportions (Sunk Key)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w &= d/4 = 40/4 = 10\,\text{mm} \\
h &= d/6 = 40/6 \approx 6.67 \rightarrow \text{use } 7\,\text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.1 Summary Table of Derived Dimensions

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Computed Value (mm) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Nominal shaft dia. | $d$ | Given | 40 |
| Bolt pitch dia. | $D_1$ | $3.0\,d$ | 120 |
| Bolt diameter | $d_b$ | $0.5\,d$ | 20 |
| Flange thickness | $t_f$ | $0.5\,d$ | 20 |
| Flange outer dia. | $D$ | $D_1 + 2(d_b + 2)$ | 164 |
| Hub diameter | $D_h$ | $2.0\,d$ | 80 |
| Hub length | $L_h$ | $1.5\,d$ | 60 |
| Key width | $w$ | $d/4$ | 10 |
| Key height | $h$ | $d/6$ | 7 |

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mate Selection Strategy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assembly order matters in CAD.** The first inserted part should be **Fixed** (ground). Mates are then applied in a **base-upward** order. The following matrix is the **KTU-recommended mate order**:

1. **Step 1:** Insert the first shaft + flange sub-assembly and **Fix** it.
2. **Step 2:** Insert the second shaft + flange sub-assembly. Apply **Concentric** mate between the two shaft cylindrical faces.
3. **Step 3:** Apply **Coincident** mate between the two flange end-faces.
4. **Step 4:** Insert bolts. Apply **Concentric** mate between bolt shank and bolt hole. Apply **Coincident** mate between bolt head underside and flange face.
5. **Step 5:** For Bushed Pin coupling, **Distance** mate is used to set pin protrusion length (typically 5–10 mm above flange surface).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often apply mates in **random order**, which leads to solver warnings (*"Over-constrained"*) and incorrect exploded views. Always mate from the **largest to smallest** feature.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Socket and Spigot Joint

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed. No reserved keywords used. Labels are clean uppercase text.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start Assembly] --> B0[Insert Socket Body]
    B0 --> B1[Fix Socket at Origin]
    B1 --> C0[Insert Spigot Pipe]
    C0 --> C1[Apply Concentric Mate]
    C1 --> C2[Shaft Axis of Spigot aligned to Socket Bore]
    C2 --> D0[Apply Coincident Mate]
    D0 --> D1[Spigot end face touches Socket internal shoulder]
    D1 --> E0[Validate No Interference]
    E0 --> F0[Create Exploded View]
    F0 --> G0[Generate BOM and Drawing]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Knuckle Joint

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Start Knuckle Assembly] --> B1A[Insert Rod with Single Eye]
    B1A --> B1B[Fix Single Eye at Origin]
    B1B --> C1A[Insert Fork Top Half]
    C1A --> C1B[Insert Fork Bottom Half]
    C1B --> C1C[Concentric Mate - All three to common Pin Axis]
    C1C --> C1D[Coincident Mate - Fork inner faces touch Single Eye faces]
    C1D --> D1A[Insert Pin]
    D1A --> D1B[Concentric Mate - Pin to Fork and Eye holes]
    D1B --> D1C[Coincident Mate - Pin head to outer fork face]
    D1C --> E1A[Insert Collar or Split Pin]
    E1A --> F1A[Validate - Check Swivel DOF]
    F1A --> G1A[Explode - One Component Offset Along Pin Axis]
    G1A --> H1A[BOM and Drawing]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Rigid Flange Coupling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A2[Start Flange Coupling] --> B2A[Insert Shaft 1]
    B2A --> B2B[Fix Shaft 1 at Origin]
    B2B --> C2A[Insert Flange 1 over Shaft 1]
    C2A --> C2B[Concentric Mate - Flange bore to Shaft 1]
    C2B --> C2C[Coincident Mate - Flange end face to Shaft 1 shoulder]
    C2C --> D2A[Insert Shaft 2 and Flange 2 sub-assembly]
    D2A --> D2B[Concentric Mate - Shaft 1 to Shaft 2 axis]
    D2B --> D2C[Coincident Mate - Flange 1 face to Flange 2 face]
    D2C --> E2A[Insert Bolts Number n]
    E2A --> E2B[Concentric Mate - Each Bolt to corresponding hole]
    E2B --> E2C[Coincident Mate - Each Bolt head to Flange outer face]
    E2C --> F2A[Insert Nuts Number n]
    F2A --> F2B[Coincident Mate - Nuts to opposite Flange face]
    F2B --> G2A[Validate Mating Integrity]
    G2A --> H2A[Explode - Bolts move radially outward]
    H2A --> I2A[Generate BOM and Orthographic Drawing]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Bushed Pin Flexible Coupling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A3[Start Bushed Pin Coupling] --> B3A[Insert Flange 1 with Shaft 1]
    B3A --> B3B[Fix Flange 1]
    B3B --> C3A[Insert Flange 2 with Shaft 2]
    C3A --> C3B[Concentric Mate - Both Flange bores]
    C3B --> C3C[Coincident Mate - Flange 1 face to Flange 2 face]
    C3C --> D3A[Insert Rubber Bushes into Flange 2 holes]
    D3A --> D3B[Concentric Mate - Bush to Hole]
    D3B --> E3A[Insert Pins through Flange 1 into Bushes]
    E3A --> E3B[Concentric Mate - Pin to Bush inner diameter]
    E3B --> E3C[Coincident Mate - Pin head to Flange 1 face]
    E3C --> F3A[Apply Distance Mate - Pin protrudes 5 to 10 mm]
    F3A --> G3A[Validate - Confirm 1 rotational DOF between flanges]
    G3A --> H3A[Explode - Pins and Bushes shown separately]
    H3A --> I3A[BOM and Drawing]
```

## 4.5 Schematic — Component Hierarchy (BOM Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Assembly File - Rigid Flange Coupling] --> SH1[Shaft 1]
    ROOT --> FL1[Flange 1]
    ROOT --> SH2[Shaft 2]
    ROOT --> FL2[Flange 2]
    ROOT --> B1[Bolt 1]
    ROOT --> B2[Bolt 2]
    ROOT --> B3[Bolt 3]
    ROOT --> B4[Bolt 4]
    ROOT --> N1[Nut 1]
    ROOT --> N2[Nut 2]
    ROOT --> N3[Nut 3]
    ROOT --> N4[Nut 4]
    ROOT --> K1[Key 1]
    ROOT --> K2[Key 2]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **Define an "Assembly Constraint" in parametric CAD. Name any two constraints with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
An *Assembly Constraint* (called a **Mate** in SolidWorks / Fusion 360) is a geometric condition that defines the relative position and orientation between two or more components in an assembly.

1. **Concentric Mate** — Aligns the axes of two cylindrical / circular features. *Example:* Aligning the shaft axis with the flange bore in a rigid flange coupling.
2. **Coincident Mate** — Makes two planar faces lie on the same infinite plane. *Example:* The end faces of two flanges touching in a coupling assembly.

*(1 mark each for the definition, 1 mark for each example = 3 marks total.)*

---

> **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **List the standard proportional values (in terms of shaft diameter $d$) for: (i) Bolt pitch circle diameter, (ii) Flange thickness, and (iii) Hub length, in a Rigid Flange Coupling.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. (i) Bolt pitch circle diameter $D_1 = 3.0\,d$ to $3.5\,d$.
2. (ii) Flange thickness $t_f = 0.5\,d$ to $0.75\,d$.
3. (iii) Hub length $L_h = 1.5\,d$ to $2.0\,d$.

*(1 mark per correct value with correct notation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - Students forget to mention **materials** in the BOM (lose 1 mark).
> - Missing **unit symbols (mm)** in the design table (lose 1 mark).
> - Mates are listed but **not ordered logically** (lose 1–2 marks).
> - Exploded view is drawn but the **explode direction is wrong** (e.g., bolts exploded tangentially instead of radially) — full marks lost on the drawing portion.

---

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks)

> **Question A.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **(a)** Design a **Knuckle Joint** for a shaft of diameter $d = 30\,\text{mm}$ and list all standard proportions. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Explain the **step-by-step assembly procedure** for the same knuckle joint in any parametric CAD software, listing all mates used. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — Design of Knuckle Joint (7 Marks)

**Given:** $d = 30\,\text{mm}$ (nominal rod diameter)

Using Unwin's empirical proportions:

| S.No. | Parameter | Formula | Computed Value (mm) |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| 1 | Pin diameter, $d_p$ | $= d$ | **30** |
| 2 | Outer eye diameter, $d_o$ | $= 2.0\,d_p$ | **60** |
| 3 | Single eye thickness, $t$ | $= 1.0\,d_p$ | **30** |
| 4 | Fork thickness (each half), $t_f$ | $= 0.75\,d_p$ | **22.5** |
| 5 | Joint head width, $b$ | $= 1.5\,d_p$ | **45** |
| 6 | Crown height, $h_c$ | $= 0.7\,d_p$ | **21** |
| 7 | Collar thickness, $t_c$ | $= 0.5\,d_p$ | **15** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note (Tolerance):** The pin hole is reamed to **H7 tolerance** for clearance fit with the pin (typically **H7/h6**).

*(Breakup: 4 marks for table, 2 marks for explanation, 1 mark for tolerance note = 7 marks.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Assembly Procedure (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Part Modelling (1 Mark):**
Create four separate part files: `single_eye_rod.sldprt`, `fork_top.sldprt`, `fork_bottom.sldprt`, `pin.sldprt`, and `collar.sldprt`. Use **Extruded Boss** and **Revolved Cut** features. Add a **2 mm fillet** at the eye inner diameter transition.

**Step 2 — Assembly Setup (1 Mark):**
Open a new Assembly file. Insert the `single_eye_rod` first and apply the **Fix** (Lock) mate to ground it at the origin.

**Step 3 — Insert Fork Halves (1 Mark):**
Insert `fork_top` and `fork_bottom`. Apply **Concentric** mate between the pin holes of both fork halves and the single eye's pin hole (using a temporary axis line or the inner cylindrical face).

**Step 4 — Coincident Mates (1 Mark):**
Apply **Coincident** mate between the inner face of `fork_top` and the upper face of the single eye. Repeat for `fork_bottom` on the lower face. This sandwiches the single eye between the fork.

**Step 5 — Pin Insertion (1 Mark):**
Insert `pin`. Apply **Concentric** mate between the pin's cylindrical surface and the common axis of the fork/eye holes.

**Step 6 — Pin Head and Collar (1 Mark):**
Apply **Coincident** mate between the pin head's underside and the outer face of `fork_top`. Insert the collar on the opposite side and apply **Concentric** + **Coincident** mates.

**Step 7 — Validation and Exploded View (1 Mark):**
Run the **Interference Check** tool. Then use the **Exploded View** command, selecting the pin as the component to move along the Z-axis by approximately 1.5 to 2 times the pin length to clearly show the sequence.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout:** Forgetting to apply the **Coincident mate** on the fork faces leads to the fork halves floating in space — the assembly will over-rotate and the explode sequence will look chaotic.

---

### Part B — Question B (Alternative Choice — 14 Marks)

> **Question B.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **(a)** Design a **Rigid Flange Coupling** to connect two shafts of diameter $d = 50\,\text{mm}$ using 4 bolts. Compute all standard dimensions. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** List and explain the **step-by-step assembly mates** for the same in SolidWorks / Fusion 360, and describe how the **Bill of Materials (BOM)** and **Exploded View** are generated. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — Design Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given:** $d = 50\,\text{mm}$, $n = 4$ bolts.

| S.No. | Parameter | Formula | Computed Value (mm) |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| 1 | Bolt pitch diameter, $D_1$ | $3.0\,d$ | **150** |
| 2 | Bolt diameter, $d_b$ | $0.5\,d$ | **25** |
| 3 | Flange thickness, $t_f$ | $0.5\,d$ | **25** |
| 4 | Flange outer diameter, $D$ | $D_1 + 2(d_b + 2)$ | **204** |
| 5 | Hub diameter, $D_h$ | $2.0\,d$ | **100** |
| 6 | Hub length, $L_h$ | $1.5\,d$ | **75** |
| 7 | Key width, $w$ | $d/4$ | **12.5** |
| 8 | Key height, $h$ | $d/6$ | **8.33 ≈ 9** |

**Material Selection (1 mark):** Flanges — **Cast Iron (Grade FG 200)**; Bolts — **M27 grade 8.8**; Shafts — **Mild Steel (EN8)**.

*(Breakup: 5 marks for table, 1 mark for material, 1 mark for sketch of proportions = 7 marks.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Assembly Procedure (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Sub-Assembly Creation (1 Mark):**
Create two identical sub-assemblies: `shaft1_flange1` and `shaft2_flange2`. Each has a shaft (with keyway), a flange (with 4 holes), and a sunk key as a separate part.

**Step 2 — Insert Sub-Assemblies (1 Mark):**
In the main assembly, insert `shaft1_flange1` and apply **Fix** mate. Insert `shaft2_flange2`.

**Step 3 — Concentric Mate (1 Mark):**
Apply **Concentric** mate between the cylindrical surface of Shaft 1 and Shaft 2 (or the two flange bores). This aligns the rotational axes.

**Step 4 — Coincident Mate (1 Mark):**
Apply **Coincident** mate between the two flange end faces. This brings the flanges into contact.

**Step 5 — Bolt and Nut Mating (1 Mark):**
Insert 4 bolts. For each bolt, apply **Concentric** mate between the bolt shank and the corresponding bolt hole. Apply **Coincident** mate between the bolt head and the outer face of Flange 1. Insert 4 nuts on the opposite side and apply **Coincident** mate to Flange 2.

**Step 6 — BOM Generation (1 Mark):**
Go to the **Bill of Materials** feature in the drawing/assembly. Customize the columns to show: **Item No., Part Number, Description, Material, Quantity**. The CAD engine auto-counts all unique parts.

**Step 7 — Exploded View (1 Mark):**
Select all 4 bolts and 4 nuts. Use the **Explode Linear** command, setting the explode direction as the **flange axis (normal to flange face)** with a step distance of 50–80 mm. Add a second explode step to move the two sub-assemblies apart horizontally. Use **Auto-Spacers** to align components.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout:** The most common mistake is **exploding the bolts tangentially** (around the flange circumference) instead of **axially**. The KTU standard is **axial explosion** for flange couplings to show the assembly sequence clearly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final High-Density Revision Block — Read Before the Exam:**

- [ ] **Assembly Modelling** is the **process of combining parts using mates** (constraints). The first part inserted must be **Fixed/Grounded**.

- [ ] The **6 Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** of a free body are removed progressively by mates. A fully constrained assembly has **0 DOF**.

- [ ] **Socket and Spigot Joint** uses **2 mates**: **Concentric** (axis) + **Coincident** (end face). Standard proportions: $L = 1.5d$ to $2.0d$, $t_s = 0.05d + 5$.

- [ ] **Knuckle Joint** has 5 standard parts: single eye, fork (2 halves), pin, collar. Mates: Concentric (pin) + Coincident (fork faces) + Distance (optional). Proportions follow **Unwin's empirical rule**: $d_o = 2d_p$, $t = d_p$, $t_f = 0.75 d_p$.

- [ ] **Rigid Flange Coupling** has 2 sub-assemblies (one per shaft). Mates: Concentric (shafts) + Coincident (flange faces) + Concentric (each bolt to hole). Proportions: $D_1 = 3.0d$, $d_b = 0.5d$, $t_f = 0.5d$.

- [ ] **Bushed Pin Flexible Coupling** is the **flexible variant** of rigid flange. The **rubber/leather bush** provides **1 rotational DOF** and absorbs shock. Pin proportion: $d_{pin} = d/3$ to $d/2$.

- [ ] The **BOM (Bill of Materials)** is auto-generated by the CAD tool and must include **Item No., Part Name, Material, Quantity**.

- [ ] The **Exploded View** must use **axial explosion** for couplings and **pin-axis explosion** for knuckle joints — never random.

- [ ] Always use **ISO 128 line conventions** in the final drawing: **Continuous thick** for visible edges, **dashed thin** for hidden edges, **chain thin** for centerlines, **continuous thin** for dimension lines.

- [ ] **Materials** in BOM: Cast Iron (FG 200) for housings, Mild Steel (EN8/EN9) for shafts and pins, Rubber/Leather for bushes, Grade 8.8/10.9 for bolts.

- [ ] **KTU Viva Questions** to prepare:
    1. *What is the difference between a rigid and flexible coupling?*
    2. *Why is Concentric mate applied before Coincident mate?*
    3. *What is the function of the collar in a knuckle joint?*
    4. *Why is the bush made of rubber and not metal in a bushed pin coupling?*
    5. *How many DOF does a fully constrained assembly have?*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creating Assembly Models of Plummer Block, Single Plate Clutch or Cone Friction Clutch
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRL307 - MACHINE DRAWING AND SOLID MODELLING
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Creating Assembly Models of Plummer Block, Single Plate Clutch or Cone Friction Clutch

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Assembly Modelling — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assembly Modelling** is the process of combining two or more independently created 3D part models into a single functional unit by applying geometric relationships called **Mates** (constraints). In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, an assembly represents the relative position, orientation, and mechanical interaction of components as they would appear in the final assembled product, generated using parametric solid modelling software such as **SolidWorks**, **CATIA V5**, **PTC Creo**, or **Autodesk Fusion 360**.

### 1.2 Components Under Study

The KTU 2024 PCBRL307 syllabus (Module Experiment 2) lists **three** classical mechanical assemblies, of which the student is expected to model **at least one**:

| Assembly | Function | Industry Use |
|----------|----------|--------------|
| **Plummer Block** | Pedestal support for a rotating shaft via a bearing | Conveyors, fans, line shafts |
| **Single Plate Clutch** | Engages/disengages power transmission via friction | Automobiles, machine tools |
| **Cone Friction Clutch** | Engages/disengages power via conical contact surface | Heavy-duty drives, differentials |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Lego Approach"

> [!NOTE]
> Think of assembly modelling as building a **Lego structure** from pre-made blocks. Each Lego block is a **part file** (with its own dimensions and features). The **snap-fit** mechanism of Lego is the CAD equivalent of a **Mate** — it locks pieces together geometrically so they cannot move relative to each other in unwanted directions. The complete Lego structure is your **assembly file**.

### 1.4 The Plummer Block — Simple English Explanation

A **Plummer Block** (also called a **Pillow Block Bearing**) is essentially a **cast-iron cradle** that holds a **ball/roller bearing** to support a rotating shaft.

**Real-world analogy:** Imagine a **swing cradle for a baby** — the cradle (Plummer Block body) holds the baby (bearing), and the baby holds a stick (shaft) which can rotate freely. The cradle is bolted to a rigid structure (floor/machine bed) and is greased from the top so the bearings can roll smoothly.

**Key Sub-assemblies of a Plummer Block:**
1. **Base** — Bolted to foundation, has ribbed structure for rigidity.
2. **Body / Pedestal** — Houses the bearing, has spherical seat for self-alignment.
3. **Cap** — Top cover, secured with bolts.
4. **Bearing Assembly** — Inner race + Outer race + Balls/Rollers + Cage + Insert.
5. **Fasteners** — Cap bolts, foundation bolts, set screws.

### 1.5 The Single Plate Clutch — Simple English Explanation

A **Single Plate Clutch** transfers rotational motion from a **driving shaft** to a **driven shaft** through **frictional contact** of a single disc.

**Real-world analogy:** Think of a **turntable** in your kitchen. The base (driving member, attached to the wall switch) has a flat surface. When you place a heavy circular plate (friction plate) on it and press it down with your hand (pressure plate), friction causes the plate to rotate with the base. Lifting your hand disengages it. The car-clutch pedal applies/removes this "hand pressure."

**Key Components:**
1. **Flywheel** — Driving member, bolted to engine crankshaft.
2. **Friction Plate (Disc)** — Sliding on splined driven shaft, has friction lining.
3. **Pressure Plate** — Presses disc against flywheel.
4. **Clutch Springs** — Provide axial clamping force.
5. **Release Levers / Throw-out Bearing** — Disengages clutch.

### 1.6 The Cone Friction Clutch — Simple English Explanation

A **Cone Friction Clutch** uses a **conical male-female pair** instead of flat discs. The conical engagement gives a **mechanical advantage** because the normal force component creates high friction with lower spring force.

**Real-world analogy:** Imagine two **ice-cream cones** — one inserted into the other. The outer cone (driven) is held still; the inner cone (driver) is pressed axially into it. Friction along the slanted conical surface causes the outer cone to spin with the inner one. The slant makes the grip "self-energizing."

**Key Components:**
1. **Driving Member (Male Cone)** — Attached to driving shaft.
2. **Driven Member (Female Cone)** — Has friction lining on inner conical surface.
3. **Springs** — Axially push male cone into female cone.
4. **Operating Mechanism** — Lever / thrust bearing collar.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard ISO/ISI Conventions for Assembly Drawings:**
> - Hidden lines are usually **omitted** in assembly views (to reduce clutter).
> - **Sectional views** are used to show internal mating parts clearly.
> - **Centre lines** (chain-dotted) must extend slightly beyond circles.
> - **Hatching** (section lines) of adjacent parts must differ in angle/density.
> - A **Bill of Materials (BOM)** and **Parts List** must accompany the assembly.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Assembly Modelling Workflow

The CAD assembly modelling process follows a strict sequence of **logical steps**. Skipping steps or mixing the bottom-up/top-down approach causes mates to fail and rebuilds to break.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bottom-Up Assembly Approach (KTU Recommended):**
> 1. Identify all parts → List dimensions.
> 2. Model each part **independently** as a `.sldprt` / `.CATPart` / `.prt` file.
> 3. Create a **New Assembly** file.
> 4. Insert the **Base component** (fixed first).
> 5. Insert other parts one by one.
> 6. Apply **Mates** between components to lock geometry.
> 7. Validate degrees of freedom → Should be **0**.
> 8. Generate drawing views from assembly.

### 2.2 CAD Mates (Constraints) — The Theoretical Building Blocks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A Mate** is a mathematical relationship that removes specific **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** between two component faces, edges, or vertices. In 3D space, every rigid body has **6 DOF**: 3 translational (X, Y, Z) and 3 rotational (roll, pitch, yaw). A typical assembly mate removes 1–4 DOF.

**Standard Mate Categories (SolidWorks terminology — KTU High-Yield):**

| Mate Type | DOF Removed | Use Case |
|-----------|-------------|----------|
| **Coincident** | 2–3 | Faces touching, axes aligning |
| **Concentric** | 2 (rotational) | Shaft inside hole |
| **Distance** | 1 | Maintain fixed gap |
| **Angle** | 1 (rotational) | Lever at specific angle |
| **Parallel** | 2 | Flat surfaces parallel |
| **Perpendicular** | 2 | Shaft axis to ground |
| **Tangent** | 2 | Roller on cam |
| **Lock** | All 6 | Fix base component |

### 2.3 Plummer Block — Deep Theoretical Breakdown

**Structural Logic:**
- The **shaft** is supported by the **inner race** of the bearing.
- The **inner race** is pressed onto the shaft (interference fit).
- The **balls/rollers** transmit radial load from inner to outer race.
- The **outer race** sits in the spherical seat of the pedestal.
- The **cap** clamps the bearing from above with bolts.
- **Set screws** lock the inner race axially on the shaft.

**Bearing Loading Equation (KTU High-Yield):**

$$P_r = \frac{F_r}{X \cdot Y}$$

Where:
- $P_r$ = Equivalent dynamic radial load (N)
- $F_r$ = Applied radial force (N)
- $X$ = Radial load factor
- $Y$ = Axial load factor

> [!NOTE]
> For pure radial load: $X = 1$, $Y = 0$ → $P_r = F_r$.

### 2.4 Single Plate Clutch — Deep Theoretical Breakdown

**Operating Principle:**
- **Engaged state:** Pressure plate clamps friction disc against flywheel by spring force $F_s$.
- **Disengaged state:** Release bearing pushes pressure plate axially, compressing springs and freeing disc.

**KTU High-Yield Formulas for Single Plate Clutch:**

**Axial Clamping Force (Total Spring Force):**

$$W = n \cdot F_s$$

Where:
- $W$ = Total axial clamping force (N)
- $n$ = Number of clutch springs
- $F_s$ = Force per spring (N)

**Frictional Torque Capacity:**

$$T = \mu \cdot W \cdot R_m$$

Where:
- $T$ = Torque transmitted (N·mm)
- $\mu$ = Coefficient of friction (typical: **0.25–0.35** for asbestos lining on cast iron)
- $W$ = Axial clamping force (N)
- $R_m$ = **Mean friction radius** = $\dfrac{R_o + R_i}{2}$ (mm)

**Uniform Pressure Theory — Actual Contact Pressure at radius $r$:**

$$p = \frac{W}{2\pi \cdot R_m \cdot (R_o - R_i)}$$

**Uniform Pressure — Maximum Pressure (at inner radius):**

$$p_{max} = \frac{W}{2\pi \cdot R_m \cdot (R_o - R_i)}$$

**Uniform Wear Theory (More Realistic) — Pressure varies inversely with $r$:**

$$p \cdot r = \text{constant}$$

**Uniform Wear — Torque Capacity:**

$$T_{uw} = \frac{\mu \cdot W \cdot (R_o + R_i)}{2} = \mu \cdot W \cdot R_m$$

### 2.5 Cone Friction Clutch — Deep Theoretical Breakdown

**Geometric Advantage:**
The conical surface provides **mechanical advantage** due to the half-cone angle $\alpha$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conical Mechanical Advantage Factor:**
> For a cone with half-angle $\alpha$, the axial clamping force $W$ produces a normal force $N$ on the cone surface given by:
> $$N = \frac{W}{\sin\alpha}$$
> Therefore, smaller $\alpha$ (sharper cone) gives **larger normal force** → higher friction. However, $\alpha$ cannot be less than **12.5°** (≈ 1:5 slope) to prevent **self-locking** (jamming of the cone).

**Cone Geometry:**

$$R_m = \frac{R_o + R_i}{2}$$

**Slant Length (L) of Cone Contact Surface:**

$$L = \frac{R_o - R_i}{\sin\alpha}$$

**Total Normal Force on Cone Surface:**

$$N = \frac{W}{\sin\alpha}$$

**Frictional Torque Capacity of Cone Clutch:**

$$T = \mu \cdot N \cdot R_m = \frac{\mu \cdot W \cdot R_m}{\sin\alpha} = \frac{\mu \cdot W}{2\sin\alpha}(R_o + R_i)$$

**Uniform Wear Torque (Cone):**

$$T_{uw} = \frac{\mu \cdot W \cdot (R_o + R_i)}{2\sin\alpha}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Ratio of Cone Torque to Plate Torque** (at same W, μ, and dimensions):
> $$\frac{T_{cone}}{T_{plate}} = \frac{1}{\sin\alpha}$$
> For $\alpha = 15°$ → Ratio ≈ **3.86**. This is why cone clutches transmit much higher torque in compact space.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Comprehensive Quick Reference)

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Definition | Unit |
|--------|----------|----------------------|------|
| $T$ | Frictional torque | $\mu \cdot W \cdot R_m$ | N·mm |
| $W$ | Axial clamping force | $n \cdot F_s$ | N |
| $R_m$ | Mean radius | $\dfrac{R_o + R_i}{2}$ | mm |
| $\mu$ | Coefficient of friction | **0.25–0.4** (typical for friction lining) | — |
| $p_{max}$ | Max contact pressure | $\dfrac{W}{2\pi R_m (R_o - R_i)}$ | N/mm² |
| $\alpha$ | Half cone angle | Typically **15°–20°** | deg |
| $N$ | Normal force on cone | $\dfrac{W}{\sin\alpha}$ | N |
| $L$ | Slant length of cone | $\dfrac{R_o - R_i}{\sin\alpha}$ | mm |
| $T_{cone}$ | Cone clutch torque | $\dfrac{\mu W R_m}{\sin\alpha}$ | N·mm |
| $n$ | Number of springs | Usually **4 to 12** | — |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why do mechanical engineers learn assembly modelling?**
> 1. **Design Validation** — Detect interferences before manufacturing.
> 2. **Mass Properties** — Auto-calculate centre of mass, moment of inertia.
> 3. **Animation** — Simulate clutch engagement, shaft rotation.
> 4. **FEA Export** — Apply loads in ANSYS/ABAQUS.
> 5. **Manufacturing Drawings** — Auto-generate with GD&T from assembly.
> 6. **Interference & Clearance Checks** — Verify tolerances in 3D.
> 7. **Exploded View Drawings** — For service manuals and assembly instructions.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Friction Plate — Uniform Pressure Theory (Full Step-by-Step)

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumption:** Pressure $p$ is **uniform** over the contact area. Valid for **new**, stiff friction linings.

**Step 1 — Consider an elemental annular ring** at radius $r$ with thickness $dr$.

Area of elemental ring:
$$dA = 2\pi r \cdot dr$$

**Step 2 — Elemental normal force on ring:**
$$dW = p \cdot dA = p \cdot 2\pi r \cdot dr$$

**Step 3 — Total axial clamping force (integrate from $R_i$ to $R_o$):**
$$W = \int_{R_i}^{R_o} p \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$

Applying uniform pressure ($p$ = constant):
$$W = 2\pi p \int_{R_i}^{R_o} r \, dr$$

$$W = 2\pi p \left[ \frac{r^2}{2} \right]_{R_i}^{R_o}$$

$$\boxed{W = \pi p (R_o^2 - R_i^2)}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $p$:**
$$p = \frac{W}{\pi (R_o^2 - R_i^2)}$$

**Step 5 — Elemental frictional torque at radius $r$:**
$$dT = \mu \cdot dW \cdot r = \mu \cdot p \cdot 2\pi r^2 \, dr$$

**Step 6 — Total torque:**
$$T = \int_{R_i}^{R_o} \mu \cdot p \cdot 2\pi r^2 \, dr$$

$$T = 2\pi \mu p \int_{R_i}^{R_o} r^2 \, dr$$

$$T = 2\pi \mu p \left[ \frac{r^3}{3} \right]_{R_i}^{R_o}$$

$$T = \frac{2\pi \mu p}{3} (R_o^3 - R_i^3)$$

**Step 7 — Substitute $p$ from Step 4:**

$$T = \frac{2\pi \mu}{3} \cdot \frac{W}{\pi (R_o^2 - R_i^2)} \cdot (R_o^3 - R_i^3)$$

$$\boxed{T_{up} = \frac{2\mu W}{3} \cdot \frac{(R_o^3 - R_i^3)}{(R_o^2 - R_i^2)}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Friction Plate — Uniform Wear Theory (Full Step-by-Step)

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumption (Reye's Hypothesis):** $p \cdot r = \text{constant} = k$. This is more realistic because as wear progresses, the wear rate is uniform across the contact area.

**Step 1 — Express pressure:**
$$p = \frac{k}{r}$$

**Step 2 — Elemental force:**
$$dW = p \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \frac{k}{r} \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = 2\pi k \, dr$$

**Step 3 — Total axial force:**
$$W = \int_{R_i}^{R_o} 2\pi k \, dr = 2\pi k (R_o - R_i)$$

$$\boxed{k = \frac{W}{2\pi (R_o - R_i)}}$$

**Step 4 — Elemental torque:**
$$dT = \mu \cdot dW \cdot r = \mu \cdot 2\pi k \, dr \cdot r = 2\pi \mu k \cdot r \, dr$$

**Step 5 — Total torque:**
$$T = \int_{R_i}^{R_o} 2\pi \mu k \cdot r \, dr = 2\pi \mu k \left[ \frac{r^2}{2} \right]_{R_i}^{R_o}$$

$$T = \pi \mu k (R_o^2 - R_i^2)$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $k$ from Step 3:**

$$T = \pi \mu \cdot \frac{W}{2\pi (R_o - R_i)} \cdot (R_o^2 - R_i^2)$$

$$T = \frac{\mu W}{2(R_o - R_i)} \cdot (R_o + R_i)(R_o - R_i)$$

$$\boxed{T_{uw} = \frac{\mu W (R_o + R_i)}{2} = \mu W R_m}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Cone Clutch — Torque Capacity (Full Step-by-Step)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Setup:** A frustum (truncated cone) with outer radius $R_o$, inner radius $R_i$, half-cone angle $\alpha$, and axial clamping force $W$.

**Step 1 — Resolve force on cone:**
The axial force $W$ acts along the shaft axis. The cone surface is inclined at $\alpha$ to the axis. Resolving perpendicular to the cone surface:

$$\text{Component of } W \text{ normal to cone} = W \sin\alpha$$

**Step 2 — Normal reaction $N$ from cone (balancing force):**
$$N = \frac{W}{\sin\alpha}$$

**Step 3 — Elemental ring on cone surface:**
Consider ring at slant radius $r$ with axial projection $dr / \cos\alpha$.

Area of elemental ring on slant surface:
$$dA = \frac{2\pi r \, dr}{\cos\alpha}$$

**Step 4 — Pressure on cone (uniform wear assumption):**
$$p \cdot r = k \Rightarrow p = \frac{k}{r}$$

**Step 5 — Elemental normal force:**
$$dN = p \cdot dA = \frac{k}{r} \cdot \frac{2\pi r \, dr}{\cos\alpha} = \frac{2\pi k}{\cos\alpha} \, dr$$

**Step 6 — Integrate to get total $N$:**
$$N = \int_{R_i}^{R_o} \frac{2\pi k}{\cos\alpha} dr = \frac{2\pi k (R_o - R_i)}{\cos\alpha}$$

**Step 7 — Equate with $N = W/\sin\alpha$ and solve for $k$:**

$$\frac{W}{\sin\alpha} = \frac{2\pi k (R_o - R_i)}{\cos\alpha}$$

$$k = \frac{W \cos\alpha}{2\pi \sin\alpha (R_o - R_i)} = \frac{W}{2\pi (R_o - R_i) \tan\alpha}$$

**Step 8 — Elemental frictional torque:**

$$dT = \mu \cdot dN \cdot r$$

$$dT = \mu \cdot \frac{2\pi k}{\cos\alpha} \cdot r \, dr$$

**Step 9 — Total torque:**

$$T = \int_{R_i}^{R_o} \frac{2\pi \mu k}{\cos\alpha} r \, dr = \frac{2\pi \mu k}{\cos\alpha} \left[ \frac{r^2}{2} \right]_{R_i}^{R_o}$$

$$T = \frac{\pi \mu k (R_o^2 - R_i^2)}{\cos\alpha}$$

**Step 10 — Substitute $k$ from Step 7:**

$$T = \frac{\pi \mu}{\cos\alpha} \cdot \frac{W \cos\alpha}{2\pi (R_o - R_i) \tan\alpha} \cdot (R_o^2 - R_i^2)$$

$$T = \frac{\mu W}{2(R_o - R_i)\tan\alpha} \cdot (R_o + R_i)(R_o - R_i)$$

$$\boxed{T_{cone,uw} = \frac{\mu W (R_o + R_i)}{2 \tan\alpha} = \frac{\mu W R_m}{\sin\alpha}}$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Single Plate Clutch (KTU Exam Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU University Exam Pattern):**
> A single plate clutch has an effective friction lining of inner radius **75 mm** and outer radius **150 mm**. The coefficient of friction is **0.3**. The total axial spring force is **8 kN**. Calculate:
> (a) Torque capacity (Uniform Pressure)
> (b) Torque capacity (Uniform Wear)
> (c) Maximum contact pressure (Uniform Pressure theory)

**Given:**
- $R_i = 75$ mm, $R_o = 150$ mm
- $\mu = 0.3$, $W = 8000$ N
- $R_m = (75 + 150)/2 = 112.5$ mm

**Part (a) — Uniform Pressure Theory:**

Using $T_{up} = \dfrac{2\mu W}{3} \cdot \dfrac{(R_o^3 - R_i^3)}{(R_o^2 - R_i^2)}$

$$R_o^3 = 150^3 = 3{,}375{,}000 \text{ mm}^3$$
$$R_i^3 = 75^3 = 421{,}875 \text{ mm}^3$$
$$R_o^3 - R_i^3 = 2{,}953{,}125 \text{ mm}^3$$
$$R_o^2 = 22500 \text{ mm}^2$$
$$R_i^2 = 5625 \text{ mm}^2$$
$$R_o^2 - R_i^2 = 16875 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$T_{up} = \frac{2 \times 0.3 \times 8000}{3} \cdot \frac{2{,}953{,}125}{16875}$$

$$T_{up} = 1600 \times 175 = \boxed{280{,}000 \text{ N·mm} = 280 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Part (b) — Uniform Wear Theory:**

Using $T_{uw} = \mu \cdot W \cdot R_m$:

$$T_{uw} = 0.3 \times 8000 \times 112.5 = \boxed{270{,}000 \text{ N·mm} = 270 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Part (c) — Maximum Contact Pressure:**

Using $p = \dfrac{W}{\pi(R_o^2 - R_i^2)}$:

$$p = \frac{8000}{\pi \times 16875} = \frac{8000}{53014.4} = \boxed{0.151 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 0.151 \text{ MPa}}$$

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Cone Clutch

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A cone clutch has $R_o = 200$ mm, $R_i = 100$ mm, half-cone angle $\alpha = 15°$, $\mu = 0.25$, $W = 10$ kN. Find torque capacity.

**Given:** $R_m = 150$ mm, $\sin 15° = 0.2588$

**Solution:**

$$T_{cone} = \frac{\mu W R_m}{\sin\alpha} = \frac{0.25 \times 10000 \times 150}{0.2588}$$

$$T_{cone} = \frac{375{,}000}{0.2588} = \boxed{1{,}448{,}684 \text{ N·mm} \approx 1448.7 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Equivalent Plate Clutch Torque:**
$$T_{plate} = \mu W R_m = 0.25 \times 10000 \times 150 = 375{,}000 \text{ N·mm} = 375 \text{ N·m}$$

**Ratio:**
$$\frac{T_{cone}}{T_{plate}} = \frac{1}{\sin 15°} = 3.864$$

**Conclusion:** The cone clutch transmits **3.86 times** more torque than an equivalent plate clutch with the same axial force.

### 3.6 CAD Assembly Modelling — Detailed Workflow for Plummer Block

**Step-by-step Procedure (SolidWorks / Fusion 360 / CATIA):**

| Step | Action | Dimension Sketched / Mated |
|------|--------|----------------------------|
| 1 | Create new Part: `Base.prt` | Length 250mm, Width 100mm, Height 30mm, with 4 mounting holes ⌀15 |
| 2 | Extrude with fillets/chamfers | Draft angle 5°–7° on side walls |
| 3 | Create `Body.prt` | Hollow block with spherical seat ⌀80mm |
| 4 | Create `Cap.prt` | U-shaped top cover |
| 5 | Standard library: `BallBearing_6205.SLDPRT` | Insert from Toolbox |
| 6 | Insert `Shaft.prt` | Cylinder ⌀25mm, length 300mm |
| 7 | Insert `BoltM10.SLDPRT` × 4 | Standard |
| 8 | Assemble: Base → Concentric with origin, **Lock** | Base fixed |
| 9 | Body → Concentric with base holes; Coincident to base top face | Vertical alignment |
| 10 | Cap → Coincident to body top; M8 bolts → Concentric + Coincident | Secures cap |
| 11 | Bearing → Concentric with body seat; Coincident to seat surface | Bearing sits in spherical socket |
| 12 | Shaft → Concentric with bearing inner race; Coincident to bearing centre plane | Shaft passes through |
| 13 | Bolts → Concentric with all holes; Coincident to cap top | Mechanical integrity |
| 14 | Validate DOF = 0 | Final check |
| 15 | Create Drawing → 4 standard views + Sectional view (AA) + Exploded view | Output deliverable |

### 3.7 CAD Assembly Modelling — Detailed Workflow for Single Plate Clutch

| Step | Action | Key Dimension |
|------|--------|---------------|
| 1 | Create `Flywheel.prt` | Disc ⌀300 × 30mm thick, with 6× M10 bolt holes |
| 2 | Create `FrictionDisc.prt` | Friction lining on both sides, ⌀200 ID × 100 OD, with splines ⌀30mm |
| 3 | Create `PressurePlate.prt` | Disc ⌀250 × 25mm, with spring pockets |
| 4 | Create `DrivingShaft.prt` | Cylinder ⌀40mm, length 200mm with spline |
| 5 | Create `DrivenShaft.prt` | Cylinder ⌀35mm with spline groove |
| 6 | Standard library: `ClutchSpring.SLDPRT` (Helical compression) | ⌀20 wire, 50mm OD, free length 80mm |
| 7 | Assemble: DrivingShaft → Concentric with Flywheel + Coincident to flywheel end | Mounted on crankshaft |
| 8 | Flywheel → Lock as driver (or lock driving shaft) | Base element |
| 9 | FrictionDisc → Concentric with driven shaft splines + Distance from flywheel 5mm | Sliding disc |
| 10 | PressurePlate → Concentric with disc + 4× springs in pockets | Clamps disc |
| 11 | Springs → Concentric with pockets + Distance/compression set | Provide clamping force |
| 12 | ReleaseBearing → Concentric with pressure plate fingers | Disengagement mechanism |
| 13 | DOF check, Exploded view, BOM | Final output |

### 3.8 CAD Assembly Modelling — Detailed Workflow for Cone Friction Clutch

| Step | Action | Key Dimension |
|------|--------|---------------|
| 1 | Create `DrivingCone.prt` | Male cone, $R_o = 80$, $R_i = 40$, $\alpha = 15°$, shaft bore ⌀30 |
| 2 | Create `DrivenCone.prt` | Female cone with friction lining, mating cavity |
| 3 | Create `DrivingShaft.prt` | Cylinder ⌀30 |
| 4 | Create `DrivenShaft.prt` | Hollow shaft for sliding fit |
| 5 | Standard library: `Spring.SLDPRT` | Helical compression |
| 6 | Create `OperatingLever.prt` | Bell-crank lever |
| 7 | Assemble: DrivingShaft → Lock (Fixed) | Base |
| 8 | DrivingCone → Concentric + Coincident to shaft end | Mounted |
| 9 | DrivenCone → Concentric to driving cone + Coincident conical surface | Engages |
| 10 | Springs → Concentric with housing pockets + compression | Axial force |
| 11 | Lever → Pivot mount + Tangent to release collar | Engagement control |
| 12 | Exploded view, BOM | Output |

> **Standard Recommended Dimensions from PSG Data Book:**

| Component | Typical Range |

| Plummer Block shaft size | **20–100 mm** |
| Bearing seat diameter | Shaft + (5 to 15 mm clearance) |

| Spring free length | 2× wire diameter multiple |

    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ASM[PLUMMER BLOCK ASSEMBLY]
        B1[Base - Foundation Plate]
        B2[Body - Pedestal Housing]
        B3[Cap - Top Cover]
        B4[Ball Bearing 6205]
        B5[Shaft]
        B6[Cap Bolts M8]
        B7[Foundation Bolts M12]
        B8[Set Screws M6]
        B9[Oil Hole Plug]
    end

    B2 -->|Concentric + Coincident| B1
    B3 -->|Coincident to top face| B2
    B4 -->|Concentric with spherical seat + Coincident| B2
    B5 -->|Concentric with bearing inner race| B4
    B6 -->|Concentric with cap holes + Coincident| B3
    B6 -.->|Bolts to body threads| B2
    B7 -->|Concentric with base holes + Coincident| B1
    B8 -->|Concentric with bearing inner race set screw holes| B4
    B9 -->|Coincident with oil hole on cap| B3
```

### 4.3 Single Plate Clutch — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ENGAGED[ENGAGED STATE]
        E1[Engine Crankshaft] --> E2[Flywheel - Driving]
        E2 -->|Friction Force| E3[Friction Disc]
        E3 -->|Splines| E4[Driven Shaft]
        E5[Pressure Plate] -->|Axial Force F_s| E3
        E6[Clutch Springs x n] -->|Compress| E5
    end

    subgraph DISENGAGED[DISENGAGED STATE]
        D1[Clutch Pedal] --> D2[Release Fork]
        D2 --> D3[Release / Throw-out Bearing]
        D3 -->|Pushes| D4[Pressure Plate Fingers]
        D4 -->|Moves Forward| D5[Compresses Springs]
        D5 -->|Releases Friction Disc| D6[Disc Free, No Torque]
    end

    D1 -.->|Driver Action| E1
```

### 4.4 Cone Friction Clutch — Engagement Mechanics Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    F1[Driving Shaft] --> F2[Driving Cone - Male]
    F3[Axial Spring Force W] --> F4[Pushes Driving Cone]
    F4 --> F5{Conical Surface Contact}
    F5 -->|Normal Force N = W/sin alpha| F6[Driven Cone - Female]
    F6 --> F7[Friction Force mu x N]
    F7 --> F8[Torque T = mu W R_m / sin alpha]
    F8 --> F9[Driven Shaft]
    F10[Operating Lever] -->|Pivot Action| F11[Release Collar]
    F11 -->|Retracts Spring Force| F5
    F5 -.->|Disengaged| F12[Shafts Decoupled]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part A question carries **3 marks** and tests direct conceptual recall under KTU 2024 Bloom's Level: **Remember / Understand**.

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define the term **'Mate'** in assembly modelling. List any **four standard mate types** with one-line applications.

**Model Answer:**

A **Mate** is a geometric constraint that defines the relative position and orientation between two or more components in an assembly, by removing specific degrees of freedom (DOF) from a rigid body.

Four standard mate types:

1. **Coincident Mate** — Used to make selected faces coplanar or aligned.
2. **Concentric Mate** — Aligns two cylindrical faces along a common axis (e.g., shaft inside a bearing).
3. **Distance Mate** — Maintains a fixed gap between two faces.
4. **Angle Mate** — Sets a specific angle between two planar faces (e.g., lever at 30°).

**Mark Allocation (3 marks):**
- Definition = 1 mark
- 4 mates with applications = 2 marks (0.5 each)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
State the **two classical theories** used to analyze the torque capacity of a friction clutch. Write the **torque equation** for each.

**Model Answer:**

The two theories are:

**1. Uniform Pressure Theory:**
$$T_{up} = \frac{2\mu W}{3} \cdot \frac{(R_o^3 - R_i^3)}{(R_o^2 - R_i^2)}$$

Assumes pressure is uniform across the entire contact surface. Used for **new clutches**.

**2. Uniform Wear Theory:**
$$T_{uw} = \mu \cdot W \cdot R_m = \frac{\mu W (R_o + R_i)}{2}$$

Assumes $p \cdot r = \text{constant}$ (Reye's Hypothesis). Used for **worn clutches** (more realistic for steady-state operation).

**Mark Allocation (3 marks):**
- Naming both theories = 1 mark
- Two equations = 2 marks (1 each)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part B question is for **14 marks** with sub-parts (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**. The student must answer **either full Question A or full Question B** — they are alternatives, not sub-options.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2 PCBRL307]**

(a) **Sketch and describe the components of a Plummer Block assembly. List the function of each component.** **(7 marks)**

(b) **Create the 3D assembly model of a Plummer Block for shaft diameter 30 mm using any standard CAD software. State the CAD commands used and mate types applied for each mating pair.** **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer for Q.A (a):**

A Plummer Block is a **pedestal bearing housing** that supports a rotating shaft via a bearing. It is bolted to the foundation/machine bed.

**Components and Functions:**

| Sl. No. | Component | Function |
|---------|-----------|----------|
| 1 | **Base** | Provides flat surface for mounting on foundation; distributes load |
| 2 | **Body / Pedestal** | Houses the bearing; has spherical seat for self-alignment |
| 3 | **Cap** | Top cover; clamps bearing from above |
| 4 | **Cap Bolts** | Secure cap to body |
| 5 | **Foundation Bolts** | Fix base to machine bed |
| 6 | **Ball/Roller Bearing** | Supports shaft radially; allows rotation with low friction |
| 7 | **Set Screws** | Lock bearing inner race axially to shaft |
| 8 | **Oil Hole / Plug** | For lubrication access |
| 9 | **Shaft** | Transmits power; rotates inside bearing |

*(Sketch: Half-sectioned Plummer Block showing all parts with hatching, BOM callouts, and centre lines.)*

**Mark Allocation (7 marks):**
- Sketch (neat, with hatching, BOM, centre lines) = 4 marks
- List of components with function = 3 marks (≥ 6 components)

---

**Model Answer for Q.A (b):**

**Step-by-step CAD Modelling Procedure (SolidWorks):**

1. **Create Base.prt:**
   - Sketch on Front Plane → Rectangle 250 × 100 mm
   - Extrude → 30 mm
   - Add 4 mounting holes ⌀15 mm at corners (Sketch → Hole Wizard)
   - Apply fillets 5 mm
   - Save as `Base.SLDPRT`

2. **Create Body.prt:**
   - Sketch → Hollow box 180 × 80 mm, height 80 mm
   - Extrude → 80 mm with draft 5°
   - Sketch on top face → spherical cut ⌀80 mm radius 40 mm
   - Save as `Body.SLDPRT`

3. **Create Cap.prt:**
   - Sketch on top plane → U-shape 180 × 80 mm, height 25 mm
   - Add 2 cap-bolt holes ⌀9 mm
   - Save as `Cap.SLDPRT`

4. **Insert Standard Bearing from Toolbox:**
   - Tool Box → Bearing → 6205 (Inner dia 25 mm, OD 52 mm, Width 15 mm)

5. **Create Shaft.prt:**
   - Extrude cylinder ⌀25 mm, length 300 mm with keyway

6. **Assembly Procedure:**
   - File → New → Assembly → Insert `Base.SLDPRT` → click ✓ to fix.
   - Insert `Body.SLDPRT` → Concentric mate with base holes, Coincident with top face.
   - Insert `Cap.SLDPRT` → Concentric with body, Coincident with body top face.
   - Insert Bearing → Concentric with body seat, Coincident with seat.
   - Insert Shaft → Concentric with bearing inner race, Coincident with bearing centre.
   - Insert 4 Cap Bolts M8 → Concentric with cap holes, Coincident with cap top.
   - Insert 4 Foundation Bolts M12 → Concentric with base holes.

7. **Validate:**
   - Tools → Evaluate → Interference Detection → Run Check → Result: **No interference**.
   - Move component check → DOF = **0**.

8. **Generate Drawing:**
   - File → Make Drawing from Assembly → Standard 3rd Angle projection
   - Insert → Section View → Section line A-A → produces half-section
   - Insert → Exploded View → drag parts apart radially
   - Insert Bill of Materials → Auto-populated from parts list

**Mate Types Applied Summary Table (to be drawn by student):**

| Mating Pair | Mate Type |
|-------------|-----------|
| Base ↔ Origin | **Lock** |
| Body ↔ Base | **Coincident** (top face) + **Concentric** (alignment holes) |
| Cap ↔ Body | **Coincident** + **Coincident** (top face) |
| Bearing ↔ Body | **Concentric** + **Coincident** |
| Shaft ↔ Bearing | **Concentric** + **Coincident** |
| Cap Bolts ↔ Cap | **Concentric** + **Coincident** |

**Mark Allocation (7 marks):**
- Step-by-step CAD procedure = 3 marks
- Mate types table = 2 marks
- Validated output (interference check + DOF = 0) = 1 mark
- Drawing generation (sectional + exploded + BOM) = 1 mark

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 PCBRL307]**

(a) **With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a Single Plate Clutch.** **(7 marks)**

(b) **A Single Plate Clutch has effective friction surface of inner radius 100 mm and outer radius 180 mm. The coefficient of friction is 0.28. The total axial spring force is 12 kN. Calculate the torque capacity using: (i) Uniform Pressure theory, (ii) Uniform Wear theory. Also find the maximum pressure intensity.** **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer for Q.B (a):**

**Sketch Description (Half-Sectional View):**

A Single Plate Clutch consists of the following parts:

1. **Flywheel** — Bolted to engine crankshaft (driving member).
2. **Friction Disc** — Sliding on splined driven shaft; has friction lining on both sides.
3. **Pressure Plate** — Clamps the friction disc against flywheel.
4. **Clutch Springs (6 nos.)** — Mounted between pressure plate and cover; provide axial clamping force.
5. **Cover Plate** — Bolted to flywheel; houses springs.
6. **Release Levers (3 nos.)** — Pivoted on cover plate; press pressure plate fingers.
7. **Release / Throw-out Bearing** — Pushes release levers axially when clutch pedal is pressed.

**Working:**

- **Engaged (Pedal Released):** Springs push pressure plate → disc clamps against flywheel → friction torque transmits power from driving shaft to driven shaft.
- **Disengaged (Pedal Pressed):** Release bearing pushes levers → levers pull pressure plate forward → springs compress → disc freed → no torque transmission.

**Mark Allocation (7 marks):**
- Neat half-sectioned sketch with hatching = 4 marks
- Construction + working description = 3 marks

---

**Model Answer for Q.B (b):**

**Given Data:**
- $R_i = 100$ mm, $R_o = 180$ mm
- $\mu = 0.28$, $W = 12{,}000$ N
- $R_m = (100 + 180)/2 = 140$ mm

**(i) Uniform Pressure Theory:**

$$T_{up} = \frac{2\mu W}{3} \cdot \frac{(R_o^3 - R_i^3)}{(R_o^2 - R_i^2)}$$

Calculate:
$$R_o^3 = 180^3 = 5{,}832{,}000 \text{ mm}^3$$
$$R_i^3 = 100^3 = 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ mm}^3$$
$$R_o^3 - R_i^3 = 4{,}832{,}000 \text{ mm}^3$$
$$R_o^2 = 32400, \quad R_i^2 = 10000$$
$$R_o^2 - R_i^2 = 22400 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$T_{up} = \frac{2 \times 0.28 \times 12000}{3} \cdot \frac{4{,}832{,}000}{22400}$$

$$T_{up} = 2240 \times 215.71 = \boxed{483{,}200 \text{ N·mm} \approx 483.2 \text{ N·m}}$$

**(ii) Uniform Wear Theory:**

$$T_{uw} = \mu \cdot W \cdot R_m = 0.28 \times 12000 \times 140$$

$$\boxed{T_{uw} = 470{,}400 \text{ N·mm} = 470.4 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Maximum Pressure Intensity (Uniform Pressure):**

$$p_{max} = \frac{W}{\pi(R_o^2 - R_i^2)} = \frac{12000}{\pi \times 22400}$$

$$\boxed{p_{max} = 0.1705 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 0.1705 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Mark Allocation (7 marks):**
- Correct given data identification = 0.5 mark
- Substitution in U.P. formula = 2 marks
- Final U.P. torque = 1 mark
- Substitution in U.W. formula = 2 marks
- Final U.W. torque + max pressure = 1.5 marks

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks (KTU Board Examiners' Report):**

1. **Forgetting to Lock the Base Component** — The very first part inserted in an assembly MUST be locked (origin mate). If not, all subsequent mates become unstable and the model "floats." Examiners check this first.

2. **Mixing Uniform Pressure and Uniform Wear formulas** — These are often confused. Remember: **U.P. has variable torque** depending on geometry, while **U.W. = μWR_m** is geometry-agnostic.

3. **Wrong Sign of DOF** — A properly assembled product has DOF = **0**. If student shows DOF > 0, the assembly is incomplete. Examiners dock 2 marks for missing mates.

4. **Missing Centre Lines in Sketches** — Plummer block shafts and clutch axles MUST have chain-dotted centre lines extending slightly beyond the component. Omission = **−1 mark**.

5. **Hatching of Adjacent Parts in Sectional Views** — Adjacent parts in the SAME section MUST have **different hatch angles** (typically 45° vs 135°). Same hatching across two parts is interpreted as **one continuous part** = **−1 mark**.

6. **Not Converting Units** — Torques must be in **N·m or N·mm**, pressure in **N/mm² (MPa)** or **N/m² (Pa)**. Mixing units = **0 marks** even if formula is right.

7. **Missing Bill of Materials (BOM)** — Every assembly drawing must include a BOM table with part number, name, material, quantity. Omission = **−2 marks**.

8. **Forgetting the Coefficient of Friction Value** — In clutch problems, μ is given; students sometimes assume μ = 1.

9. **Not Showing the Sectional View** — KTU 2024 mandates **at least one sectional view (A-A)** in any assembly drawing. Only 3D iso views = **insufficient** for full marks.

10. **Forgetting the Spacing Between Components in Exploded View** — The exploded view must have **clean spacing** showing mating direction clearly. Overlapping components = **−1 mark**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Bullet Summary — Read Before Exam:**

#### **Core Concepts**
- Assembly modelling = **Part files + Mates** combined in a single assembly file.
- A **Mate** removes specific degrees of freedom (DOF); total DOF of an assembly should equal **0**.
- Standard mates: **Coincident, Concentric, Distance, Angle, Parallel, Perpendicular, Tangent, Lock**.
- Always **lock the first component** (base) at origin.
- Validate with **Interference Detection** tool before finalizing.

#### **Plummer Block**
- Pedestal housing for shaft bearing; key parts = Base, Body, Cap, Bearing, Bolts, Set Screws.
- Spherical seat in body allows bearing **self-alignment**.
- Bearing standard: **Deep groove ball bearing** (e.g., 6205 for 25 mm shaft).
- Always include: **Sectional view, BOM, centre lines, oil hole**.

#### **Single Plate Clutch**
- Components: **Flywheel, Friction Disc, Pressure Plate, Springs, Release Levers, Release Bearing**.
- **Engaged:** Springs clamp disc → torque transmitted via friction.
- **Disengaged:** Release bearing pushes pressure plate → disc freed.
- Uniform Pressure Theory: $T_{up} = \dfrac{2\mu W}{3} \cdot \dfrac{(R_o^3 - R_i^3)}{(R_o^2 - R_i^2)}$
- Uniform Wear Theory (Reye): $T_{uw} = \mu W R_m = \dfrac{\mu W(R_o + R_i)}{2}$
- Max pressure (U.P.): $p_{max} = \dfrac{W}{\pi(R_o^2 - R_i^2)}$
- Typical μ for asbestos lining on cast iron = **0.25–0.35**.
- Number of clutch springs = **4 to 12** (commonly 6).

#### **Cone Friction Clutch**
- Uses **truncated conical** surfaces; half-angle α typically **15°–20°**.
- Mechanical advantage factor = $\dfrac{1}{\sin\alpha}$; cone transmits **3.86×** more torque than plate at α = 15°.
- Normal force on cone: $N = \dfrac{W}{\sin\alpha}$.
- Torque (Uniform Wear): $T_{cone} = \dfrac{\mu W R_m}{\sin\alpha}$.
- Slant length: $L = \dfrac{R_o - R_i}{\sin\alpha}$.
- **Self-locking** occurs when α < **12.5°** (≈ 1:5 slope). To avoid jamming, use **α ≥ 15°**.
- Cone clutch components: **Driving Cone, Driven Cone, Springs, Operating Lever, Release Collar**.

#### **CAD / Drawing Standards**
- Hidden lines **omitted** in assembly views.
- Adjacent parts in section view must have **different hatch angles**.
- Centre lines must extend slightly beyond circles.
- Use **chain-dotted lines** (. . . . .) for centre lines.
- Sectional view mandatory = **A-A** section line shown on one view.
- BOM must include: **Part No., Name, Material, Qty**.
- Dimension shaft, bearing bore, cap bolt spacing — at minimum.
- Title block: **Name, Roll No., Date, Scale, Sheet No., Assembly drawing number**.

#### **Numerical Problem Solving — Always Remember**
1. Identify: $R_i$, $R_o$, $W$, $\mu$ → compute $R_m$.
2. For U.P.: compute $R_o^3 - R_i^3$ and $R_o^2 - R_i^2$ first.
3. For U.W.: $T = \mu W R_m$ (simpler, geometry-agnostic).
4. Always state the theory used before substituting.
5. Final answer in **N·m** (not N·mm) unless asked otherwise.

#### **Quick Mental Math Conversions**
- $1 \text{ N·m} = 1000 \text{ N·mm}$
- $1 \text{ MPa} = 1 \text{ N/mm}^2$
- $\sin 15° \approx 0.2588$
- $\sin 20° \approx 0.3420$
- $\sin 30° = 0.5$

---
